sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 1079 has a weak ansae bar, pseudo-ring spiral arms, a co-located HI ring, and enhanced HI and SFR at the oUHR.

quotes.quote: NGC 1079: This galaxy is observed to have a weak `ansae' type bar with two spiral arms emanating from the ends of the bar and winding by more 180 with respect to the bar, almost forming an outer ring-like structure. A ring of 1 gas coincides with the outer stellar pseudo-ring, while the velocity map shows fairly ordered rotation. Refer to the centre right panels in Fig. fig:mosaic2 for the optical and 1 maps. We are able to only measure the radius of the oUHR for this galaxy and find that it nicely traces the location of tightly wound spiral arms on either side of the bar. Corresponding to the oUHR, we also find the co-location of an 1 ring. An enhancement in both _ 1 and _ SFR is also observed from the surface density plots.

gmw_00006894.name: IC 1438
gmw_00006894.overview: IC 1438 is a distant Sa-type spiral galaxy [arXiv:0710.4036] that features a strong central stellar bar [arXiv:0710.4036] and a low inclination [arXiv:0710.4036]. Ongoing star formation is concentrated at the ends of this bar [arXiv:2003.08946], as well as within a nuclear star-forming ring [arXiv:0710.4036].

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 1438 as a distant, poorly studied Sa spiral whose central region is bar-shaped and whose nuclear ring is traced by SINFONI emission-line maps.

quotes.quote: IC 1438 At a distance of 33.8 , the poorly studied Sa spiral IC 1438 is the most distant galaxy in our sample, and with a radius of about 500 , the star formation ring is at the limit of what we consider ``nuclear''. Continuum emission Over the field of view of SINFONI, the K-band contours of IC 1438 are rather elongated outside the very nucleus. This is unlikely to be a projection effect because IC 1438 has a rather low inclination of i = 32 kna06 . Indeed, the outer disk appears fairly round in the DSS image. On the other hand, the central 10 of IC 1438 are dominated by a strong stellar bar which is also evident in the I-band image of . It is therefore likely that the gravitational potential in the center of IC 1438 is bar-shaped as well.

Emission lines Due to the lower S/N of the SINFONI data, the \ map does not reveal the nuclear ring as clearly as the \ map, mostly because the fainter, extended emission is not as obvious. Nevertheless, the two maps agree rather well, especially in the location of the ``hot spots''. Star formation is strongest at the south-east corner (PA 120 ) where an extended arc dominates the emission in both maps.

sources.summary: The paper uses IC 1438 as a barred-galaxy test case, deriving photometric frequency curves and finding its corotation and outer Lindblad resonance consistent with earlier estimates while its ILR lies inward.

quotes.quote: Figure Fig1 shows examples of the frequency curves (right panels) for IC 1438 and NGC 2935 obtained from our photometric approach with our adopted dynamical M/L ratio.

The pattern speeds _ bar of these two galaxies were estimated by 2019Schmidt from photometric methods mentioned above. _ bar is displayed by a horizontal line of orange color. R_ ILR (green), R_ UHR (gray), R_ CR (blue), and R_ OLR (red) are presented with uncertainties on the horizontal line of _ bar .

For IC 1438 (top row), our measurements of R_ CR and R_ OLR are consistent with the estimates of see Figure 3 2019Schmidt within errors, while R_ ILR is located inward compared to theirs. When comparing R_ ILR , we note different ways to deal with a bulge: they adopted an ellipsoidal distribution of b/a=1 , while we considered the bulge region to be exponentially distributed in the z direction like the disk.

sources.summary: The paper finds IC 1438 has star formation at bar ends and edge blobs, V-shaped bar-age structure, and older mass-weighted populations on the bar edges.

quotes.quote: This figure shows that most galaxies have ongoing star formation either along spiral arms (NGC 4303, NGC 4981, NGC 5248), at the ends of the bar (IC 1438) or in a ring-like feature (NGC 6902, NGC 7755). Some galaxies show star formation at the edges of the bar (NGC 4303 and a few blobs in IC 1438, NGC 4981, NGC 6902, NGC 7755) while others show none at all.

This feature is not exclusive for this galaxy but can be seen in at least 5 out of 9 galaxies in our sample (IC 1438, NGC 4643, NGC 4981, NGC 6902, NGC 7755). In this case, the mass-weighted maps, since they highlight older stellar populations better, show at least in IC 1438 and NGC 7755 older populations on the edges of the bar (although, admittedly, this effect is not as clear in the other galaxies).

gmw_00006895.name: XSG1
gmw_00006895.overview: XSG1 is a massive early-type galaxy [arXiv:1509.08250, arXiv:1610.03853] with a spherical E0/E1 morphology and a de Vaucouleurs light profile [arXiv:1610.03853]. Its stellar population exhibits a bottom-heavy initial mass function in its central regions that transitions to a standard Milky-Way-like profile at larger radii [arXiv:1509.08250].

sources.summary: The paper targets XSG1, a massive early-type galaxy, and finds a bottom-heavy central IMF that becomes Milky-Way-like by about half an effective radius.

quotes.quote: We obtain a significant radial trend of the IMF slope in XSG1, a massive early-type galaxy (ETG), with velocity dispersion 300 , observed with the VLT/X-SHOOTER instrument. In addition, we constrain -- for the first time -- both the shape and normalization of the IMF, using only a stellar population analysis. We robustly rule out a single power-law to describe the IMF, whereas a power law tapered off to a constant value at low masses (defined as a bimodal IMF) is consistent with all the observational spectroscopic data and with the stellar M/L constraints based on the Jeans Anisotropic Modelling method. The IMF in XSG1 is bottom-heavy in the central regions (corresponding to a bimodal IMF slope 3, or a mass normalization mismatch parameter 2), changing towards a standard Milky-Way like IMF ( 1.3; 1) at around one half of the effective radius.

The main target of the present work is XSG1 (SDSS J142940.63+002159.0), a massive ( M_ 2 10^ 11 M_ ; 300 ) ETG at redshift z 0.057 , taken from the SPIDER survey SpiderI . The galaxy has been selected from the sample of LB13 to have a high abundance ratio ( 0.4 ). Fig. fig:image shows the SDSS -band image of XSG1, together with the residual map obtained by fitting the galaxy surface brightness distribution with a two-dimensional, PSF-convolved, S ersic model, using the software 2DPHOT LBdC08 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies XSG1 as a massive early-type SPIDER galaxy with spherical morphology, de Vaucouleurs light profile, radial sodium trends, and a Na-inferred IMF consistent with other indicators.

quotes.quote: The targets of the present work are XSG1 (SDSS J142940.63+002159.0) and XSG2 (SDSS J002819.30-001446.7), two massive (M _ 1--2 10^ 11 M _ ; 300 ) ETGs at redshift z 0.05574 and z 0.06009, respectively. Both galaxies are selected from the SPIDER survey SpiderI , more specifically from the subsample of 160 ETGs with 280< <320 \ defined in LB13. XSG1 has spherical morphology (b/a 0.9, corresponding to an E0/E1 type), whose light distribution is well described by a de Vaucouleurs model, with an effective radius of 4.1^ (see LB16 for details).

For XSG1, where line strengths are measured out to 0.8 , the radial trend of \ is similar to \ and , being constant out to 0.5 , and decreasing by 0.2--0.3 dex at 0.8 , without any clear correlation with local metallicity. Such a result seems to be in contrast with the predicted increase of Na nucleosynthetic yields from AGB stars and Type II SNe. For XSG1, the Na-inferred IMF radial profile is consistent, within the errors, with that derived from TiO features and the Wing-Ford band, presented in a recent paper.

gmw_00006896.overview: The radio source 1009+4655 is a quasar that is classified as a likely fat double [arXiv:0708.1145]. It features two bright lobes with extended emission, which resolve into elongated jet-like structures without a visible central compact object [arXiv:0708.1145].

sources.summary: This 7C quasar is classified as a likely fat double with resolved jet-like lobe features and possible diverted low-brightness plumes.

quotes.quote: This source shows two bright lobes with extended emission. There is no compact object visible at the central pointing position. Examination of the 8-GHz image reveals that the two peaks present in the 1.4-GHz image are each resolved into elongated jet-like features as seen in some FR Is. These do not appear compact in the high resolution image. The axial ratio means that this object would most likely be classified as a fat double.

gmw_00006897.overview: The radio source 1018+3542 is the brightest object in its sample, with a flux density of nearly 0.7 Jy [arXiv:0708.1145]. It is likely core-dominated and exhibits high flux-density variability, with detected features hinting at a highly asymmetric structure consistent with relativistic Doppler boosting [arXiv:0708.1145].

sources.summary: The source is the brightest in the sample, probably core dominated, with detected features suggesting a highly asymmetric structure consistent with Doppler boosting.

quotes.quote: The flux-density behaviour of this object points to high variability and does not suggest any structure is being resolved out at any stage. This source is by far the brightest object in the sample, at almost 0.7 Jy, and it is likely that the strength of the core is limiting the dynamic range of the image making detection of extended low surface brightness emission difficult. The features which are detected hint at the presence of a highly asymmetric structure, as is consistent with several other objects in this sample (see below). This may be a consequence of relativistic Doppler boosting.

gmw_00006898.overview: The radio source 1021+4523 is classified as a hybrid or fat-double object [arXiv:0708.1145]. Its southern region contains a compact hotspot linked to the core, while its overall structure suggests undersampled extended emission typical of an FR I type source [arXiv:0708.1145].

sources.summary: The paper describes this source as an unusual fat-double or hybrid object, with undersampled extended structure and a southern compact hotspot.

quotes.quote: The flux density values for this object increase with decreasing array size suggesting undersampling of smooth extended structure by the extended arrays. This would ususally be an indicator of an FR I type object however the southern part of this object appears to contain a compact hot spot with a coherent structure linking it to the core. This unusual object would probably be classified as a fat double, or hybrid source. This object exhibits similarities with hybrid sources such as 0131--367 (Gopal-Krishna Wiita, 2002) with jets of FR I and FR II characteristics on different sides of the nucleus.

gmw_00006899.overview: The source 1022+3931 is a quasar that is probably a Fanaroff-Riley II classical double radio galaxy [arXiv:0708.1145]. Its structure includes a core, a likely hotspot to the northwest, and a probable second hotspot to the east [arXiv:0708.1145].

sources.summary: The source is probably an FR II classical double, with a core, likely hotspot, and possible second hotspot not visibly linked by emission.

quotes.quote: The central pointing coordinates are coincident with the brightest of the four components visible in the map. The next brightest peak to the north west of the core is most likely a hotspot and this object is probably a FR II classical double. No emission linking the quasar to the peak visible to the east was found, even by varying the size of the restoring beam. It is likely however that this is emission from a second hotspot.

gmw_00006900.overview: The source 1023+3604 is classified as a Fanaroff-Riley class I twin-jet quasar [arXiv:0708.1145]. It exhibits low-level extended emission on both sides of its core, which may indicate a single twist or precession in its radio jets [arXiv:0708.1145].

sources.summary: The source is classified as an FR I twin-jet quasar, with low-level extended emission on both sides and a possible jet twist.

quotes.quote: This source can be classified as a FR I twin jet source even though the extended structure is very close to the noise in the map. The extended emission on each side of the core share a parallel axis even though they are slightly offset from the core. This is characteristic of a single twist in the jets although precession of the jet axis may also be a possibility (.f. \ E 1821+643, Blundell Rawlings, 2001, and 3C294, Erlund et al., 2006).

gmw_00006901.overview: The source 1023+4414 is a radio-emitting object whose exact morphological classification remains inconclusive [arXiv:0708.1145]. However, degraded-resolution imaging reveals a diffuse envelope and extended emission that hints at a wide-angle tail structure [arXiv:0708.1145].

sources.summary: The source remains morphologically inconclusive, but degraded-resolution imaging reveals extended diffuse emission suggestive of a wide-angle tail.

quotes.quote: The spatial resolution of this image was deliberately degraded in order to reveal the extended emission. Higher resolution imaging showed only the core and the peak to the west, resolving out the diffuse envelope surrounding them. The fifth positive contour hints at meandering emission with a comparatively large transverse angular size. While features such as these are indicative of wide angle tail sources, our classification for this object remains inconclusive.

gmw_00006902.overview: The object 1023+4824 is a classical double Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy [arXiv:0708.1145]. It features a compact core, asymmetrical jets, hotspots, extensive edge brightening, and a southern lobe with a region of extended backflow [arXiv:0708.1145].

sources.summary: The paper identifies this as the sample’s clearest classical double FR II, with edge brightening, hotspots, a compact core, and backflow.

quotes.quote: The best example of a classical double FR II structure in the sample, 1023+4824 shows extensive edge brightening, a compact core and hotspots and asymmetrical jets. The southern lobe shows what appears to be a region of extended backflow. Classical doubles with large angular sizes will exhibit variations in flux density with larger baseline arrays, hence our selection criteria will not fully exclude objects such as this one.

gmw_00006903.overview: The object 1030+3346 is a quasar that exhibits Fanaroff-Riley class I type radio emission extending to the west of its core [arXiv:0708.1145]. It is most likely a twin-jet source, featuring a diffuse eastern emission peak that is also connected to the core [arXiv:0708.1145].

sources.summary: The source is interpreted as likely a twin-jet FR I quasar based on FR I-like western emission, an associated eastern feature, and flux measurements.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:radiomaps2 shows the maximum resolution map for 1030+3346 showing FR I type emission coming from the west of the core. The diffuse feature to the east of the object is probably associated with the quasar. In a similar way to the maximum resolution image of 1029+3224 the emission to the east appears as a secondary low brightness peak connected to the core in both the B-array data associated with this sample and the VLA FIRST survey image. This object is most likely to be a twin jet FR I source, a claim backed up by comparison of the flux density measurements (see Table tab:fluxtable ).

gmw_00006904.overview: The radio source 1030+4309 is classified as a Fanaroff-Riley class I quasar [arXiv:0708.1145]. High-resolution 8-GHz imaging of the object reveals a central core, a bright peak located 2.5 arcseconds to the north, and a faint southwestern extension [arXiv:0708.1145].

sources.summary: The radio image indicates a likely FR I quasar, with emission fading away from the core and 8-GHz structure including a core, northern peak, and faint extension.

quotes.quote: The radio image of 1030+4309 shows extensive emission decreasing in surface brightness away from the core, albeit on fairly small (i.e. \ arcsecond) scales which explains the fairly steady flux measurements across different arrays. The 8-GHz image reveals the core, the bright peak 2.5 arcseconds to the north and a faint south western extension. The radio morphology points to this source being an FR I quasar although it is possible that the extended emission represents plumes diverted from the hotspots at the ends of short jets.

gmw_00006905.overview: The quasar 1032+4932 is associated with a distorted Fanaroff-Riley class II classical double radio structure [arXiv:0708.1145]. Its radio emission is heavily dominated by its core and the nearest peak, with very weak extended components [arXiv:0708.1145].

sources.summary: The quasar is described as a distorted FR II classical double dominated by its core and nearby peak, with weak extended components.

quotes.quote: All evidence suggests that this quasar has a distorted classical double FR II structure associated with it. The flux density remains steady for all array configurations. The 8-GHz radio map shows the four components also visible in the 1.4-GHz image although the southern extension and north eastern component are very weak. The faint extended emission to the east may represent diffuse emission associated with the quasar although the radio emission is very much dominated by the core and the peak nearest to it.

gmw_00006906.overview: 1033+4116 is a radio-loud quasar located at a redshift of z = 1.1185 [arXiv:1712.06314]. High-resolution radio observations resolve its core into an inner jet structure with two distinct components [arXiv:1712.06314], while flux-density variations suggest the source is variable [arXiv:0708.1145].

sources.summary: The paper describes 1030+415/J1033+4116 as a z=1.1185 Fermi-detected radio quasar whose new 43/86 GHz VLBA images resolve inner jet components.

quotes.quote: 1030+415 (J1033+4116) The radio quasar 1030+415 is at a redshift of z = 1.1185 2010MNRAS.405.2302H and significant ray emission of 24.7 was detected by Fermi /LAT 2015ApJS..218...23A . The VLA in its A-array configuration at 1.4 GHz detected a one-sided jet extending to the east to about 0.5 arcsec 1995ApJS...99..297X . This jet was marginally detected in the Faint Images of the Radio Sky at Twenty-Centimeters (FIRST) survey with the VLA B-array at 1.4 GHz 1997ApJ...475..479W . At 8.4 GHz the VLA observation revealed a point source with a flux density of 384.4 mJy 2007ApJS..171...61H .

Figs. fig:1030+415 d present the 43- and 86-GHz images of 1030+415 with a high resolution of 0.18 mas (corresponding to 1.5 pc). The core which was unresolved in 15-GHz images is resolved into a core and inner jet structure. Two new jet components are detected within 0.5 mas in the 43-GHz image: J2 at 0.19 mas away from the core in a position angle of 110 , and J1 at 0.41 mas in P.A. = 54 .

sources.summary: The paper leaves this source inconclusive, noting only an eastern extension in radio images and attributing flux-density variation probably to variability.

quotes.quote: Radio imaging of this object revealed only a hint of an eastern extension at B-array, also seen in the FIRST survey image. The cause of the variation in the flux density values is most probably source variability. The true morphology of this source remains inconclusive.

gmw_00006907.overview: The quasar 1038+3729 is a probable fat-double or hybrid radio source [arXiv:0708.1145]. It features a pair of radio jets with a southern hotspot, a diffuse northern counter-jet, and a swept-back morphology that may result from motion through the ambient medium [arXiv:0708.1145].

sources.summary: The paper describes this source as a probable fat-double or hybrid with jets, a hotspot, possible wing-like structure, and swept-back morphology.

quotes.quote: Another probable fat double or hybrid source with morphology remarkably similar to that of 1021+4523 showing a pair of jets with a southern hotspot and a more diffuse northern counter jet. The east-west feature seen running between the core and the southern hotspot was coincident with the sidelobe structure of a strong confusing source to the east although, after careful consideration, we think it is unlikely that this feature is an imaging artifact due to its curvature. It is uncertain whether it is associated with the quasar, although radio galaxies have been seen to exhibit unusual X shaped wing structures (e.g. Dennett-Thorpe et al., 2002). The stronger peak on the eastern edge of the map is coincident with a nearby unrelated source. The jets of this source also appear to be swept back slightly in an easterly direction, possibly a result of motion through the ambient medium.

gmw_00006908.overview: At a redshift of 2.5, 1054+4541 is a distant quasar [arXiv:0708.1145]. Radio observations of the source suggest the presence of associated extended structure and hint at oppositely directed jet activity [arXiv:0708.1145].

sources.summary: The source is the most distant in the sample and shows evidence for associated extended structure and possible two-sided jet activity.

quotes.quote: At a redshift of 2.5 this is the most distant object in the sample. Flux density measurements from different VLA arrays suggest there is extended structure associated with this object and there is a hint of oppositely directed jet activity in the radio image. The extended features in this image are real, but are not coincident with any compact features in a high-resolution MERLIN image (Heywood et al., in prep.).

gmw_00006909.name: object A
gmw_00006909.overview: Object A is a barred late-type galaxy that appears to be kinematically decoupled from the Abell 85 cluster [arXiv:2207.11287]. Its star formation history reveals two major bursts at 10 Gyr and 2.3 Gyr, followed by a rapid shutdown and a recent central star formation episode [arXiv:2207.11287].

sources.summary: Object A is classified as a barred late-type galaxy, not dynamically linked to Abell 85, with fossil-record evidence for two bursts and central recent star formation.

quotes.quote: The SB of object A shows evidence of a bar structure oriented along its mayor-axis. The spectral analysis shows that it is blue-shifted by 1,666 km/s from Holm15A. Taking into account that the reported velocity dispersion of Abell 85 is 750 km/s Bravo-Alfaro+2009,Lopez-Cruz+2014 , the line-of-sight relative velocity of object A indicates that it could be kinematically decoupled from Abell 85. The relative velocity with the central galaxy of Abell 85 is larger than two times the escape velocity of the cluster Serra+2011 .

The sSFH shows evidence of a constant star formation rate at all radii with two dominant bursts, one at the age of 10 Gyr and a second at the age of 2.3 Gyr. After that, the galaxy suffers a rapid star formation (SF) shutdown that begins at the central part of the galaxy. In the central region, another SF episode begins at 130 Myr.

gmw_00006910.name: object B
gmw_00006910.overview: Object B is an early-type galaxy and a member of the Abell 85 system [arXiv:2207.11287]. Its stellar population shows an inside-out assembly mode, characterized by an early star-forming burst, a subsequent decline, and a second star-forming burst that peaked 40 million years ago [arXiv:2207.11287].

sources.summary: Object B is an early-type Abell 85 member whose fossil record shows an old burst, later star-formation reactivation, inside-out assembly, and subsolar stellar populations.

quotes.quote: Object B did not show any evidence of a bar structure like the object A . Its redshift shows that object B has a relative velocity with Holme15A of 280 km/s, implying that it is kinematically linked to the Abell 85 system. The fossil record reconstruction shows a similar but not equal sSFH as object A . For object B , we observe a first SF burst at late epochs ( 10 \ Gyr ) from which its sSFR declines, and it reaches a minimum at the age of 600 \ Myr . After this minimum, object B shows a new star forming burst that reaches its peak at 40 \ Myr (see right panels of Figure A_B_SFH ).
The ASM shows a constant rate on the mass assembly until 2 Gyr, and it is dominated by an inside-out assembly mode Ibarra-Medel+16 . It has a T_ 50 of 8.7 Gyr and a T_ 90 of 4.0 Gyr.

gmw_00006911.name: object G
gmw_00006911.overview: Object G is a star-forming galaxy candidate that exhibits strong nebular emission lines, including [O II], H-beta, H-gamma, H-delta, and [O III] [arXiv:2207.11287]. Its recent star formation rate has declined steeply, and its metallicity profile suggests a possible dilution driven by an intense infall of pristine gas [arXiv:2207.11287].

sources.summary: Object G is a star-forming member candidate with a declining recent sSFR, low D4000, strong nebular emission, and metallicity dilution suggesting fresh gas infall.

quotes.quote: The sSFH for objects G and H show the same initial SF burst at an age of 6 Gyr. After this initial burst, their sSFR decreases until an age of 1 Gyr, where the sSFR reaches a constant value ( sSFR 10.0 dex). The sSFR remains constant until an age of 200 Myr, where objects G and H start to differentiate. Object G suffers a more steep decrease on their sSFR reaching an observed time sSFR of -11.2 dex.

The difference among the mass and light weighted metallicities are 0.2 and 0.4 dex for object G and H . The positive value of this difference indicates a possible metallicity dilution by the in-fall of pristine gas like objects A and B but in a much intense rate. The spectra of objects G and H (see Figure plot_spectra2 and Table tab2 ) show the emission of the nebular lines for [ O II ] 3728 , H , H , H , and [ O III ] 4959,5007 , with H displaying the most strong lines.

gmw_00006912.name: object H
gmw_00006912.overview: Object H is a star-forming galaxy candidate that exhibits strong nebular emission lines, including [O II], H-beta, H-gamma, H-delta, and [O III] [arXiv:2207.11287]. These lines, along with strong Balmer and Ca absorption lines, indicate the presence of an intense, recent star formation burst [arXiv:2207.11287].

sources.summary: Object H is a star-forming member candidate with sustained recent sSFR, strong emission lines, low D4000, and stellar-population evidence for gas dilution.

quotes.quote: The sSFH for objects G and H show the same initial SF burst at an age of 6 Gyr. After this initial burst, their sSFR decreases until an age of 1 Gyr, where the sSFR reaches a constant value ( sSFR 10.0 dex). The sSFR remains constant until an age of 200 Myr, where objects G and H start to differentiate. Object G suffers a more steep decrease on their sSFR reaching an observed time sSFR of -11.2 dex. On the other hand, object H maintains an slower decrement on their sSFR reaching a value of sSFR 10.7 dex at the moment of the observation.

The spectra of objects G and H (see Figure plot_spectra2 and Table tab2 ) show the emission of the nebular lines for [ O II ] 3728 , H , H , H , and [ O III ] 4959,5007 , with H displaying the most strong lines. These emission lines indicate the existence of an intense and resent SF burst. The stellar spectra show the existence of strong Balmer and Ca absorption lines.

gmw_00006913.name: object J
gmw_00006913.overview: Object J is characterized by an intermediate stellar population with a light-weighted age of 3.6 Gyr and a mass-weighted age of 4.6 Gyr [arXiv:2207.11287]. Its star formation history shows several weak bursts, though its specific star formation rate has continuously decreased to its present-day value [arXiv:2207.11287].

sources.summary: Object J has an intermediate fossil record, with several weak bursts, declining present sSFR, homogeneous mass growth, and near-solar metallicity.

quotes.quote: Intermediate objects : this group only contains object J , its spectrum is similar to the ones observed for the interacted objects. The SPS analysis reconstructs an sSFH that shows a late SF burst that reaches a maximum at the age of 6 Gyr, with small SF bursts at 2 Gyr, 500 Myr, and 80 Myr. Those SF bursts are not strong enough to maintain the sSFR, continuously decreasing until the present day value of -13.5 dex. Their ASM shows an homogeneous mass growth at all radii showing a flat assembly mode at all epochs. The ASM gives a T_ 50 age of 5.3 Gyr with a T_ 90 age of 2.8 Gyr.

The light (mass) weighted age is 3.6 (4.6) Gyr, with a D4000 index value of 1.82 dex. Its metallicity rounds the solar value (see Table tab2 ) with a difference among the mass and light-weighted values of -0.03 dex.

gmw_00006914.name: object M
gmw_00006914.overview: Object M is a high-redshift galaxy detected at a redshift of 1.0126 [arXiv:2207.11287]. Fossil reconstruction of its star formation history reveals an initial burst followed by rapid quenching, which was later reactivated to reach a peak star formation rate at 280 Myr [arXiv:2207.11287].

sources.summary: Object M is the farthest detected galaxy-spectrum object, at z=1.0126, with fossil-record evidence for quenching, later SFR reactivation, strong emission, and metallicity dilution.

quotes.quote: Object M Figure M_SFH shows the fossil reconstruction of the sSFH and the ASM distribution of object M . The fossil reconstruction shows an initial star formation burst at the age of 5 Gyr, followed by a rapid SF quenching that drops the sSFR at a minimum value of 11.5 dex at the age of 830 Myr. After this age, the sSFR is reactivated and reaches an sSFR peak ( 10.2 dex) at 280 Myr. Then, the sSFR slowly decreases until its present-day value (-10.8 dex).

Object M is the farthest object that we detect with a well-defined galaxy spectrum. It has a redshift value of 1.0126; at that redshift, the universe had an age of 5.9 Gyrs. Therefore, the SPS analysis of object M allows us to access the archaeological information at the cosmic noon without suffering from a significant bias inherited from the archaeological inferences limitations Ibarra-Medel:2019aa . Their nebular emission lines confirm the archaeological view of a recent reactivation of the SFR.

gmw_00006915.name: CXOHDFN 123556.12+621219.1
gmw_00006915.overview: CXOHDFN 123556.12+621219.1 is an active galactic nucleus with an X-ray redshift of z = 1.14 [arXiv:astro-ph/0307426]. The object exhibits intrinsic absorption and is classified as a highly obscured, highly luminous type 2 quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0307426].

sources.summary: The paper reports a 3 keV line, z=1.14 X-ray redshift, intrinsic absorption, and quasar-level luminosity for CXOHDFN 123556.12+621219.1.

quotes.quote: As an example we report here the detection of a highly significant emission line feature which is present around 3 keV in the spectrum of the unidentified Chandra source CXOHDFN 123556.12+621219.1 ( F_ 2-8 keV = 1.9 10^ 14 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , R =23.7, f_x/f_ opt 18; ). The line energy corresponds to an X--ray redshift of z =1.14 ^ +0.11 _ 0.04 assuming neutral iron. There is also evidence of intrinsic absorption with a column density N_H 3 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 . Given its unabsorbed X--ray luminosity of about 10 ^ 44 erg s ^ 1 in the 2--10 keV band, this object could be classified as an highly obscured, highly luminous type 2 quasar.

gmw_00006916.name: TOL 1351-375
gmw_00006916.overview: TOL 1351-375 is a Seyfert 1.9 active galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0002460]. Its X-ray spectrum is characterized by an absorbed power-law, an iron K-alpha line, and a thermal component [arXiv:astro-ph/0002460].

sources.summary: The paper describes TOL 1351-375 as a Seyfert 1.9 galaxy whose ASCA spectrum is fit by absorbed power-law, iron-line, and thermal components with a measured 2-10 keV flux.

quotes.quote: TOL 1351-375: TOL 1351-375 is a Seyfert 1.9 galaxy observed by ASCA in 1997. The spectrum obtained after the standard data reduction has a good signal-to-noise and has been fitted with a multi-component model consisting of an absorbed powerlaw, an iron K _ line and a thermal component with kT 1.2 keV. The best fit gives the following values: photon index =1.94 0.13 ; absorbing column density N_H=1.6 3 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 ; rest frame line energy E=6.38 ^ +0.2 _ 0.3 keV; line equivalent width EW=175 120 eV. The measured 2-10 keV flux is 3.8 10^ 12 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 .

gmw_00006917.name: NGC 1792
gmw_00006917.overview: NGC 1792 is a spiral galaxy classified as SA(rs)bc [arXiv:1811.11386] that features strong spiral arms and a point-like nucleus [arXiv:1003.3015]. It belongs to a loose group [arXiv:1811.11386], where gravitational interactions with NGC 1808 have triggered an asymmetric distribution of star formation [arXiv:1202.2713].

sources.summary: The paper places NGC 1792 in a loose group, classifies it as SA(rs)bc, and finds neither disturbed atomic gas nor evidence that stripping controls its gas content.

quotes.quote: NGC 1792 is part of the ``NGC 1792 group" or the ``NGC 1808 group" - a loose group. It is about 160 kpc ( 0.5R _ 200 ) from the group centre. This galaxy falls under the morphological classification SA(rs)bc.

Dahlem et al. ( dahlem01A ) looked for diffuse extended intergalactic 1 gas in the form of tidal debris and tails between NGC 1792 and NGC 1808 but find no evidence of such features. Our 1 maps also do not show signs of a disturbed 1 disc. Since we do not find any clear evidence of ram pressure or tidally induced gas stripping, we conclude that such environmental parameters may not be playing a significant role in affecting the 1 gas content of this galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper uses K-band decomposition to estimate NGC 1792 properties, describing strong spiral arms, a point-like nucleus, and an inclination-driven lower limit on Eddington rate.

quotes.quote: For the bulge-disc decomposition image analysis, we have obtained archival K band imaging for ESO 121-G006, NGC 1448 and NGC 1792.

The residual image of the moderately inclined galaxy, NGC 1792, contains strong spiral arms as well as a point-like nuclear source. We note that due to the inclination angle of this source, the derived may be systematically over-estimated by a factor of 2 (see above). Thus, our derived Eddington rate for NGC 1792, presented in 5.1, should be considered a lower limit.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1792 as interacting with NGC 1808, having unexpectedly high multiwavelength luminosity and asymmetric star formation triggered by gravitational interaction.

quotes.quote: NGC 1792 is in a strongly interacting galaxy group with the dominant galaxy NGC 1808. NGC 1792 has surprisingly high luminosities, comparable to those of its more massive partner NGC 1808, at many different wavelengths 1994ApJ...432..590D . Upon closer inspection, 1994ApJ...432..590D found a strongly asymmetric star formation distribution caused by the external trigger of gravitational interactions with NGC 1808.

gmw_00006918.name: SDSS J072908.71+400836.6
gmw_00006918.overview: SDSS J072908.71+400836.6 is classified as a type 1.9 quasar [arXiv:1907.06676]. Its spectrum and spectral energy distribution are well fit by a composite quasar model reddened by approximately 1.1 magnitudes of visual extinction [arXiv:1907.06676].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J072908.71+400836.6 as a type 1.9 quasar whose spectrum and SED are fit by a reddened composite quasar model with A_V about 1.1 mag.

quotes.quote: SDSS J072908.71+400836.6 is a type 1.9 quasar. It has a broad-line component in \ but not in \ (Figure fig:spec , upper panel). Its SED and spectrum can be well fit (Figure fig:cand ) by a composite quasar SED reddened by an extinction curve model (Equation eq:fm ) with A_V 1.1 mag (Table table:table2 ).

gmw_00006919.name: LAB-01
gmw_00006919.overview: LAB-01 is a galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z = 2.6 [arXiv:2010.07934]. Spectral energy distribution fitting suggests the galaxy is a dusty quasar with weak, absorbed X-ray emission and strong far-infrared emission [arXiv:2010.07934].

sources.summary: The paper identifies LAB-01 with nearby WHDF/HST/SPIES counterparts, weak absorbed X-ray emission, FIR emission, and an AGN SED fit suggesting a dusty QSO at z about 2.6.

quotes.quote: LAB-01 In Fig. fig:hst_alma_spies and Table table:ALMA_mag ) we see that LAB-01 shows weak X-ray emission at S_X(1.2-2keV)=5.84^ +13.5 _ 4.18 10^ 17 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 and S_X(2-7keV)=7.42^ +10.1 _ 4.58 10^ 16 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 that appears to be absorbed with a column estimated as N_H 1 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 . Strong FIR emission from HeLMS is seen (see Table table:helms ) similar to LAB-05 and LAB-11 although no radio emission is detected. There is a WHDF detection at =25.4 , whdf5449, only 0.''15 offset from the ALMA position and also detected at b and H .

In Fig. fig:lab123 we see that the weak X-ray emission may be absorbed with a neutral H column estimated as N_H 1 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 . The comparison with the best fit 1.4 m dip AGN model gives a dust reddening of A_V=1.75 0.25 mag and a redshift of z=2.6 0.15 . The fit to the starforming galaxy is formally worse at least at the ^2=9.54 , 4 level for all A_V values than the AGN fits.

gmw_00006920.name: LAB-02
gmw_00006920.overview: LAB-02 is a likely high-redshift quasar at an estimated redshift of z=3.1 [arXiv:2010.07934]. The galaxy exhibits strong far-infrared emission and 8.4 GHz radio emission, though it shows no detected X-ray emission [arXiv:2010.07934].

sources.summary: The paper treats LAB-02 as a likely high-redshift QSO, with FIR and radio detections, an HST counterpart, AGN-favored fitting, and caveats about source offsets.

quotes.quote: LAB-02 In Fig. fig:hst_alma_spies and Table table:ALMA_mag we see that LAB-02 shows no X-ray emission. Strong FIR emission is seen as well as 8.4 GHz radio emission (see Table table:helms ), although the radio source lies at 2.''9 from the ALMA position. The optical position (whdf9271) is also 1.''65 away and faint at =25.8 .

In Fig. fig:lab123 the comparison with the best fit 1 power-law AGN model implies a dust absorption of A_V=0.25 0.38 mag, a redshift z=3.1 0.25 and T 39K . The best fit to a starforming galaxy is AS2UDS with A_V=0.5 0.75 and z=0.0 0.15 with a low fitted dust temperature of T<10K . The star-forming fit is marginally worse than the 1-power-law AGN model at the ^2=1.0 level but this rises to ^2>9.5 for any z>0.5 whatever the A_V . The FIR/radio ratio also suggests a high redshift object, with the caveat about the ALMA/radio offset.

gmw_00006921.name: LAB-03
gmw_00006921.overview: LAB-03 is classified as an obscured quasar candidate [arXiv:2010.07934]. It exhibits weak X-ray emission in its harder bands at the ALMA position, though its optical and near-infrared counterparts remain uncertain [arXiv:2010.07934].

sources.summary: The paper classifies LAB-03 as an obscured quasar candidate, emphasizing hard-band X-ray evidence at the ALMA position despite uncertain optical and infrared counterparts.

quotes.quote: LAB-03 In Fig. fig:hst_alma_spies and Table table:ALMA_mag we see that LAB-03 shows some evidence of X-ray emission in the two harder bands S_X(1.2-2keV)=1.77^ +2.5 _ 1.1 10^ 16 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 and S_X(2-7keV)=6.29^ +4.8 _ 1.0 10^ 17 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 within 0.''17 of the ALMA position. We are reasonably satisfied that the WHDF source whdf9547 at =24.15 is identified with the double galaxy shown on the HST i (see Fig. fig:hst_alma_spies ) image which is offset by 1.''82 from the ALMA position. A SPIES 3.6 m source close to the detection limit is seen at 1.''57 offset from ALMA and 1.''24 from whdf1234. It is classified as a probable galaxy in the [ 3.6 ] m SPIES band with class-star-ch1=0.48 .

Generally, there is significant doubt about whether any of these optical/NIR detections are directly associated with the ALMA sub-mm source which is significantly offset from the WHDF (+HST) galaxy at a separation of 1.''54 and from the SPIES source at 1.''57 (see Fig. fig:lab123 ). We note that there is a faint object detected only in the H band which may be the actual counterpart as shown in Fig. fig:hst_alma_spies at the `H band' coordinate listed in Table table:ALMA_mag . We have therefore fitted the SED (see Fig. fig:lab123 ) assuming the whdf1234, HST and SPIES sources are from the double galaxy but this object is unlikely to be the direct counterpart of the sub-mm sources and more likely to be a companion. The SED best fit is the 1.4 m dip AGN model with z=2.7 0.35 and A_V=0.25 0.25 , T<10K but the AS2UDS star-forming fit with z=0.2 , A_V=0.0 , T<10K is only negligibly worse ( ^2=0.1 ). In the SPIES data, [ 3.6 ] =19.49 (automag Vega) and it is undetected in the [ 4.5 ] band. The upper limit on the [ 4.5 ] flux gives [ 3.6 ] [ 4.5 ] <0.31 for aperture 1. Here, because of the uncertainty of the optical/NIR counterpart we weight the detection of the weak X-ray source at the exact ALMA position more highly and conclude that LAB-03 is an obscured quasar possibly associated indirectly with the object at z 2.7 , again bearing in mind the caveats about the uncertain optical/NIR counterpart.

gmw_00006922.name: LAB-04
gmw_00006922.overview: LAB-04 is a submillimeter galaxy located in the William Herschel Deep Field [arXiv:2010.07934]. It exhibits a quasar-like Spitzer color, though its spectral energy distribution makes it difficult to definitively distinguish between an active galactic nucleus and a star-forming origin [arXiv:2010.07934].

sources.summary: The paper calls LAB-04 an archetypal WHDF SMG with near-infrared and X-ray hints, ambiguous AGN/star-forming fits, and quasar-like Spitzer color.

quotes.quote: LAB-04 This is the archetypal SMG in the WHDF with no UBRI detections and only detections at H , [ 3.6 ] and [ 4.5 ] . However, the H and SPIES detections are relatively strong and are both within the 0.''25-0.''5 range. There is also a hint of X-ray detection in the two softer bands at S_X(0.5-1.2keV)=1.09^ +2.5 _ 0.78 10^ 16 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 and S_X(1.2-2keV)=2.28^ +1.3 _ 0.4 10^ 16 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 . although none in the harder band. The X-ray source is within 0."095 of the ALMA position.

From the SED's in Fig. fig:lab456 it is difficult to rule out either an AGN or SF origin for LAB-04 although there is a marginal ^2=0.47 preference for a z=4.5 , A_V=0.25 AS2UDS star-forming galaxy template over the z=3.0 , A_V=2.5 1-power-law AGN. However, the [ 3.6 ] [ 4.5 ] =1.03 colour satisfies the [ 3.6 ] [ 4.5 ] >0.4 criterion for a quasar.

gmw_00006923.name: LAB-06
gmw_00006923.overview: LAB-06 is a bright Sab spiral galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.046 [arXiv:2010.07934]. It serves as a template for a dusty star-forming galaxy and is detected in Herschel, VLA, and Chandra X-ray observations [arXiv:2010.07934].

sources.summary: The paper identifies LAB-06 as a bright z=0.046 Sab spiral galaxy, the unambiguous LABOCA counterpart and a dusty star-forming template detected from Herschel to Chandra.

quotes.quote: LAB-06 Although this LABOCA source has no ALMA data, it is such a bright ( =16.07 mag) spiral galaxy ( z=0.046 ) that it is the unambiguous counterpart of the sub-mm sources. The position of the galaxy (whdf3406) is 5.''0 away from the LABOCA position, well within the the 11.''3 tolerance listed in Table 1 of . It is therefore a typical Sab spiral at the knee of the galaxy luminosity function, M^* . In Fig. fig:lab456 we then see that this galaxy is detected in Herschel, VLA and Chandra X-ray, the latter at a faint flux of S_X(1.2-2keV) 6.8 10^ 17 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 at the optical position. We have already noted our use of this sub-mm galaxy as one of our two templates for a dusty star-forming galaxy.

gmw_00006924.name: LAB-07
gmw_00006924.overview: LAB-07 is a high-redshift infrared galaxy that is classified as a probable quasar [arXiv:2010.07934]. Spectral energy distribution fitting strongly favors active galactic nucleus templates over star-forming galaxy templates, indicating a redshift of approximately 3.1 [arXiv:2010.07934].

sources.summary: The paper treats LAB-07 as a probable QSO with uncertain LABOCA-only counterpart identification, AGN-favored SED fits, red Spitzer color, and Herschel emission.

quotes.quote: LAB-07 Here we have LABOCA data but no ALMA data. The uncertainty on the position is =11.''6 according to . There are 3 possible optical sources associated with 3 SPIES sources at 8.''0 (LAB-07-1), 4.''5 (LAB-07-2) and 3.''0 (LAB-07-3) offsets (see Fig. fig:lab6789_irband ). The faintest optical/SPIES source at the largest offset is also a very faint ( 1 ) X-ray source in the two harder bands. But since this is below our X-ray significance threshold and since the brightest optical/SPIES source (LAB-07-3) is also the closest to the LABOCA position and also has a low-significance X-ray detection, we consider this source (whdfext3652) as the most likely counterpart. This source is classified morphologically as a galaxy in SPIES and in WHDF.

LAB-07 is also close to a strong Herschel source at 250, 350 and 500 m (see Table table:helms ). In terms of SED fitting, both AGN fits give z=3.1 0.1 , A_V=0.0 0.1 and T=48K and are significantly better than either of the star-forming galaxy fits with SF6 giving the best fit with z=0.1 , A_V=0.0 and T=13K . Moreover, the AS2UDS SED fits both strongly reject redshifts z>0.5 .

gmw_00006925.name: LAB-08
gmw_00006925.overview: LAB-08 is a galaxy detected in the optical and Spitzer 4.5-micron bands [arXiv:2010.07934]. It is a probable quasar with strong Herschel emission, and its spectral energy distribution is best fit by an active galactic nucleus model [arXiv:2010.07934].

sources.summary: The paper marks LAB-08 as a probable QSO but uncertain counterpart, citing faint optical/Spitzer detections, galaxy morphology, UVX color, Herschel emission, and AGN-favored SED fits.

quotes.quote: LAB-08 Again we have LABOCA but no ALMA data. The uncertainty on the LABOCA position is =12.''6 according to . No X-rays nor radio emission are detected. The optical source whdfext3250 is faint at =25.6 and lies at 4''.2 E, 0.''8 N from the LABOCA coordinate (see Fig. fig:lab6789_irband ).

We therefore take whdfext3250 as the counterpart 4.''3 from the LABOCA position as this is the closest candidate, together with this [ 4.5 ] m source, despite these larger than usual offsets between the optical and the [ 4.5 ] m positions. Morphologically, whdfext3250 is identified as a galaxy in the band and the 4.5 m band. Although not detected at 3.6 m, whdfext3250 is strongly detected in the u band and is formally UVX at u-b=-0.67 0.2 .

LAB-08 is also close to a strong Herschel HeLMS source at 250, 350 and 500 m. The AGN SED fits are both much better than the star-forming galaxy fits with the best 1-power law AGN model with z=4.0 0.2 , A_V=0.75 0.5 nd T>50K having ^2=16.02 over the best AS2UDS fit with z 0.0 , A_V 0.0 and T>50K .

gmw_00006926.name: LAB-09
gmw_00006926.overview: LAB-09 is a galaxy with a faint optical and infrared counterpart [arXiv:2010.07934]. It is classified as a probable quasar or star-forming galaxy, exhibiting a Spitzer color consistent with a quasar and a best-fit spectral energy distribution matching a star-forming galaxy model [arXiv:2010.07934].

sources.summary: The paper presents LAB-09 as a probable QSO or star-forming galaxy, noting weak possible X-ray emission, a faint optical/IR counterpart, QSO-like Spitzer color, and ambiguous SED fits.

quotes.quote: LAB-09 Again we have LABOCA but no ALMA data. Neither VLA radio emission nor Herschel FIR emission is detected. Only a weak X-ray source in the softest and hardest bands may be detected at the LABOCA position but these are not even jointly significant at 1 . A faint optical/IR source (whdfext5230) with =25.6 and H=21.05 is detected within 2'' of the LABOCA position. whdfext5230 is coincident with a source detected in SPIES at 3.6 and 4.5 ms (see Fig. fig:lab6789_irband and Table table:LAB_mag ).

The [ 3.6 ] [ 4.5 ] =0.44 colour is above the 0.4 mag threshold for a QSO. With class-star-ch1=0.407 and class-star-ch2=0.117, both correspond to galaxies and similarly for the band morphology. The best overall SED fit is given by the AS2UDS star-forming galaxy model with z=0.8 0.35 , A_V=1.0 0.75 and formally T<10K but with 3 upper limits from HeLMS there is essentially no constraint on the dust temperature.

gmw_00006927.name: LAB-10
gmw_00006927.overview: LAB-10 is a source detected in both ALMA and LABOCA observations [arXiv:2010.07934]. It exhibits far-infrared emission detected by Herschel at 250 and 350 micrometers, but lacks detected radio or X-ray emission [arXiv:2010.07934].

sources.summary: The paper leaves LAB-10 unknown, describing ALMA/LABOCA detections, difficult optical identification near an early-type galaxy, FIR emission, and offset companion candidates.

quotes.quote: LAB-10 This source has ALMA data as well as LABOCA data. We note that it lies in the outskirts of a nearby early-type galaxy and this makes detection more difficult in the optical bands. Neither radio nor X-ray emission is detected. Herschel FIR emission is detected at 250 and 350 m.

An optical source, whdf428 is detected at =23.97 and b=25.62 , offset by 1.''68 from the ALMA position (see Fig. fig:hst_alma_spies and Table table:LAB_mag ). A point-like source is also detected on the HST i frame, 1.''2 West of the ALMA source at i=25.85 0.05 and mid-way to the WHDF source with a further 1.''2 offset and is probably the counterpart to whdf428. A source is also detected quite strongly at 3.6 and 4.5 m in SPIES at 1.''9 offset from the ALMA source. This SPIES source is probably the HST diffuse source to the SW of the HST point source. Although the HST point source could be the counterpart of whdf428 and the SPIES source, we regard the 1.''2 offset of the HST point source from the ALMA source as too far for it to be its counterpart. Although the offsets for optical and SPIES sources are >1''.0 we treat these as the detected components of a single object, given the possible astrometric errors.

gmw_00006928.name: LAB-12
gmw_00006928.overview: LAB-12 is a submillimeter source and a probable quasar [arXiv:2010.07934]. It is detected in the near-infrared and Hubble Space Telescope i band, with a spectral energy distribution that is best fit by an active galactic nucleus model [arXiv:2010.07934].

sources.summary: The paper describes LAB-12 as a probable QSO, fainter than LAB-11, with ALMA, near-infrared, HST, and Spitzer detections and QSO-like color.

quotes.quote: LAB-12 This object was picked up in the same ALMA observation as for LAB-11. It is 2 fainter at 870 m than LAB-11 and lying 10.''6 SE. It is in the direction of a second 8.4GHz radio peak detected in EVLA-6 (see Fig. 2 of ), now called EVLA-6-2, but EVLA-6-2 is a further 4.''05 0.''75 E and 4.''91 1.''91 S of LAB-12 (see Table table:helms ), suggesting EVLA-6-2 is not a radio counterpart of LAB-12. No X-rays were detected from this sub-mm source.

There is also no WHDF optical detection but a faint source is detected in the HST i band at 0.''43 offset from the ALMA source with i_ Vega =25.38 0.04 . It is also strongly detected in the H , K bands at 0.''32 offset with H=21.04 0.02 and K=19.87 0.02 .
Again this is well into the QSO locus. The best SED fit is given by the AGN 1.4 m dip model with ^2=2.07 over the AS2UDS star-forming template.

gmw_00006929.name: TucIV
gmw_00006929.overview: Tucana IV is an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy orbiting the Milky Way [arXiv:1805.01839, arXiv:2402.08731]. It features a sparse, scattered structure and a shifted density center, which may have been caused by a past collision with the Large Magellanic Cloud [arXiv:1805.01839, arXiv:2311.14102].

sources.summary: The paper estimates Tucana IV proper motion without public spectroscopy, finds its density peak may be offset, and reports agreement with previous proper-motion and membership estimates.

quotes.quote: We present mean absolute proper motion measurements for seven ultra-faint dwarf galaxies orbiting the Milky Way, namely Bo o tes III, Carina II, Grus II, Reticulum II, Sagittarius II, Segue 2 and Tucana IV. For four of these dwarfs our proper motion estimate is the first ever provided. The adopted astrometric data come from the second data release of the Gaia mission. We determine the mean proper motion for each galaxy starting from an initial guess of likely members, based either on radial velocity measurements or using stars on the Horizontal Branch identified in the Gaia ( G_ BP G_ RP , G) colour-magnitude diagram in the field of view towards the UFD.

The UFDs Bo o tes III, Grus II, Sagittarius II and Tucana IV do not have publicly available spectroscopic information. As mentioned in Sect. method we therefore use Horizontal Branch (HB) candidates located within 15 from the centre of each UFD (from mc12 ) to obtain an initial guess on the mean astrometric parameters. HB stars have been chosen because they are easy to detect in the CMD, being bluer than the typical sequences described by the majority of stars located in the respective fields of view. This is clearly demonstrated in Fig. sel_hb , where the CMD regions adopted to select HB stars are marked with a cyan box. To demonstrate the effectiveness of the selection procedure described in Sect. method , Fig. sel_hb shows a snapshot of the last iteration step from the point of view of the CMD. The red filled circles are stars that survived the 2.5 clipping astrometric selection and items 4 ii ) and 4 iii ) of the procedure. However, only those located inside the blue delimited box will be used for the final mean proper motion estimate. In general, the selected members are located on the typical evolutionary sequences characteristic of the stellar populations seen in dwarf galaxies. These sequences are quite well-defined despite the meagre numbers ( 40 50 stars). The fact that the poorly populated red giant branches are also quite faint (the brightest HB is at G 19 ) contributes in spreading them out in G_ BP G_ RP colour. The colour uncertainty at these magnitudes is 0.1 , whereas the measured spread amounts to 0.15 . Therefore we cannot exclude the presence of multiple, possibly chemically heterogeneous sub-populations (in fact UFDs typically show spread in their metallicity distribution, see tolstoy09 ). The distribution in the VPD of the final members of the four UFD galaxies is shown in Fig. vpd_hb with red filled circles. Like for the dwarfs analysed in Sec. wspec , they describe a reasonably well concentrated clump of stars, clearly separated from the bulk of the field population (black dots). It is interesting to note that in the case of Bo o tes III, a third concentration of stars clearly stands out at ( _ , _ ) (-5, -1) : it is made up of stars belonging to the Galactic globular cluster NGC 5466, located very close to the galaxy also on the sky. Their quite different distances from the Sun (with the UFD 30 kpc farther away, ferraro99 ) and kinematics, with Bo o tes III having ( _ , _ ) = ( 1.21 0.13 , 0.92 0.17 ) , corresponding to a difference in velocity of about 100 km s ^ 1 , implies that the two systems are not associated to each other. The spreads in the distributions of members in the VPDs of the four UFDs are consistent with the typical proper motion uncertainties of their stars. This is true even for the case of Sagittarius II, whose distribution seems significantly more extended and asymmetric than for the other systems. This is because for its stars the errors on _ are a factor of 1.5 larger than those on _ . The cyan filled circles in Fig. vpd_hb mark the initial HB candidates, most of them being located well within the region of the final members. Finally, Fig. sky_hb shows the distribution of the selected members on the sky. As in Sect. wspec , only stars represented as black dots and within the red circle have been considered for the final mean proper motion estimate. All the four galaxies show a peak in the concentration of their members within the projected distance cut given by the radius of the red circle. Interestingly however, for some of these (namely Bo o tes III and Tucana IV), the peak of the density distribution seems somewhat offset with respect to the nominal centre (given by the centre of the red circle, and taken from grillmair09 and dw15 , respectively). Moreover all the systems, with maybe the exception of Sagittarius II (which has been proposed to be a globular cluster rather than a UFD, see mp18 ), appear asymmetric and possibly depict an elongated shape. It is beyond the aim of this paper to make a quantitative analysis on these aspects, yet our findings already highlight how powerful can be to better assess the density profiles and centres of systems like the UFDs, which suffer from large amounts of contamination by field stars. The location of all the initially selected candidates HB stars are marked in Fig. sky_hb with cyan empty circles. Few of these HB stars have been lost during the procedure, but this was to be expected given that the initial selection is astrometrically blind. The fact that the actual members all lie well within the adopted projected distance cut from the nominal centre is a further check on the robustness of our procedure. Sagittarius II might show hints of the presence of few HB members outside this distance cut. Increasing such a radius does not improve the final proper motion estimate, and given the high degree of contamination in that field we cannot conclude they are false members. In this paper we have estimated the mean absolute proper motion for seven UFD satellites of the Milky Way, all located within 70 kpc from us and with an integrated V-band magnitude brighter than V=15. This has ensured that a sufficiently large number of members are above magnitude limit of G 21 . For four of these seven dwarfs, ours is the first proper motion estimate ever provided. To determine likely member stars from which to compute the mean proper motion of each system we developed a new procedure which proved to be less prone to systematic uncertainties than using only radial velocity members in case of very low number statistics. This procedure starts from an initial guess on the mean proper motion and parallax coming from an initial sample of tentative members, and then refines their membership and the mean astrometric parameters by coupling photometric (CMD selection) and astrometric information together. The initial sample of candidate members is made up of spectroscopic members, if radial velocity measurements are publicly available, or HB stars, otherwise. For all the seven UFDs studied here, our procedure works well. We obtain well-defined evolutionary sequences in the CMDs and a clear concentration of stars in the VPDs. Interestingly, the distribution on the sky of the selected members often highlights elongated or asymmetric structures, suggestive of tidal perturbations or complex dynamical histories, and a couple of galaxies might be offset with respect to their previously determined density centres. Future investigations based on photometric and astrometric datasets, possibly in combination with dynamical models, will be used to assess these findings. When comparing our results to those of and for the sub-sample of UFDs with spectroscopic information, we find good agreement for Carina II and Reticulum-II, where small differences can be ascribed to the different sample of members used (ours not being limited to spectroscopic members only) and to the fact that we took into account the full covariance matrix in the determination of the mean proper motions. On the other hand, we found a 1.5 difference with the results of for Segue 2, a system that this author recognised as peculiar due to the wide spread of its stars in the VPD. In this case, the sub-sample of spectroscopic members turns out to be systematically shifted in the VPD. This demonstrates that when limited to small samples systematic effects related to small numbers statistics, might bias the results. In the case of UFDs without public spectroscopic information, we find good agreement for Bo o tes III with the results of , who used proprietary radial velocities to assess the stellar membership and found a mean proper motion which matches ours well within a 1 uncertainty. This is possibly the best confirmation on the validity of our method. Finally, our estimates on Grus II and Tucana IV agree well with those of , both in terms of mean proper motions and of expected number of members.

sources.summary: The paper discusses TucIV as a sparse satellite whose scattered structure, possible LMC encounter, foreground overlap, and shifted center affected member recovery.

quotes.quote: The number of spectroscopic members retrieved by TucIII, BoöIII, TucIV and BoöI are relatively lesser (less than 85 ) compared to other satellite galaxies. In the case of TucIII, the core and tidal tails of TucIII contain a total of 48 spectroscopic members (36 present in Gaia - DECam catalogs). However, the implementation of DBSCAN (HDBSCAN) has successfully identified only 18 (26) of these members. TucIII has extended tidal tails, and we find members extending till the edge of the field that is considered using HDBSCAN . A larger area may yield better results through better characterization of low-density spatial frequencies. However, when utilizing DBSCAN, it is evident that the algorithm still needs to capture stars within the tidal tails. Additionally, our experiments with varying hyperparameters in DBSCAN ( lmtt eps and lmtt min samples ) resulted in a significant number of non-members, likely due to the density of TucIII being lower than the average density of the MW Pace_2022 . The elevated velocity dispersion, small pericenter, and the potential association with the Styx stream (agreement with the predicted retrograde motion of the coincident Styx stellar stream; ), BoöIII has been suggested to be experiencing tidal disruption Carlin_2009 . The lower member recovery rates in BoöIII could be attributed to its relatively low average density Carlin_2018 , pointing to its status as a potential tidally disrupting galaxy candidate. One of the reasons for lower candidate member recovery rates in TucIV can be the sparsely spaced members in spatial and PM space. The scattered structure of TucIV might have been caused by a collision with the Large Magellanic Cloud, which may have altered its trajectory and internal kinematics Sales_2017,Simon_2020 .

Additionally, one of the spectroscopic members in TucIV was later found to be an outlier in the proper motion ( _ cos = 0.900 [ mas/year ] , _ = -0.225 [ mas/year ] ).This star is positioned significantly away from the proper motion of TucIV ( _ cos = 0.534 ± 0.050, _ = 1.707 ± 0.055), and is also reported in Pace_2022 . The lower rate of recovering members in TucIV might be attributed to the overlap in proper motion between TucIV and foreground stars in the MW Pace_2019 and the spread in PM space. In TucIV, there is a slight shift observed in the center of the galaxy compared to the results in Drlica-Wagner_2015 in both DBSCAN and HDBSCAN. As a result, we have opted to utilize the revised center coordinates provided in Simon_2020 .

sources.summary: The paper reviews Tucana IV size measurements, explains the HST field covered only the inner third, and cites spectroscopy and mass-to-light ratio confirming it as a galaxy.

quotes.quote: Tucana IV Tuc IV had the largest angular size of the ultra-faint satellite candidates discovered in the DES year-two data, with an azimuthally-averaged r_ h =9.1' a_ h, p 11 8 ; drlica2015 . The analysis using DES DR1 by measured a slightly smaller a_ h, p 9 2 , consistent within 1.3 with the value. From the DES Y3A2 catalog, published the most recent structural parameters for Tuc IV, reporting a_ h, p 9 3 .

Our two ACS pointings would cover approximately the inner one-third of the target, so we did not attempt to characterize the morphology with our HST data. published the first spectroscopic study of Tuc IV, significantly resolving a velocity dispersion of 4.3 ^ +1.7 _ 1.0 . From eight RGB stars, they measured a metallicity dispersion of 0.18 ^ +0.20 _ 0.18 dex. calculated a mass-to-light ratio of 3100 ^ +2900 _ 1600 M_ /L_ , indicating a dark-matter dominated nature, and thus spectroscopically confirmed Tuc IV as a galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper measures Tucana IV but notes its diffuse structure, foreground overlap, and few high-probability members make its systemic proper motion difficult.

quotes.quote: Tucana IV has few members with high probability but many with medium probability. Similar to Grus II, we find that the MW foreground overlaps with the Tucana IV proper motion and makes recovery of the satellite systemic proper motion difficult. Because of its low surface brightness and large size the mixture model has difficulty separating nearby members from the MW. As mentioned in sec:new , the number of members from the likelihood fit ( p_i = 16 ) is much smaller than the expected number of members based on its luminosity and distance ( N_ expected = 29 5 ; Table table:pm ). In fact, our method has trouble identifying any high probability members outside the half-light radius; only 3 stars outside the half-light radius have p_i>0.5 .

gmw_00006930.name: RBS 0281
gmw_00006930.overview: RBS 0281 is a Seyfert 1 active galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0101137]. It was previously misidentified with the radio source 3C 59, which may represent either a background quasar or a radio lobe belonging to RBS 0281 [arXiv:astro-ph/0101137].

sources.summary: The paper treats RBS 0281 as a Seyfert galaxy formerly confused with 3C 59, notes ISOPHOT coverage, and flags uncertain millimeter photometry from mispointing.

quotes.quote: The latter was previously misidentified with the Seyfert 1 galaxy RBS 0281. The maps of Meurs Unger (1991) show three components: a north-western source (the original 3C 59), a central source associated with RBS 0281, and a south-eastern source. 3C 59 has no optical counterpart, so it is either a lobe of RBS 0281, or a background core-dominated quasar. With ISOPHOT only RBS 0281 was observed. We shall refer to the Seyfert galaxy as RBS 0281, and to the north-eastern hotspot/background object as 3C 59.

Due to unintentional mispointing, for 3C 59 and RBS 0281 the targeted positions differ from the radio positions in the maps of Meurs Unger (1991, see also Table 2). A signal is measured at the targeted position toward RBS 0281, which however is 10 \ off the radio peak, so that the millimeter flux from the peak radio position could well be higher than observed.

gmw_00006931.name: 3C 334
gmw_00006931.overview: The object 3C 334 is a quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0101137, arXiv:astro-ph/0005196] that exhibits strong variability at millimeter wavelengths [arXiv:astro-ph/0101137] and possible variability in X-ray observations [arXiv:astro-ph/9907034]. It displays solid far-infrared detections [arXiv:astro-ph/0005196] and shows residual extended emission on scales of tens of arcseconds [arXiv:astro-ph/9907034].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 334 as a quasar with evidence for strong millimeter variability, contrasting prior OVRO flux expectations with a much lower MAMBO upper limit.

quotes.quote: The quasars in the sample show signs of relativistic beaming at millimeter wavelengths, and the quasar 3C 334 shows evidence for strong variability.

3C 334 is probably a variable millimeter source. Previous observations with the Owens Valley Radio Observatory found a 3 mm flux density of 37 2 mJy (van Bemmel et al. 1998). Assuming F_ ^ 1 , a 1.2 mm flux of 15 mJy would be expected, much higher than our upper limit of 3 mJy. 3C 334 is known to be variable at 4.85 GHz, with variations of order 10 over a few decades (van Bemmel et al. 1998). Variability tends to increase at higher frequencies, but one would not expect a dramatic change in flux such as implied by the OVRO and MAMBO measurements.

sources.summary: The quasar 3C 334 appears point-like in PSPC data but shows residual HRI extension and possible variability after dewobbling and profile modeling.

quotes.quote: 3C 334 This quasar appears point-like in a PSPC observation. A long HRI observation shows some extension in a NW-SE direction but, as this is in the same direction as a `hot line' at the edge of the image, it seems plausible that much of the extension is due to aspect smearing. However, when the source is dewobbled, radial profiling reveals some residual extension on scales of tens of arcseconds. The best-fitting model (Table profile ) has significant extended emission. Such a model is consistent with the PSPC radial profile, although the total count rate in the HRI observation is considerably higher than would be inferred from the PSPC data, suggesting variability.

sources.summary: The paper reports solid far-infrared detections of quasar 3C 334, finding it brighter than paired radio galaxy 3C 19, with beamed emission insufficient to explain the difference.

quotes.quote: In the pair 3C 334 and 3C 19, the radio galaxy is not detected in any band, whereas the quasar has three solid detections (lowest S/N is 6). Since the upper limits take the difference of the backgrounds into account, the radio galaxy is definitely fainter than its paired quasar.

The only exception could be 3C 334, where a maximum of about 10 beamed radiation might be present in the infrared, confirming previous results (van Bemmel et al. 1998). Still, this is not enough to explain the difference between 3C 19 and 3C 334, given that their flux densities appear to differ by about a factor of two.

sources.summary: The core fit leaves low-energy residuals suggestive of a soft excess, but adding thermal or power-law components does not improve the reduced-chi-squared fit.

quotes.quote: 3C 334 : a reduced ^2 of 1.35 suggests that an additional component may be necessary. However, the addition of a thermal or power-law component does not improve the fit. Some residuals are observed at low energy (below 0.7 keV) suggesting a soft excess.

gmw_00006932.name: 3C 460
gmw_00006932.overview: The elliptical radio galaxy 3C 460 contains a cold dust mass of 2.4 times 10 to the 8th solar masses [arXiv:astro-ph/0005196, arXiv:astro-ph/0101137]. This dust is likely associated with star formation in the host galaxy, which may be related to a close companion galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0005196].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 460 as a radio galaxy with strong 160 micron emission, cold dust, and a likely host-galaxy star-formation contribution possibly linked to a companion.

quotes.quote: The pair consisting of quasar 3C 323.1 and radio galaxy 3C 460 displays a difference at the two short wavelengths. The radio galaxy is not detected at 60 m and has a flux density well below that of the quasar at 90 m. However, an interesting change occurs at long wavelengths; the 160 m flux density of 3C 460 exceeds the 3C 323.1 value. Below we will argue that cool dust associated with star formation is likely responsible for this luminous 160 m radiation.

Strong evidence for a host galaxy contribution comes from 3C 460, where a cold dust mass of 2.4 10^8  M _ is observed. This amount of dust is most likely due to star formation in the host galaxy, which could be related to a close companion galaxy at 1.5 separation (de Koff et al. 1996).

sources.summary: The paper says 3C 460 is an elliptical radio galaxy where a cold-dust explanation for the 1.2 mm emission is possible but problematic and uncertain.

quotes.quote: In 3C 460, e.g., the 1.2 mm flux could be dominated by thermal dust emission if <1.4 or T<16 , but then the implied dust mass would be larger than 5 10^8 M _ . Such a large dust mass is hard to reconcile with 3C 460 being an elliptical galaxy.
In our data the opposite is true, and therefore the millimeter emission is probably dominated by synchrotron emission, except maybe for 3C 460, where this case is not so clear.
Any thermal contribution to the millimeter emission arising from cold dust is estimated to be less than 15 , except for 3C 460, where the case is not clear.

sources.summary: 3CR 460 shows only extended emission in the NICMOS image, so the paper sets an upper limit on its infrared nuclear luminosity consistent with the color-profile measurement.

quotes.quote: 3CR 460 : 3CR 460 has been observed with NICMOS (F205W filter). From the NIC2 image in the public archive the emission is extended, and there is no clear sign of a nuclear point-like source. We set as an upper limit the light excess of the central circular aperture of radius 0 17, obtaining L_ < 10 ^ 42.82 0.04 at 2.05 m, consistent with our measurement of 10 ^ 42.79 0.19 at 2.12 m.

gmw_00006933.name: component `B'
gmw_00006933.overview: Component 'B' is a dusty, gas-rich galaxy undergoing a massive burst of star formation [arXiv:0808.2188]. It is coincident with an extremely red active galactic nucleus and is located approximately 30 kiloparsecs from the high-redshift radio galaxy core [arXiv:0808.2188].

sources.summary: The paper identifies component B as a dusty, gas-rich starburst/AGN in the 4C 60.07 system, with broad CO emission and a steep red infrared SED.

quotes.quote: Spitzer imaging shows that one of these components (`B') is coincident with an extremely red active galactic nucleus (AGN), offset by 4 arcsec ( 30 kpc) from the HzRG core.

In contrast, the SED of component `B' climbs steeply to _ obs 8.0 m. It is fit well by a S_ ^ 3.3 power law: an extremely red galaxy, akin to HR 10 (= ERO J164502+4626.4) at z 1.4 which has excellent coverage of its SED -- see Fig. sed dey99, stern06 . The value of S_ 8.0 m /S_ 4.5 m seen for component `B' ( 6.3 1.5 , or [ 4.5 ] [ 8.0 ] = 2.0) is unusual: redder than any SMG in the SHADES sample ivison07 , and ranking with the reddest of the 8- m-selected objects explored by magdis08 .

We identify component `B' as the other high-mass component, as evident from its very broad CO(4--3) line emission -- a dusty, gas-rich galaxy, as evident from the submm emission, undergoing a huge burst of star formation.

gmw_00006934.name: FDS16 DWARF257
gmw_00006934.overview: FDS16 DWARF257 is a dwarf galaxy located in the Fornax cluster [arXiv:2405.13500]. It is classified as a globular-cluster-rich system, hosting an estimated 12.2 globular clusters, which is two to three times more than average for its stellar mass [arXiv:2405.13500].

sources.summary: The paper identifies FDS16 DWARF257 as one of three GC-rich Fornax dwarf galaxies with an above-average globular-cluster count, comparable to GC-rich ultra-diffuse galaxies.

quotes.quote: Some of the dwarf galaxies show a higher than average GC number, namely FDS16 DWARF257, FDS11 DWARF294, and FDS11 DWARF306 with estimated GC numbers of 12.2, 13.0 and 11.1 (background-subtracted and completeness-corrected). These GC-rich dwarf galaxies are indicated in the upper panel of Fig. gc-dwarf-result3 and are located above the black curve.

In this context, The GC numbers of the three GC-rich dwarf galaxies (FDS16 DWARF257, FDS11 DWARF294, FDS11 DWARF306) seem to be an extension of the GC-rich UDGs with 2 to 3 times more GCs than average at a given stellar mass. These dwarf galaxies are a UDG by definition (FDS11 DWARF306 with R_ e =1.45 kpc is marginally a UDG).

gmw_00006935.name: FDS11 DWARF294
gmw_00006935.overview: FDS11 DWARF294 is a dwarf galaxy located in the Fornax cluster [arXiv:2405.13500]. It is classified as a globular-cluster-rich system, hosting an estimated 13.0 globular clusters, which is two to three times more than average for its stellar mass [arXiv:2405.13500].

gmw_00006936.name: FDS11 DWARF306
gmw_00006936.overview: FDS11 DWARF306 is a dwarf galaxy located in the Fornax cluster [arXiv:2405.13500]. It is classified as a globular cluster-rich galaxy that marginally qualifies as an ultra-diffuse galaxy due to its effective radius of 1.45 kiloparsecs [arXiv:2405.13500].

sources.summary: The paper identifies FDS11 DWARF306 as one of three GC-rich Fornax dwarf galaxies with an above-average globular-cluster count, comparable to GC-rich ultra-diffuse galaxies.

gmw_00006937.name: NGC 783
gmw_00006937.overview: NGC 783 is a spiral galaxy [arXiv:1205.3910]. Spectroscopic observations of its disk have been used to determine the radial distribution, central intercept values, and gradients of oxygen and nitrogen abundances across its H II regions [arXiv:1205.3910].

sources.summary: The paper reports BTA spectroscopy of H II regions in NGC 783, deriving oxygen and nitrogen abundances, electron temperatures, extinction, and radial abundance information across the galaxy disk.

quotes.quote: Here we report spectra of 63 H ii regions in a sample of six spiral galaxies: NGC 628, NGC 783, NGC 2336, NGC 6217, NGC 7331, and NGC 7678. A first motivation of the spectral observations is the determination of the oxygen and nitrogen abundances and electron temperatures in individual H ii regions using the recent NS- and ON calibration pilyugin2010,pilyuginmattsson2011 . The abundances will be used in the computation of the grid of evolutionary models of star clusters embedded in studied H ii regions.

Spectroscopic observations of 63 H ii regions in six spiral galaxies (NGC 628, NGC 783, NGC 2336, NGC 6217, NGC 7331, and NGC 7678) were carried out with the 6-meter telescope (BTA) of Russian Special Astrophysical Observatory with the Spectral Camera attached to the focal reducer SCORPIO in the multislit mode with a dispersion of 2.1 /pixel and a spectral resolution of 10 . These observations were used to estimate the oxygen and nitrogen abundances and the electron temperatures in H ii regions through the recent variant of the strong line method (NS calibration). The parameters of the radial distribution (the extrapolated central intercept value and the gradient) of the oxygen and nitrogen abundances in the disks of spiral galaxies NGC 628, NGC 783, NGC 2336, NGC 7331, and NGC 7678 have been determined. The abundances in the NGC 783, NGC 2336, NGC 6217, and NGC 7678 are measured for the first time.

gmw_00006938.name: NGC 6217
gmw_00006938.overview: NGC 6217 is a nearby barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:1708.06155, arXiv:2306.10136] featuring a central ring [arXiv:astro-ph/0504393] and a nuclear starburst [arXiv:1708.06155, arXiv:astro-ph/0107161]. The galaxy hosts a low-luminosity active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1708.06155] and exhibits a candidate one-sided X-ray jet structure [arXiv:1708.06155, arXiv:astro-ph/0107161].

sources.summary: The paper reports BTA spectroscopy of H II regions in NGC 6217, deriving oxygen and nitrogen abundances, electron temperatures, extinction, and radial abundance information across the galaxy disk.

quotes.quote: Here we report spectra of 63 H ii regions in a sample of six spiral galaxies: NGC 628, NGC 783, NGC 2336, NGC 6217, NGC 7331, and NGC 7678. A first motivation of the spectral observations is the determination of the oxygen and nitrogen abundances and electron temperatures in individual H ii regions using the recent NS- and ON calibration pilyugin2010,pilyuginmattsson2011 . The abundances will be used in the computation of the grid of evolutionary models of star clusters embedded in studied H ii regions.
