This supports strongly the second infall scenario where the NGC 4839 passed the Coma center about 1.6 Gyr ago, and began the second infall after reaching the apocenter in the south-west recently.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4839 as the central galaxy of Coma’s southwest subcluster, giving its stellar mass and using the associated halo, X-ray emission, and gas fraction to compare feedback simulations.

quotes.quote: Figure fig_xraymap shows the X-ray map around the Coma cluster and as discussed earlier, the map contains two prominent structures: a subcluster in the southwest and a shock front in the central region. The central galaxy of the subcluster is NGC 4839, with a stellar mass of M_*/ =11.07 Yang2007ApJ . According to the stellar mass-halo mass relations of the empirical models e.g. Behroozi2019 , the halo mass of the subcluster is estimated to be M_ h / =13.5 . The halo mass of the corresponding simulated subcluster is M_ 200c / =13.6 , consistent with the halo mass of NGC 4839.

As shown in Figure fig_xraymap , the X-ray emission from the subcluster is quite prominent in the observational data, and in the two simulations without AGN feedback, but is quite weak in the three simulations with AGN. The X-ray luminosities of the NGC 4938 subcluster in G3-H-CM and GZ-SBnA-CM are comparable and about 10 to 20 times higher than those in the other three simulations with AGN feedback. estimated the gas mass and total mass within the central 98 of the NGC 4839 subcluster using X-ray data and weak lensing, respectively. They obtained a gas fraction of 6.7 .

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 4839 as a Coma substructure reference, comparing UDG velocities to its recession velocity and treating it as a recent-infall subgroup.

quotes.quote: The kinematics of the UDGs from our outer mask most likely reflect the peculiar local effect within the substructure centered on NGC 4839. This well-known substructure has been identified in several spatio-velocity, X-ray profile, and stellar population studies of the Coma cluster ( Biviano_1996, Colless_1996, Briel_2001, Neumann_2003, Adami_2005, Smith_2009, Smith_2012 , etc.). The 4 UDGs from our outer mask have a mean radial velocity of 7630 \ , comparable with the recession velocity of NGC 4839, i.e., 7360 \ .
Galaxies with smaller R_ proj V_ los have been in the cluster longer, and vice versa, such that NGC 4874 is at the center of the cluster potential and NGC 4839 is a relatively recent infall.

We emphasize that most of these galaxies are not co-spatial with NGC 4839 despite their similar accretion epochs. There is thus evidence that the NGC 4839 subgroup may not be the sole driver of the trends we observed earlier in the cluster outskirts, although there is a noticeable overdensity of UDGs and dwarfs with accretion epochs similar to NGC 4839.

sources.summary: The paper places NGC 4839 and its associated group in the Coma cosmic web, finding no southwestern filament and favoring a post-merger path through the cluster core.

quotes.quote: On the same side of the Coma cluster, southern of the Neumann2003 shock, is located the NGC4839 group. This galaxy and the associated group present a well detected feature both in the X-rays and in the SZ signal. The most common explanation for the presence of this feature is that these objects are infalling on the cluster for the first time from a filament located in the direction of Abell 1367, SW of the Coma cluster see e.g. Neumann2001, Neumann2003, Adami2005, Adami2007LF, BrownRudnick2011, OgreanBruggen2013 . We do not detect the presence of such a filament in the Cosmic Web. While there is indeed a filament connecting to Coma in the direction of Abell 1367, it is the aforementioned E-W aligned filament, with no structure connecting to the cluster at the SW corner where NGC 4839 is located.

Although this finding is in clear disagreement with the scenario of first infall for the NGC 4839 group, it is actually very consistent with recently proposed analyses e.g. Sheardown2019, Lyskova2019 which advocate for a post-merger scenario for NGC 4839, where the galaxy has crossed the cluster core already once and is now coming out the other side.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC4839 as having a prominent cD envelope, detected rotation and central KDC, and SSP ages compared with Coma BCG literature values.

quotes.quote: NGC4839 Surface brightness profiles were published by Schombert (1986), Oemler (1976) as well as Jord an et al. \ (2004), who all confirm the presence of a very prominent cD envelope. Rotation of the order 44 km s ^ 1 and a KDC in the centre of the galaxy are detected (Paper 1). Table Toorberg shows the SSP-equivalent ages and metallicities for the three BCGs in the Coma cluster from the literature (as summarised by Trager et al. \ 2008). The data from this study is for 1 8 R _ e . For Mehlert et al. \ (2000), S a nchez-Bl a zquez et al. \ (2006b) and Trager et al. \ (2008) the data is for 2.7 arcsec diameter equivalent circular apertures (by Trager et al. \ 2008), and for the other references as originally published.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4839 as the brightest galaxy of the merging group, associated with 5C4.51 and showing connected radio lobes with unresolved nucleus and measurable polarization.

quotes.quote: The radio source 5C4.51 is identified with NGC 4839, the brightest galaxy of the group NGC 4839, which is currently merging with the Coma cluster. The redshift of the source is 0.25 2000MNRAS.313..469S . 5C4.51 is located at 0.6^ from the Coma cluster centre. The source largest extension is 1' , and it is composed of two extended and connected radio lobes, while the nucleus is not resolved. The source is polarised on average at the 7 and 9 level at 1.485 GHz and 4.935 GHz, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4839 as the BCG of an infalling Coma group with a wide-angle-tailed radio source and diffuse trailing radio emission.

quotes.quote: 5: NGC 4839 The optical host is an SA0, a low/average surface brightness disk-dominated galaxy Oemler1976 , and is the BCG of the NGC 4839 group KentandGunn . It lies in the cluster outskirts ( 1 Mpc in projection) southwest of the cluster center. X-ray observations of the Coma cluster have revealed a wealth of substructures and one of the most prominent is the infalling group of galaxies associated with the galaxy NGC 4839. The associated radio source has a wide-angle tailed morphology, i.e., the two-sided radio jet with slight distortions emerging in P.A. 0 ^ with a bow-shock-like feature with indications of ram pressure stripping, which suggests that the NGC 4839 group is falling into the Coma cluster 2001A&A...365L..74N . The high-resolution image, along with the low-angular resolution image of the source in the 250--500 MHz band (see Fig. fig:n4839-xray ), suggests there are two finger-like features emanating toward the southwest. These images also suggest that there is low-surface brightness diffuse radio emission toward the west, which trails behind the wide-angle-tailed radio galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 4839 using photometric data because significant rotation is absent; its triaxiality is poorly constrained, and its steadily rising ellipticity is noted in the sample handling.

quotes.quote: NGC 1600, NGC 4374, NGC 4636 and NGC 4839. Because of the lack of any significant rotation in these galaxies, photometric data alone is used to estimate their shape likelihood distributions. The triaxialities of these four galaxies are therefore poorly constrained. They are included on the grounds that omitting them could bias our results away from strongly triaxial systems, most of which are probably slowly rotating. In each case, the data are averaged from the center out to the largest radius for which kinematic data is available (see Table tbl-1 ). The ellipticity of NGC 1600, NGC 4374 and NGC 4636 varies by about 0.10 in this interval, but the ellipticity of NGC 4839 rises steadily from 0.20 near the center to 0.50 at 32 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4839 as the true radio host, discusses its mixed morphology classifications, extended low-surface-brightness component, and largely point-like net X-ray counts.

quotes.quote: B2 1254+27 There is a large discrepancy between the positions given for this object in the NASA Extragalactic Database (NED) and the SIMBAD Astronomical Database. This is because the radio source has, in some cases, been incorrectly associated with the galaxy NGC 4819 rather than the true host galaxy for the radio emission which is NGC 4839. NGC 4839 is classified confusingly as morphological type S0 (Eskridge Pooge 1991), E/S0 (Jorgensen, Franx Kjaegaard 1992) or as a cD (Gonzalez-Serrano, Carballo Perez-Fournon 1993; Fisher, Illingworth Franx 1995). Andreon et al. (1996) also mention that the low average surface brightness suggests that this galaxy is dominated by an extended disc.

sources.summary: The paper calls NGC4839 an unusually located Coma cD galaxy with a prominent envelope, and detects both rotation and a central kinematically decoupled core.

quotes.quote: NGC4839: The location of this cD galaxy far from the centre of the Coma cluster is unusual. Surface brightness profiles were published by Schombert (1986), Oemler (1976) as well as Jord an et al. \ (2004), who all confirm the presence of a very prominent cD envelope. Rotation of the order 44 km s ^ 1 and a KDC in the centre of the galaxy are detected.

gmw_00007012.name: Vd-17871
gmw_00007012.overview: Vd-17871 is a high-redshift Lyman-break galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of z = 4.622 [arXiv:1412.3799]. The system consists of two elongated ultraviolet components separated by 1.5 arcseconds, suggesting a galaxy merger, with compact rest-frame far-infrared emission associated with the northern component [arXiv:1412.3799, arXiv:1802.07751].

sources.summary: The paper describes Vd-17871 as an LBG with faint radio emission, submillimeter follow-up, MAMBO detection, z=4.622 spectroscopy, and two components suggesting a merger.

quotes.quote: Vd-17871 has initially been identified as an Lyman-break galaxy (LBG) exhibiting faint radio emission (consistent with the properties of e.g. \ AzTEC1 and J1000+0234). Subsequently it was followed up in the (sub-)millimeter wavelength regime. It has been detected at 1.2 mm with MAMBO on the IRAM 30 m telescope (A. Karim et al., in prep.). Keck II/DEIMOS, VLT/VIMOS, and PdBI spectroscopy (A. Karim et al., in prep.) have shown that Vd-17871 is located at a redshift of z_ spec =4.622 , and that it breaks-up into two components, separated by 1 5. Karim et al. \ show that both components are at the same (spectroscopic) redshift, suggesting a galaxy merger.

sources.summary: Vd-17871 has two elongated UV components at the same spectroscopic redshift, with compact rest-frame FIR emission associated with the northern component.

quotes.quote: Vd-17871 : This system has two main UV components 1 5 apart (Vd-17871-N and Vd-17871-S), both with elongated morphologies. Both North and South components are spectroscopically confirmed at the same redshift Karim et al., in prep smolcic15 . The compact rest-frame FIR emission is associated with the North component.

gmw_00007013.name: AK03
gmw_00007013.overview: AK03 is a high-redshift system initially identified as a Lyman-break galaxy with faint radio emission [arXiv:1412.3799]. The system consists of two components separated by 0.9 arcseconds in the optical that may be merging [arXiv:1412.3799].

sources.summary: The paper describes AK03 as a two-component high-redshift system with faint radio and submillimeter detections, redshift constraints for both components, and a possible merger interpretation.

quotes.quote: AK03 has initially been identified as an LBG exhibiting faint radio emission (consistent with the properties of, e.g., AzTEC1 and J1000+0234), and subsequently followed up in the (sub-)millimeter wavelength regime. It is a 3.7 detection in the SCUBA-2 450 m map ( , their source 450.61), and a tentative, 3 , detection in the JCMT/AzTEC map (similar to J1000+0234; ; M. Yun, priv. comm.). The system, showing two components with a projected separation of 0 9 in the optical, lies 1 3 away from the SCUBA-2 source, and 7 away from the position of the AzTEC source.

A Keck II/DEIMOS spectrum of the Northern component of AK03 (AK03-N hereafter) puts it at a redshift of z_ spec =4.747 , while the Southern component (AK03-S hereafter), detected also in the UltraVISTA near-IR and radio maps, has a comparable photometric redshift. Following the photometric-redshift computation described in detail in Ilbert et al. \ (2009) for AK03-S (coincident with radio emission; see Fig. fig:stamps ) we find a photometric redshift of z=4.40 0.10 if galaxy templates ( ) are used or z=4.65 0.10 if quasar templates ( ) are employed. The ^2 statistics suggest a roughly equally good fit for the galaxy and quasar libraries, and the errors on the photometric redshift reported reflect the uncertainties obtained via a comparison with spectroscopic redshifts. Thus, the two (North and South) components of AK03 may be associated, and perhaps merging.

gmw_00007014.name: QSO J000958.66+015755.18
gmw_00007014.overview: QSO J000958.66+015755.18 is a high-redshift quasar located at a redshift of z = 2.973 [arXiv:2101.08218]. Its spectrum features a ghostly damped Lyman-alpha system at a redshift of z = 2.97635, which is best modeled by a wind with a 30-degree inclination [arXiv:2101.08218].

sources.summary: The paper models QSO J000958.66+015755.18 as the highest-SNR ghostly DLA case, giving its quasar and DLA redshifts, emission-line treatment, and best-fit wind geometry.

quotes.quote: Among the 7 spectra only three have a high enough signal-to-noise ratio (SNR > 10) to perform a realistic fit with our models, QSO J000958.66+015755.18 having the highest SNR (SNR > 20). Fit of QSO J000958.66+015755.18 The spectrum of QSO J000958.66+015755.18 has the highest SNR in the sample of ghostly DLAs. In addition, the redshift of the quasar is z_ QSO =2.973 which means that the Lyman- line is seen in the spectrum. The ghostly DLA is at z_ DLA =2.97635 , derived from the numerous strong metal absorption lines.

Before comparing the observations with our different models, we have to remove the N v contribution from the quasar emission. We fit a Gaussian emission located at _ rest = 1240.1 and remove it from the spectrum. During the fit we have excluded the pixels affected by strong absorptions unrelated to our system and located around 4000 , 2000 and 6000 km s ^ 1 . The best fit of the QSO Lyman- emission line is displayed on Fig. fig:fit_obs_7 . The fit is a wind model with an inclination relative to the line of sight of 30°.

gmw_00007015.name: QSO J124202.03-002209.00
gmw_00007015.overview: QSO J124202.03-002209.00 is a high-redshift quasar located at a redshift of 2.37925 [arXiv:2101.08218]. The system features an associated damped Lyman-alpha system at a redshift of 2.3792, which has been modeled with a wind profile and a high neutral hydrogen column density [arXiv:2101.08218].

sources.summary: The paper briefly fits QSO J124202.03-002209.00, giving the quasar and DLA redshifts, finding no narrow component is needed, and deriving a wind-model H I column density.

quotes.quote: QSO J124202.03-002209.00 has z_ QSO =2.37925 and z_ DLA =2.3792 . The fit displayed on Fig. fig:fit_obs_2 shows that no narrow component is needed to reproduce the spectrum. The fit is a wind model with a 60° inclination and log N (H i ) = 21.2 .

gmw_00007016.name: QSO J125437.96+315530.84
gmw_00007016.overview: QSO J125437.96+315530.84 is a high-redshift quasar located at a redshift of 2.299 [arXiv:2101.08218]. The system features a damped Lyman-alpha system at a redshift of 2.301 and is modeled with a weak narrow component and a wind [arXiv:2101.08218].

sources.summary: The paper briefly fits QSO J125437.96+315530.84, reporting the quasar and DLA redshifts, a weak narrow component, and a wind model with log N(H I)=21.4.

quotes.quote: QSO J125437.96+315530.84 has z_ QSO =2.299 and z_ DLA =2.301 . The fit is presented on Fig. fig:fit_obs_6 and one can see that a narrow component is needed but its contribution is weak. The fit is a wind model with an inclination of 40° and log N (H i ) = 21.4 .

gmw_00007017.name: J1333--3141
gmw_00007017.overview: J1333--3141 is a head-tail radio galaxy located in the A3562 cluster [arXiv:2206.06176]. Its radio tail connects to a faint, straight radio filament that extends approximately 200 kpc eastward [arXiv:2206.06176].

sources.summary: MeerKAT imaging shows J1333--3141 as a head-tail radio galaxy whose tail connects to a faint, straight radio filament in the A3562 core.

quotes.quote: The head-tail radio source J1333--3141 and surrounding diffuse radio halo were known before V03, G05, 2000MNRAS.314..594V, 2018A&A...620A..25D . The head-tail source is located 1^ =60 kpc (projected) away from BCG and is associated with a cluster member galaxy with a radial velocity cz=15068 km s ^ 1 that is only 283 km s ^ 1 (about one third of the velocity dispersion, =769 30 km s ^ 1 ) from the cluster mean velocity ( cz=14786 km s ^ 1 ; Haines et al. 2018). The improved sensitivity and angular resolution of the new MeerKAT image reveals new details and previously-unknown features in A3562, that we describe in the next sections.
The radio filament appears to branch out at about a right angle from the J1333--3141 tail and can be traced for approximately 200 kpc eastward (see the zoomed-in images in Fig.  fig:meerkat_fila ).

gmw_00007018.name: Brightest Cluster Galaxy
gmw_00007018.overview: The Brightest Cluster Galaxy in A3562 hosts a weak radio source with a total flux of 0.78 mJy [arXiv:2206.06176]. This radio emission consists of a compact component aligned with the optical peak and a structure extending 10 kpc southward [arXiv:2206.06176].

sources.summary: The A3562 brightest cluster galaxy hosts a very faint, steep-spectrum radio source, interpreted as starved of accretion fuel by cluster-core sloshing.

quotes.quote: The BCG emission Another feature unveiled by the MeerKAT image of A3562 is weak radio emission at the position of the BCG. In Fig.  fig:bcg , we show the 1284 MHz radio contours overlaid on the VLT Survey Telescope (VST)-OmegaCAM g -band image of the galaxy from the Shapley Supercluster Survey (ShaSS, Merluzzi et al. 2015). The radio emission consists of a compact component with 0.47 0.02 mJy flux and coincident with the optical peak, and an additional structure extending 10 kpc southward and with a flux of 0.31 0.02 mJy. The total radio flux is 0.78 0.04 mJy and the luminosity at 1284 MHz is 4 10^ 21 W Hz ^ 1 .

The BCG radio spectrum is quite steep ( =-1.1 , sec:bcgsp ), suggesting the radio source is old and possibly unfed. The XMM image in Fig.  fig:bcgxmm shows that the gas density peak may in fact be offset from the BCG, likely by the same sloshing motions that have created the cold front southwest of the cluster center.

gmw_00007019.name: NGC 3656
gmw_00007019.overview: NGC 3656 is a disturbed merger remnant [arXiv:0811.1381] classified as an elliptical galaxy with shells [arXiv:0801.0591, arXiv:0911.0720], though its morphological classification remains uncertain [arXiv:0911.0720]. The galaxy features a warped dust disk [arXiv:0801.0591, arXiv:0811.1381] and star formation that powers its radio and infrared emission [arXiv:0811.1381].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 3656 as an early-age major-merger elliptical with H I shells, tidal tails, warped dust emission, asymmetric extinction, and similarities to Centaurus A.

quotes.quote: Resolved imaging of NGC 3656 by bal01 showed that H I occurs in shells and tidal tails that are thought to be signatures of a recent (or early-age ), major merger of this galaxy. The H I is more extended than the galaxy's main optical component. Sensitive CCD optical images of the dust features in NGC 3656 were studied by bal97 and rot06 , who suggest that the dust is a remnant of a dust-rich merger that involved this galaxy's primary optical elliptical component. Figure fig:ngc3656 shows that large-scale, 350 m-dust continuum spurs in the southeast of NGC 3656 generally follow the CO-emission and optical spurs respectively presented by you02 and bal97 .

Figure fig:ngc3656 also shows that the extinction contours of NGC 3656 are very asymmetric, depicting strong extinction in the galactic east and dense north-south central dust distribution. The dust in NGC 3656 is probably in a thin, warped disk that provides the strongest extinction where the disk is tangent to the line of slight.

sources.summary: NGC 3656 is treated as a disturbed merger remnant with a molecular/dust disk where radio, 24 micron, and CO morphologies indicate star formation.

quotes.quote: NGC 3656 NGC 3656 is the most disturbed galaxy of this sample, being a clear example of a fairly recent merger remnant with a dramatic blue ring and shells. Figure n3656 shows that the molecular gas is located in a nearly edge-on disk of radius 10 \ = 1.9 kpc, also clearly visible as a dark dust lane bisecting the nucleus. The structure of the dust lane is somewhat irregular, suggesting a warped or folded lane rather than the very thin, flat, relaxed disks seen in NGC 4526 and NGC 4459 (Sections 4459 and 4526 ).

The radio continuum emission is clearly elongated in the same direction as the gas/dust disk, with a deconvolved Gaussian source size (HWHM) of 3.0 \ 1.4 \ lucero . The 24 \ emission is only modestly resolved but is also clearly elongated north-south, and is adequately fit by an exponential disk of scale length 2 \ 0.4 \ = 0.39 0.08 kpc and axis ratio 0.3:1.0 (Table fittable ). Thus, the morphologies of radio continuum, 24 , and CO emission are all consistent with the interpretation that star formation powers the bulk of the radio and 24 \ emission.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 3656 as morphologically uncertain, citing peculiar and irregular classifications plus near-infrared work describing it as a disturbed elliptical with shells and a dust lane.

quotes.quote: Two galaxies have uncertain classification (NGC 3656 and NGC 5666).

NGC 3656 is classified as (R')I0: pec by NED. Its morphological classification is not reported in the HyperLeda catalog, and is listed as non-Magellanic irregular in RC2 catalog. From near infrared photometry, Wiklind, Combes Henkel (1995) classify NGC3656 as a disturbed elliptical with an envelope of faint shells and a dust lane. They point out that it could be also considered a face-on S0.

gmw_00007020.name: UGC 1503
gmw_00007020.overview: UGC 1503 is a quiescent field elliptical galaxy that shows no current optical signatures of a merger or accretion event [arXiv:0801.0591]. It contains a slightly edge-on molecular and dust disk where star formation is occurring in an annular structure [arXiv:0801.0591, arXiv:0811.1381].

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 1503 as a quiescent or very-late-accretion field elliptical with no current optical merger signatures and slightly edge-on dust traced by CO and 350 micron contours.

quotes.quote: Of the ellipticals presented here, UGC 1503 and NGC 807 are the only object that are (1) field galaxies and (2) have no currently identified optical morphological signatures of a merger or accretion event. Therefore, they are referred to as quiescent or very-late-accretion, to mean that if there has been any possible accretion event during their evolutionary lives, that happened a very long time ago, and the galaxies have now settled beyond any such event. Being field galaxies also means that there is no near neighbor that may have interacted with them in the near past. UGC 1503 and NGC 807 have intrinsic 50 contours of the CO-emission and 350 m-continuum fluxes that are comparable in extent to and only slightly smaller than those of the optical. These galaxies probably have dust that is distributed in slightly edge-on disks with their near-side in the northeast and northwest for UGC 1503 and NGC 807 respectively.

sources.summary: UGC 1503 has a molecular and dust disk whose 24 micron, CO, radio, and FIR morphologies point to star formation in an annulus.

quotes.quote: UGC 1503 Figure u1503 presents optical, unsharp-masked, 24 , CO, and radio images of UGC 1503.

The radio continuum emission is also found in an asymmetric ring of similar size. The 24 \ image shows a central dip, which we have verified is not due to saturation, and this image is well fit with a ring of radius 4.8 \ 3.5 \ (1.6 1.2 kpc).

For these reasons we argue that the simplest explanation for the CO, radio and FIR morphologies of UGC 1503 is that star formation is taking place in an annulus of the molecular/dust disk. The FIR/radio flux density ratios support this interpretation, as discussed further in Section discussion .

gmw_00007021.name: NGC 807
gmw_00007021.overview: NGC 807 is a field elliptical galaxy [arXiv:2304.08552, arXiv:0801.0591] that features a low-surface-brightness spiral disk with dust lanes [arXiv:2304.08552] and faint tidal arms [arXiv:2304.08552]. The galaxy contains an asymmetric molecular gas disk [arXiv:2304.08552, arXiv:0801.0591] and exhibits nuclear, plateau, and envelope components in its 24-micron emission [arXiv:0811.1381].

sources.summary: NGC 807 is discussed as a disturbed elliptical possible parent, with unusual extended HI, HI clumps, tidal arms, asymmetric CO, and low-surface-brightness disk-like structure.

quotes.quote: NGC 807 is the next closest potential parent to AGC 123216, with a minimum projected separation of 231 8 kpc and a heliocentric velocity difference of 361 218 km s ^ 1 . It has been classified as an elliptical galaxy in multiple catalogs MCG1964, UGC1973, RC31991 . Since the vast majority of TDGs originate from interactions between gas-rich galaxies, an elliptical galaxy would not normally be considered as a likely progenitor (especially with two other potential spiral parents nearby). However, the extended amount of HI surrounding NGC 807 is unusual for an elliptical galaxy, and it has clearly been disturbed (Fig. fig:AGC123216full ). Fig. fig:velmaps (a) shows two small clumps of HI to the left and right of NGC 807 which appear to have similar heliocentric velocities to that galaxy.

found that the neutral hydrogen in NGC 807 has the double-horned line profile characteristic of a rotating disk of gas. Further studies with deeper optical imaging have even revealed faint tidal arms Lucero2013 . mapped the CO emission of NGC 807 and found that it was strongly asymmetric, with 70 of the CO mass being located in the southeast half of the galaxy; also estimated that the southeastern tail comprised 70 of the HI gas mass within the tails. mentions that the asymmetry of the CO gas would not be expected to persist for more than 1 Gyr due to shearing by differential rotation. Inspection of Fig. fig:NGC807 reveals that the bright bulge is in the center of a low surface brightness spiral disk with dust lanes (left panel), and there are several low surface brightness features (tidal arms) extending outwards in several directions (right panel).

sources.summary: NGC 807 shows nuclear, plateau, and envelope 24 micron components; the paper weighs star formation, AGN activity, circumstellar dust, and atomic-gas-associated dust.

quotes.quote: NGC 807 Figure n807 shows the 24 \ emission from NGC 807 along with an optical image, optical dust maps, CO line emission and 20 cm radio continuum emission. The dust distribution traced in the V-R color map and in the MGE residuals suggests a symmetric, dynamically relaxed disk. The 24 \ emission from NGC 807 shows an unresolved peak on the nucleus of the galaxy, an elongated plateau and a surrounding envelope.

The nuclear point source component of the 24 \ image might be related to either an AGN or to unresolved nuclear star formation. It is curious, though, that no corresponding nuclear peak is found in the radio continuum emission.

This component could conceivably originate in star formation in the molecular disk since its size scale is similar to that of the molecular gas. (The CO dist extends to radii 20 , and the plateau is flat to a semimajor axis 11 \ and declines with a scale length of 4 \ beyond.) The third component in NGC 807, the exponential component with a scale length of 17 , could also conceivably originate in star formation in the molecular gas or it could trace circumstellar emission.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 807 as a quiescent or very-late-accretion field elliptical with no current optical merger signatures and a dust/gas disk whose content is bulkier in the south.

quotes.quote: Of the ellipticals presented here, UGC 1503 and NGC 807 are the only object that are (1) field galaxies and (2) have no currently identified optical morphological signatures of a merger or accretion event. Therefore, they are referred to as quiescent or very-late-accretion, to mean that if there has been any possible accretion event during their evolutionary lives, that happened a very long time ago, and the galaxies have now settled beyond any such event. Being field galaxies also means that there is no near neighbor that may have interacted with them in the near past. UGC 1503 and NGC 807 have intrinsic 50 contours of the CO-emission and 350 m-continuum fluxes that are comparable in extent to and only slightly smaller than those of the optical. These galaxies probably have dust that is distributed in slightly edge-on disks with their near-side in the northeast and northwest for UGC 1503 and NGC 807 respectively.
you02 report that in NGC 807, 70 of the CO emission is detected in the south of the galaxy (see Figures fig:ngc807 ), suggesting the bulk of the disk's content is in the southern parts.

gmw_00007022.name: NGC 4476
gmw_00007022.overview: NGC 4476 is a dwarf elliptical galaxy located in the Virgo Cluster [arXiv:0801.0591] that hosts an embedded stellar disk with spiral arms [arXiv:0812.3272]. This disk contains externally accreted dust [arXiv:0801.0591], H II regions [arXiv:0801.0591, arXiv:0812.3272], and ongoing star formation [arXiv:0811.1381, arXiv:0812.3272].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4476 as a Virgo-cluster dwarf elliptical with externally accreted dust, H II regions, a relaxed CO disk, and submillimeter dust emission distinct from its stellar component.

quotes.quote: Despite directed searches, H I has not been detected in the cluster galaxy NGC 4476 (or NGC 759) e.g., luc05 ; this is in spite of CO-gas detections in these galaxies lee91, wik95, wik97, you02 . It is thought the H I may been destroyed by the hot cluster gas or, in the case or NGC 4476, by ram-pressure stripping luc05 . Using HST V (F555W) imaging, van95 showed that NGC 4476 has a regular, ``spiral-like'' dust lane that is about 20'' in diameter. They determined a difference of 19 degrees between the major axis position angle of the dust lane and that of the stellar component and used the evidence in this and a sample or other early galaxies to argue that the dust lane in NGC 4476 did not originate from the stellar component but was instead externally accreted .

tom00 confirmed the existence of the regular dust lane using HST E(V-I) [ F555W - F814W ] extinction images and showed that NGC 4476 has H II regions. They also noted a blue unresolved nucleus that they suggested may come from a young star cluster(s) associated with the dust system. you02 detected CO gas in a relaxed disk that is co-spatial with the dust lane and has a diameter of about 27'' .

Figure fig:ngc4476 shows 350 m-continuum contours of dust emission from NGC 4476 that generally follow the direction of the ellipticity and major axis position angle of those of the CO disk and SDSS-extinction maps except at parts of the 350 m-continuum at 30 flux levels.

sources.summary: NGC 4476 is described as a Virgo early-type galaxy with a dust and molecular disk, matching 24 micron morphology, and star formation evidence despite radio non-detection.

quotes.quote: NGC 4476 NGC 4476 is the second of our three Virgo Cluster early-type galaxies with well-developed dust disks (Figure n4476 ). The F475W image also shows numerous bright point sources embedded in the dust. As for NGC 4459 and NGC 4526, the molecular gas is confined to this disk, which has a semimajor axis of 11 \ = 0.9 kpc.

The bulk of the 24 \ emission can also be attributed to this dusty disk, as an excellent model of the image is achieved from a ring with its maximum intensity at a semimajor axis of 6 \ (0.5 kpc) and gradually decreasing intensities on the interior and exterior (Table fittable ). Thus, there is extremely good agreement between 24 \ morphology and CO morphology. No radio continuum emission is detected from NGC 4476, and lucero hypothesize that the magnetic field and/or the relativistic electrons may have been stripped by interactions with the intracluster medium.

However, GALEX images of NGC 4476 show significantly bluer colors at radii 12 , becoming as blue as (FUV-NUV) 1.2 mag in the central resolution element compared to (FUV-NUV) 2.5 mag in the outskirts of the galaxy gdp07 . In the absence of the radio continuum detection it is not obvious that the 24 \ emission indicates star formation activity, but the optical point sources and the GALEX colors strongly suggest this interpretation.

sources.summary: VCC 1250 hosts a large embedded disc with spiral arms, dust lanes, HII regions, prominent emission lines, ongoing star formation, and roughly solar metallicity.

quotes.quote: VCC 1250 = NGC 4476 (Fig figvcc1250 ). This galaxy hosts a large embedded disc with the spiral arms, dust lanes, and HII regions clearly visible on the HST ACS images presented in . Our data covers the region completely dominated by the disc. We see prominent emission lines suggesting on-going star formation. The luminosity weighted age is about 2 Gyr and the metallicity is as high as solar without statistically significant changes along the radius.

gmw_00007023.name: 4C +00.58
gmw_00007023.overview: 4C +00.58 is an X-shaped radio galaxy [arXiv:1005.4928, arXiv:0912.3001] featuring a bent eastern jet [arXiv:0912.3001] and faint wings [arXiv:1005.4928, arXiv:0912.3001]. The system contains X-ray cavities [arXiv:1005.4928] and stellar shells [arXiv:1005.4928, arXiv:1008.0789], which suggest a recent galaxy merger [arXiv:1005.4928, arXiv:1008.0789].

sources.summary: The paper presents 4C +00.58 as an odd X-shaped radio galaxy whose minor-axis jet, long wings, X-ray cavities, and possible stellar shell favor merger-induced jet reorientation over hydrodynamic models.

quotes.quote: In this Letter, we present a deep Chandra observation of 4C +00.58, an oddball XRG with its jet oriented along the minor axis. By using the X-ray data in tandem with available radio and optical data, we estimate relevant timescales with which to evaluate wing formation models. The hydrodynamic models have difficulty explaining the long wings, whereas the presence of X-ray cavities (suggesting jet activity along a prior axis) and a potential stellar shell (indicating a recent merger) favor a merger-induced reorientation model.

The primary lobes of the radio galaxy lie nearly on an east--west axis and have a well defined boundary, whereas the faint wings are oriented in a north--south direction. The jet experiences a dramatic bend (by 60 ^ ) just before terminating, and the cocoon in the 1.4 GHz map is notable for a well defined edge with a surface brightness about five times that of the wings. The 5 GHz map resolves the jet into a string of knots ( 3.3). We detect no counterjet.

sources.summary: 4C +00.58 is described as a candidate X-shaped radio galaxy whose high-resolution map shows a bent eastern jet and ambiguous diffuse X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: 4C +00.58 ( z=0.059 ) : 4C +00.58 was classified as a candidate XRG cheung07 due to faint wings in the FIRST image. A new, higher resolution radio map reveals that the jet to the east of the core is bent toward the northwest and enclosed in a more symmetric radio lobe. We detect the inner part of this jet in the X-ray band (confirmed by line-fitting to determine the PA using the bootstrap resampling method). The PA of the jet was determined very well, so we masked it from our fit. It is unclear whether the diffuse emission represents the ISM or the IGM.

sources.summary: The paper notes shells in 4C + 00.58 and argues that even its unusual jet orientation might fit a jet-shell collision scenario if gradual accretion-driven reorientation is valid.

quotes.quote: In addition, more direct evidence for merger has recently come from the recent detection of shells in two XRGs, 3C 403 (Almeida et al. \ 2011) and 4C + 00.58 (Hodges-Kluck et al. \ 2010b).
Shells have also been detected in the XRGs 3C 403 and 4C + 00.58 (Almeida et al. \ 2011; Hodges-Kluck, et al. 2010b).

Finally, the jet-shell collision scenario for the formation of wings may even apply in the exceptionally rare case of the XRG 4C + 00.58, where the jet appears aligned with the optical minor axis, provided a gradual jet reorientation via accretion, as inferred by Hodges-Kluck et al. \ (2010b), is a valid description of the system.

gmw_00007024.name: host of GRB 081008
gmw_00007024.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 081008 is a high-redshift system at z=1.9683 [arXiv:1108.1049, arXiv:1108.1084] that is best described as a dusty spiral galaxy [arXiv:1903.01353]. Its interstellar medium consists of at least three distinct components [arXiv:1108.1049, arXiv:1108.1084] and exhibits subsolar metallicity with modest optical extinction [arXiv:1108.1049].

sources.summary: The paper characterizes the GRB 081008 host galaxy at z=1.9683 as a multi-component, clumpy ISM with measured column density, subsolar metallicity range, dust depletion, and modest optical extinction.

quotes.quote: We study the GRB 081008 environment with simultaneous high- and low-resolution spectroscopy using UVES and FORS2 data acquired 5 hr after the Swift trigger. The interstellar medium (ISM) of the host galaxy at z=1.9683 is constituted by at least three components which contribute to the line profiles. Component I is the redmost one, and is 20 km/s and 78 km/s redward component II and III, respectively. We detect several ground state and excited absorption features in components I and II.

The hydrogen column density associated to GRB 081008 is N_ H / cm ^ 2 = 21.11 0.10 , and the metallicity of the host galaxy is in the range [ X/H ] = -1.29 to 0.52 . In particular, we found [ Fe/H ] =-1.19 0.11 and [ Zn/H ] =-0.52 0.11 with respect to solar values. This discrepancy can be explained by the presence of dust in the GRB ISM, given the opposite refractory properties of iron and zinc. By deriving the depletion pattern for GRB 081008, we find the optical extinction in the visual band to be A_V 0.19 mag.

sources.summary: The paper identifies the host of GRB 081008 at the burst redshift and uses absorption spectroscopy to infer a multicomponent ISM, metal abundances, dust depletion, and visual extinction.

quotes.quote: The GRB-Damped Lyman Alpha system at z = 1.9683 shows that the interstellar medium (ISM) of the host galaxy is constituted by at least three components which contribute to the line profiles. Component I is the redmost one, and is 20 km/s and 78 km/s redward component II and III, respectively. We detect several ground state and excited absorption features in components I and II. These features have been used to compute the distances between the GRB and the absorbers. Component I is found to be 52 6 pc away from the GRB, while component II presents few excited transitions and its distance is 200^ +60 _ 80 pc.

The hydrogen column density associated to GRB 081008 is N_ H / cm ^ 2 = 21.11 0.10 , and the metallicity of the host galaxy is in the range [ X/H ] = -1.29 to 0.52 . In particular, we found [ Fe/H ] =-1.19 0.11 and [ Zn/H ] =-0.52 0.11 with respect to solar values. This discrepancy can be explained by the presence of dust in the GRB ISM, given the opposite refractory properties of iron and zinc.

sources.summary: The paper models the z=1.968 GRB 081008 host with dust-inclusive chemical evolution, deriving an age of 0.32 Gyr and leaving its morphology uncertain but consistent with spiral or proto-spheroid possibilities.

quotes.quote: In Figure 081008 , we show the results for the host of GRB 081008 at z=1.968 studied by D'Elia et al. (2011). In order to assess the importance of dust in this kind of analysis, in Figure 081008 (only), are shown the predictions for elements, Zn and Ni relative to Fe both in the models with dust (left column) and without dust (right column).

Applying the method to the three GRBs analyzed in this paper, we find an age of 50 Myr for the galaxy hosting the GRB 120327A, 0.32 Gyr for the GRB081008 host and 15Myr for the GRB120815 host. These young ages indicate that we find GRBs to be "young" galaxies, namely galaxies in which the star formation is very active, probably suffering their first major star formation episode, but they are not necessary typical of the main population of galaxies that could have started forming stars already much before.

Our results suggest that one of the galaxies hosting GRB120815 is probably a massive proto-spheroid catched during the active star formation phase, whereas for the other two galaxies hosting GRB120327A and GRB081008, no firm conclusion can be drawn, although some of the data seem to suggest spiral or proto-spheroid systems with high mass and star formation rate.

sources.summary: The paper finds the GRB 081008 host is best described as a dusty spiral galaxy, with abundance ratios improved by increased massive-star dust production and age 0.45 Gyr.

quotes.quote: GRB 081008 For this host, as shown in Figure f:081008 , the observed abundance ratios are quite well fitted by the reference models for spiral galaxies, in particular looking at [ Si/Fe ] and [ Ni/Fe ] panels. Also the not so high [ Zn/Fe ] ratio ( 0.6 dex ) corroborate the idea of having a late-type galaxy (as the spiral is). Performing our test, the resulting best model was found to be the one of for spiral galaxies with increased dust production by massive stars (Sp + model). Adopting the dusty spiral model as the best one for this host galaxy, we estimate the age of the host at the time of the GRB event to be 0.45 Gyr .

gmw_00007025.name: SDSS J131014.04+323115.9
gmw_00007025.overview: SDSS J131014.04+323115.9 is a very faint dwarf galaxy with an estimated redshift of z = 0.0173 [arXiv:1707.00611]. It is associated with UGC 08250 and served as the host galaxy for the supernova SN 2016bdu [arXiv:1707.00611].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J131014.04+323115.9 as the faint host of SN 2016bdu, estimates its redshift, association with UGC 08250, low reddening, and absolute magnitude.

quotes.quote: The SN candidate exploded in the very faint ( g = 21.19 , = 20.94 mag) galaxy SDSS J131014.04+323115.9. The SN is clearly offset by 2.1'' from the center of its host in the PS1 images.

We will assume that the faint host of SN 2016bdu (SDSS J131014.04+323115.9) is associated with UGC 08250 (see Figure fig1 ). From the position of the peaks of the most prominent narrow emission lines in the transient's spectra, we estimate the redshift of SDSS J131014.04+323115.9 to be z = 0.0173 0.0002 ( d_L = 72.0 Mpc and = 34.29 mag), consistent with this hypothesis. We adopt the Virgo-infall-corrected distance modulus for this redshift of _ Vir = 34.37 0.15 mag. The nondetection of the interstellar Na I doublet (Na I D) absorption lines at the redshift of SDSS J131014.04+323115.9 suggests that there is negligible reddening due to the host galaxy; hence, we adopt only the Milky Way contribution E(B-V) = 0.013 mag sch11 as the total interstellar extinction toward SN 2016bdu.
Given these distance and reddening estimates, SDSS J131014.04+323115.9 has a total absolute magnitude M_g = -13.23 mag, and an intrinsic colour of g- = 0.23 mag.

gmw_00007026.name: ILT J112543.06+553112.4
gmw_00007026.overview: ILT J112543.06+553112.4 is a Fanaroff-Riley class II radio galaxy hosted by a spiral galaxy [arXiv:2110.04018]. It features a galaxy-scale jet with two disconnected radio lobes, no observable radio core, and spectral properties that suggest it may be a remnant source [arXiv:2110.04018].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ILT J112543.06+553112.4 as an FRII spiral-hosted galaxy-scale jet with disconnected radio lobes, no core, bright hotspots, and remnant-like properties.

quotes.quote: ILT J112543.06+553112.4 The VLA contours for this source appear as two disconnected islands of radio emission, one to the north west and the other to the south east. There is no observable radio core. Most easily seen using the B-configuration VLA data (Figure fig:ILTJ112543BConfig ), the northern island contains what appears to be a bright hot spot, whilst the southern island has a less pronounced hot spot confirming the FRII nature of this source. The hotspot in the north west lobe has a surface brightness approximately 25 per cent brighter than the hotspot in the south east. Whilst the centre of the LoTSS-identified host galaxy is significantly closer to the north east lobe, it does lie directly along the line joining the two hotspots.

Remnant GSJ ILT J112543.06+553112.4 and ILT J121847.41+520128.4, the two FRII spiral-hosted sources, have spectral indices towards the steeper end of the range of values found for the 3CRR sample Laing1983BrightGalaxies . Though not atypical of spectral indices found for larger AGN, recent studies have shown that these values are intermediate between sources typically classed as active and remnant AGN Mahatma2018RemnantField . Therefore, the possibility that these are remnant sources must be considered.

gmw_00007027.name: ILT J121847.41+520128.4
gmw_00007027.overview: ILT J121847.41+520128.4 is a radio galaxy that exhibits a Fanaroff-Riley Class II morphology [arXiv:2110.04018]. Its core emission is believed to be a mixture of ongoing active galactic nucleus activity and star formation [arXiv:2110.04018].

sources.summary: The paper treats ILT J121847.41+520128.4 as an FRII spiral-hosted galaxy-scale jet, noting its FRII morphology, spectral gradients, core behavior, and relatively steep injection index.

quotes.quote: ILT J121847.41+520128.4 The VLA radio contours for this source show a peak `core' emission lying on a line joining the two lobes. The `core' is offset from the host galaxy centre where an AGN would most likely be located by 1.4 suggesting that the host may have been misidentified. The `core' is also not the Gaussian shape typically associated with radio cores, being slightly elongated and extending to the south west of the host. We therefore investigate the possibility that the offset `core' is not AGN related and is due entirely to star formation activities.

For ILT J121847.41+520128.4, the 150 MHz to 3 GHz spectral index of the core is 0.72 0.09 consistent with ongoing AGN activity e.g. Laing1983BrightGalaxies,Sabater2019TheOn . This, combined with the morphological evidence suggesting the core of this source is a mix of AGN and star-formation related emission (Section sec:OpticalHosts-ILTJ121847 ) means we do not believe this is a remnant source.

gmw_00007028.name: ILT J120326.64+545201.5
gmw_00007028.overview: ILT J120326.64+545201.5 is a radio galaxy that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2110.04018]. High-resolution observations confirm the presence of a central radio core and separately identifiable lobes [arXiv:2110.04018].

sources.summary: The paper confirms ILT J120326.64+545201.5 has a host-centered AGN radio core, a low integrated spectral index, measurable lobe/core spectra, and anomalous flat-spectrum regions.

quotes.quote: The VLA data shows a radio core located within 0.15 of the centre of the host galaxy, confirming the host ID as well as an AGN core for three of our sources: ILT J120326.64+545201.5 ILT J120645.20+484451.1 ILT J124627.85+520222.1 One of the sources, ILT J122037.67+473857.6, was undetected by the VLA, meaning that the host ID could not be confirmed, though in this particular case we are happy to retain the source as the LoTSS image leaves little doubt the host has been correctly identified.

For the three GSJ within our sample that have separately identifiable lobes and cores in both the LoTSS and VLA images, ILT J120326.64+545201.5, ILT J121847.41+520128.4 and ILT J124627.85+520222.1, we calculated the spectra of the lobe and core sub-components, using the same technique as described in Section sec:Observations to calculate the flux densities for both LoTSS and VLA images.
The spectral index maps also show the different core spectral behaviour for ILT J120326.64+545201.5 and ILT J121847.41+520148.4 mentioned in Section sec:SpectralIndices-Component .

gmw_00007029.name: ILT J124627.85+520222.1
gmw_00007029.overview: ILT J124627.85+520222.1 is a radio-loud active galactic nucleus host galaxy [arXiv:2110.04018]. It features a galaxy-scale jet with a combined length under 80 kiloparsecs, and its eastern jet contains two regions with a flat spectral index [arXiv:2110.04018].

sources.summary: The paper classifies ILT J124627.85+520222.1 as a galaxy-scale jet, confirms its host-centered AGN core, and discusses its lobe/core spectra and anomalously flat eastern jet regions.

quotes.quote: Subsequent optimisation of the W21 criteria to exclude star-forming galaxies and larger objects meant that the other three sources identified using the pre-release LoTSS DR1 catalogue, ILT J122037.67+473857.6, ILT J124627.85+520222.1 and ILT J125453.46+542923.4 are not included in the W21 sample. However, all three sources have combined jet lengths less than our 80 kpc limit with the first two sources also appearing in the radio-loud AGN catalogue of Hardcastle2019Radio-loudSources . Both sources can therefore be considered as GSJ.

Although obeying this general trend, the eastern jet of ILT J124627.85+520222.1 does have two regions of flat spectral index at the tip and midway along the jet. This is discussed further in Section sec:SpectralModels-SpectralAges . The spectral index maps also show the different core spectral behaviour for ILT J120326.64+545201.5 and ILT J121847.41+520148.4 mentioned in Section sec:SpectralIndices-Component . Though less obvious, the core of ILT J124627.85+520222.1 also shows a different core spectral behaviour with a slightly flatter spectral index than the surrounding regions.

gmw_00007030.name: ILT J122037.67+473857.6
gmw_00007030.overview: ILT J122037.67+473857.6 is a steep-spectrum remnant radio galaxy [arXiv:2110.04018]. It is characterized by a small physical size comparable to its host galaxy, suggesting its radio jets were active for only a short period [arXiv:2110.04018].

sources.summary: The paper keeps ILT J122037.67+473857.6 as a galaxy-scale jet candidate from LoTSS, notes its VLA non-detection, and identifies it as a steep-spectrum remnant source.

quotes.quote: ILT J122037.67+473857.6 is undetected in the VLA observations and so, using three times the RMS as the detection threshold, an upper limit is given. This source has an integrated spectral index far steeper than the criterion of 1.3 traditionally used to identify remnant sources e.g.

Unlike the two spiral-hosted sources, the extremely steep spectral index of ILT J122037.67+473857.6 means this is definitely a remnant source Murgia2011DyingClusters,Mahatma2018RemnantField with the lack of detection in our 3 GHz VLA images lending tentative support to the idea that remnant sources fade quickly Kaiser2002TheRelics,Mahatma2018RemnantField . The small scale of the emission seen by LOFAR makes this the first known source with remnant emission of a similar size to the host. Whilst LOFAR is expected to be able to detect a large fraction of remnant sources, the outward diffusion of electrons means they are expected to be physically large e.g. Hardcastle2016LOFAR/H-ATLAS:Field , making the small size of this source unusual. Whilst we cannot directly measure the age of the source, the fact that this source never grew to large sizes does imply the radio jets were active for only a short period of time, consistent with expectations for low mass systems Heckman2014TheUniverse .

Like the two FRII spiral-hosted sources, ILT J122037.67+473857.6 does not have the morphologically relaxed appearance typical of remnant sources Brienza2016LOFARRedshift,Morganti2017ArchaeologySpectrum .

gmw_00007031.name: ILT J145604.90+472712.1
gmw_00007031.overview: ILT J145604.90+472712.1 is a galaxy-scale jet source with two distinct regions of radio emission extending east and west from its host galaxy [arXiv:2110.04018]. Although it lacks a detected radio core, this young and luminous source exhibits fast expansion speeds and may evolve into a larger radio galaxy [arXiv:2110.04018].

sources.summary: The paper describes ILT J145604.90+472712.1 as a galaxy-scale jet with two radio-emission regions aligned with the host, no detected core, and possible evolution into a larger radio source.

quotes.quote: ILT J145604.90+472712.1 Although the VLA image does not show a radio core for this object, it does still show two separate regions of emission emanating to the west and east of the host galaxy consistent with jetted activity. The core location from which these two regions of radio emission originate is consistent with the host identification and so we are confident that the host has been correctly identified and that this is a GSJ.
The youngest, most luminous source in our sample, ILT J145604.90+472712.1, has some of the fastest expansion speeds and may be evolving into a larger, FRI/FRII-type source.

gmw_00007032.name: host galaxy of XRF 040912
aliases_external: host galaxy of XRF 040912 / CXOU J235642.9-005520
gmw_00007032.overview: The host galaxy of XRF 040912 is a late-type galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.563 [arXiv:astro-ph/0610157]. It exhibits an extended optical morphology and experiences moderate-to-high star formation activity [arXiv:astro-ph/0610157].

sources.summary: The paper identifies an extended optical source as the likely XRF 040912 host, argues for z=1.563 from an [OII] line, and infers a young, star-forming late-type galaxy.

quotes.quote: The non-fading nature of the optical source positionally consistent with the X-ray afterglow candidate and its apparently extended morphology, strongly suggest that this source is the host galaxy of XRF 040912 (Fig. f:hostima ). We computed the chance probability to find a galaxy with R 24.0 mag within the 1'' radius error circle. Following Piro et al. (2002), we found P= 1.8 10^ 2 . This value makes the chance of association unlikely, though not negligible.

Another interpretation, consistent both with the lack of the Ly cut-off and with the absence of any other strong emission line blue-ward of the detected one, is that we are indeed observing the [ OII ] ( 3727 ) emission line, redshifted to z=1.563 0.001 .

The derived SFR values have not been corrected for a possible local extinction and therefore (2.3 0.8) M_ yr^ 1 should be considered as the lower limit of the real SFR. Our findings are consistent with the typical SFR range of values ( 0.7 12.7 M _ yr ^ 1 , not corrected for extinction) found for GRBs (Christensen et al. 2004). Together with the age estimation obtained by ^2 minimization from the template spectra fitting, a coherent description of a late type galaxy with moderate-high star formation activity is emerging, further strengthening the identification of this galaxy as the host of XRF 040912.

gmw_00007033.name: LAE 1
gmw_00007033.overview: LAE 1 is a high-redshift Lyman-alpha emission line galaxy candidate [arXiv:2001.04453]. It is located at a redshift of z = 4.947, placing it at a distance of 176 pkpc from a z = 4.9482 absorption system [arXiv:2001.04453].

sources.summary: The paper reports LAE 1 as the only candidate near the z=4.9482 absorption system, at 176 pkpc, with a faint line absent from sky spectra but uncertain skewness.

quotes.quote: LAE 1 This candidate was detected at 27.242 \ from the QSO and is the only LAE for the \ system at z_ =4.9482 . The emission line is detected at z_ =4.947 , which puts this object at 176 pkpc and V=70.8 from the \ system. The 1D spectrum of Figure f:ap1 shows a faint emission line that is absent in the comparison sky spectrum. The skewness of the line profile is negative, but it should be noted that a fraction of the object emission line could be coincidental with the strong sky emission line at =7240 and thus not included in the calculation of the line flux.

gmw_00007034.name: LAE 3
gmw_00007034.overview: LAE 3 is a high-redshift Lyman-alpha emission line galaxy located at a redshift of z=5.518 [arXiv:2001.04453]. It is a neighbor of LAE 2 and is detected in narrow-band imaging, though it shows no evidence of detection in the rest-frame ultraviolet [arXiv:2001.04453].

sources.summary: The paper describes LAE 3 as a neighbor of LAE 2 at z=5.518, closer to the absorber sightline, detected in the narrow-band image but not rest-frame UV.

quotes.quote: LAE 3 LAE 3 is also at z=5.518 , making it a neighbor of LAE 2. It lies at 6.807 \ (42 pkpc) from the QSO line of sight, thus it is closer to the \ system at z_ =5.5172 than LAE 2. In the close view in Figure f:laes_1 , the object is clearly seen in NB( ) but there is no evidence in the rest-frame UV. The 2D spectrum shows an emission line brighter than LAE 2 but similarly affected by the sky emission.

gmw_00007035.name: LAE 4
gmw_00007035.overview: LAE 4 is a faint high-redshift emission line galaxy candidate located 21.419 arcseconds from a QSO sight-line [arXiv:2001.04453]. It is situated near an absorber system at a redshift of 5.5172, though its large velocity offset and line asymmetry suggest it could be a lower-redshift interloper [arXiv:2001.04453].

sources.summary: The paper presents LAE 4 as a faint source near the z=5.5172 absorber with a confirmed emission line, while noting its large velocity offset and possible interloper nature.

quotes.quote: LAE 4 LAE 4 is a faint source at 21.419 \ from the QSO sight-line. If it is the redshift is z_ BLUE =5.530 and the impact parameter is 131 pkcp. Thus, it is near the \ system at z_ =5.5172 .

Figure f:laes_1 shows a faint detection in NB( ) and nothing in the other images. The emission line is confirmed in the 2D and 1D spectra (Figure f:ap4 ). The velocity difference to the \ system is lager than 500 , and the asymmetry of the line is negative which could be indicative of a lower redshift interloper.

gmw_00007036.name: LAE 5
gmw_00007036.overview: LAE 5 is a high-redshift Lyman-alpha emitting galaxy [arXiv:2001.04453]. It is located at a redshift of z = 5.721 and lies at a physical distance of 218 pkpc from a strong absorber system [arXiv:2001.04453].

sources.summary: The paper identifies LAE 5 as the brightest sample member, previously reported and confirmed, lying 218 pkpc from the strong z=5.72419 absorber with high-redshift-galaxy photometric behavior.

quotes.quote: LAE 5 This is the brightest LAE in the sample. It was previously reported by diaz2014 and confirmed in diaz2015 . In this work, we measure an angular distance to the QSO line of sight of 36.411 \ and a redshift of z_ =5.721 . As a result, it lies at 218 pkcp from to the strong \ system at z_ =5.72419 . LAE 5 is clearly seen in the z'-band image in Figure f:laes_1 and, like all other source in the sample, LAE 5 is detected in NB( ) but not detected in the i'-band, which is typical of high- z galaxies.

The 2D and 1D spectra are the highest signal-to-noise of the sample and the asymmetry of the line is obvious in both representations (Figure f:ap5 ). Line flux measurements, redshift, S _w and EW _0 estimates are in agreement with previously reported values.

gmw_00007037.name: LAE 6
gmw_00007037.overview: LAE 6 is a faint Lyman-alpha emitting galaxy candidate located at a redshift of approximately 5.720 [arXiv:2001.04453]. It is situated very close to a quasar line of sight and is considered a potential satellite of the neighboring galaxy LAE 5 [arXiv:2001.04453].

sources.summary: The paper treats LAE 6 as a faint candidate very close to the QSO sightline and potentially closest to the z=5.72419 absorber, but with low signal-to-noise limits.

quotes.quote: LAE 6 This LAE candidate is at 1.627 \ from the QSO line of sight. The emission line is consistent with \ at z_ BLUE =5.720 that would make it the closest LAE to the \ system at z_ =5.72419 , and a satellite of LAE 5. The object is faint in NB( ) and there is no trace of it in other images. The virtual slit in Figure f:laes_1 captures the LAE and part of the QSO light. As a result, the 2D spectrum shows the emission line of LAE 6 and the \ forest in the QSO spectrum.

The 1D spectrum in Figure f:ap6 shows a faint emission line in a region free of strong sky residuals. The signal is spread across several adjacent pixels in the sky (e.g. NB( )) and in wavelength direction (2D and 1D spectra). The low signal-to-noise of the emission line, which seems quite symmetric, results in large errors in S _w .

gmw_00007038.name: LAE 7
gmw_00007038.overview: LAE 7 is a high-redshift Lyman-alpha emission-line galaxy [arXiv:2001.04453]. Located east of the QSO sightline, the galaxy is detected in the narrow band and exhibits a clear, uncontaminated asymmetric emission-line profile [arXiv:2001.04453].

sources.summary: The paper describes LAE 7 as a solid high-redshift detection east of the QSO sightline, with an uncontaminated asymmetric emission-line profile confirmed in spectra.

quotes.quote: LAE 7 This galaxy is a solid detection, the asymmetry of the line profile is consistent with high- z \ at z_ BLUE =5.758 , which is at V 638.6 \ to the \ system at z_ =5.7428 . LAE 7 is at 27.887 \ (167 pkpc) to the East of the QSO sight-line and it is only detected in NB( ) like most of the sample. The emission line is not contaminated by sky residuals, and the 2D and 1D spectra clearly show an asymmetric profile confirmed by S _w (Table t:candidates ).

gmw_00007039.name: LAE 8
gmw_00007039.overview: LAE 8 is a high-redshift Lyman-alpha emitting galaxy [arXiv:2001.04453]. It shares a redshift of 5.758 with LAE 7 and is the closest candidate to the absorber system at redshift 5.7428 [arXiv:2001.04453].

sources.summary: The paper describes LAE 8 as sharing LAE 7’s redshift and being the closest candidate to the z=5.7428 absorber, with spectra typical of high-redshift LAEs.

quotes.quote: LAE 8 This LAE is at the same redshift of LAE 7 ( z_ BLUE =5.758 ) and the line profile supports the LAE identification. It is at 12.854 \ (77 pkpc) to the North of the QSO sight-line, thus it is the closest LAE to the \ system at z_ =5.7428 . The emission in the 2D spectrum is typical of high- z LAEs and the 1D spectrum confirms the asymmetry of the line with a skewness of S _w=8.0 1.5 .

gmw_00007040.name: LAE 9
gmw_00007040.overview: LAE 9 is a high-redshift Lyman-alpha emitting galaxy [arXiv:1104.4194, arXiv:2001.04453] located at a redshift of approximately 5.97 [arXiv:1104.4194, arXiv:2001.04453]. The galaxy has an estimated star formation rate of 7.8 solar masses per year [arXiv:1104.4194].

sources.summary: The paper confirms J103024.08+052420.41 as a Ly-alpha-emitting high-redshift galaxy, measuring its line redshift, flux, luminosity, and inferred star formation rate.

quotes.quote: Target 1: J103024.08+052420.41 This is the only galaxy with spectroscopic confirmation in the sample of st5 . We detect a clear ( 15 ) emission line (see Fig. fig:target1 ) which we identify as \ at z_ Ly =5.973 0.002 , in good agreement with the redshift z_ Ly =5.970 reported by st5 . The total line flux is F_ Ly =22 1.5 10^ 18 erg s ^ 1 , measured by integrating over 23 \ ( 800 ) of the spectrum; it is not possible to compare with the data by st5 because these authors did not report a measurement of the line flux. The \ line flux corresponds to a luminosity L_ Ly =8.6 0.6 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 in our cosmology, and a star formation rate SFR _ Ly = 7.8 0.5 M _ yr ^ 1 with the conversion (and caveats) given above (see Table mag ).

sources.summary: The paper re-identifies Target 1 as LAE 9, a previously confirmed Ly-alpha emitter outside the MUSE field but within 500 km/s of two z~5.98 absorption systems.

quotes.quote: Target 1 (J103024.08+052420.41) was confirmed by its \ emission in stiavelli2005 and then by diaz2011 at z_ em 5.973 . We recalculated the redshift using our definition based on the bluest pixel of the \ line profile and found z_ BLUE =5.968 (Figure f:lae9 ). This LAE is at 57.163 \ (335 pkpc) to the South-West of the QSO sight-line, thus it is outside MUSE's FoV but it lies within 500 \ of the two \ systems at z_ =5.9757 and 5.9784, for which no other galaxy candidate was identified in the datacube. Following the identification number assigned to the LAEs in the sample, in this work we will refer to this galaxy as LAE 9 (see Table t:lae9 for details).

gmw_00007041.name: PRG1
gmw_00007041.overview: PRG1 is a giant Lyman-alpha nebula located at a redshift of approximately 1.67 [arXiv:1411.2589]. Its extended emission is likely produced in situ by photoionization from an active galactic nucleus or distributed star formation within a rotating, clumpy turbulent disk [arXiv:1411.2589].

sources.summary: The paper studies PRG1 as a z~1.67 giant Ly-alpha nebula whose emission-line structure and kinematics are consistent with a rotating, clumpy turbulent disk forming a massive galaxy or group.

quotes.quote: In this context, our discovery of a giant \ nebula (henceforth referred to as PRG1) with bright, spatially-extended 1640 emission and somewhat weaker metal lines pres09,pres12a,pres13 provides a rare opportunity.

Using the spatially extended emission in \ as well as in less optically thick emission lines, we study the role of scattering and the kinematics of the extended gas within PRG1, a \ nebula at z 1.67 . The low measured kinematic offset of \ and the similarity of the surface brightness profiles observed in different emission lines are strong arguments that the extended \ is being produced in situ within the spatially extended gas, most likely due to photoionization from an AGN or distributed star formation, rather than scattered from a central source.

Although the halo masses of \ nebulae are poorly constrained, it is possible that we are seeing a similar phenomenon in \ nebulae systems like PRG1 and SSA22-LAB2 martinchris14 , i.e., the early formation of a large Milky Way mass galaxy or galaxy group. In the case of PRG1, the rotation period implied by our disk modeling ( T_ rot 4.9 10^ 8 yr, assuming V_ max 250 km s ^ 1 at R=20 kpc) is consistent with the system undergoing a handful of rotations by z 1.67 (when the age of the Universe was 3.9 Gyr).

gmw_00007042.name: UDG11
gmw_00007042.overview: UDG11 is an ultra-diffuse dwarf galaxy with an old, metal-poor stellar population [arXiv:2308.11493]. It hosts an estimated population of approximately six associated globular clusters and exhibits a high dynamical mass-to-light ratio [arXiv:2308.11493].

sources.summary: The paper presents UDG11 as the LEWIS test galaxy, deriving old metal-poor stellar populations, low velocity dispersion, associated globular clusters, and a large dynamical mass-to-light ratio.

quotes.quote: To assess the quality of the data and demonstrate the feasibility of the science goals, we report the preliminary results obtained for one of the sample galaxies, UDG11. For this target, we i) derived the stellar kinematics, including the 2-dimensional maps of line-of-sight velocity and velocity dispersion, ii) constrained age and metallicity, and iii) studied the globular cluster (GC) population hosted by the UDG. Results are compared with the available measurements for UDGs and dwarf galaxies in literature. By fitting the stacked spectrum inside one effective radius, we find that UDG11 has a velocity dispersion = 20 8 km/s, it is old ( 10 1 Gyr), metal-poor ( [ M /H ] =-1.17 0.11 dex) and has a total dynamical mass-to-light ratio M /L_V 14 , comparable to those observed for classical dwarf galaxies.

The spatially resolved stellar kinematics maps suggest that UDG11 does not show a significant velocity gradient along either major or minor photometric axes, and the average value of the velocity dispersion is _ e = 27 8 km/s. We find two GCs kinematically associated with UDG11. The estimated total number of GCs in UDG11, corrected for the spectroscopic completeness limit, is N_ GC = 5.9^ +2.2 _ 1.8 , which corresponds to a GC specific frequency of S_N = 8.4^ +3.2 _ 2.7 .

gmw_00007043.name: UDG22
gmw_00007043.overview: UDG22 is a low surface brightness dwarf galaxy with a measured systemic velocity of 5198 km/s [arXiv:2308.11493]. Based on Hubble-flow calculations, it is located at a distance of approximately 74 Mpc, placing it in the background of the Hydra I cluster [arXiv:2308.11493].

sources.summary: The paper flags UDG22 as a velocity outlier, estimates a 74 Mpc Hubble-flow distance, and says it would be more diffuse and fainter if behind Hydra I.

quotes.quote: The three remaining galaxies, UDG22 with V_ sys = 5198 5 km/s, LSB6 with V_ sys = 5193 5 km/s, and LSB8 with V_ sys = 2718 5 km/s, could be background or foreground galaxies. In the photometric work of Iodice2020b and LaMarca2022b , to discriminate UDGs from normal dwarfs LSB galaxies via their physical sizes, we assumed that all newly detected galaxies are at the distance of the Hydra I cluster (i.e. 51 Mpc). Now, having confirmed the cluster membership of most UDGs and LSB galaxies with our LEWIS spectroscopy, we stick to the fixed distance for deriving their effective radii and confirming their morphological classification, as already published in our previous papers (see also Table tab:UDGsample ). We are aware that the Hydra I cluster might have a physical depth of a few Mpc, and thus some of the UDGs might be located in front or back of the cluster, resulting in size differences of 4 for a relative distance of 2 Mpc with respect to Hydra I. For the three outliers in the redshift distribution, we have used the Hubble law, assuming H _0 =70 km/s/Mpc, to derive the Hubble flow distance based on the measured V_ sys . For UDG22 and LSB6, we obtained a distance of 74 Mpc and the new values for their effective radius are R_ e =5.22 kpc and R_ e =5.80 kpc, respectively. For LSB8, which has a lower V_ sys , R_ e =1.12 kpc. Therefore, UDG22 and LSB6 turn out to be more diffuse and fainter, if they would be a UDG and a LSB in the background of Hydra I cluster.

gmw_00007044.name: LSB6
gmw_00007044.overview: LSB6 is a low surface brightness galaxy with a measured systemic velocity of 5193 km/s [arXiv:2308.11493]. It is a possible background or foreground object relative to the Hydra I cluster, with an estimated Hubble flow distance of 74 Mpc [arXiv:2308.11493].

sources.summary: The paper identifies LSB6 as a possible background/foreground object, gives a 74 Mpc distance and revised effective radius, and says it would be fainter and more diffuse.

quotes.quote: The three remaining galaxies, UDG22 with V_ sys = 5198 5 km/s, LSB6 with V_ sys = 5193 5 km/s, and LSB8 with V_ sys = 2718 5 km/s, could be background or foreground galaxies. In the photometric work of Iodice2020b and LaMarca2022b , to discriminate UDGs from normal dwarfs LSB galaxies via their physical sizes, we assumed that all newly detected galaxies are at the distance of the Hydra I cluster (i.e. 51 Mpc). Now, having confirmed the cluster membership of most UDGs and LSB galaxies with our LEWIS spectroscopy, we stick to the fixed distance for deriving their effective radii and confirming their morphological classification, as already published in our previous papers (see also Table tab:UDGsample ). We are aware that the Hydra I cluster might have a physical depth of a few Mpc, and thus some of the UDGs might be located in front or back of the cluster, resulting in size differences of 4 for a relative distance of 2 Mpc with respect to Hydra I. For the three outliers in the redshift distribution, we have used the Hubble law, assuming H _0 =70 km/s/Mpc, to derive the Hubble flow distance based on the measured V_ sys . For UDG22 and LSB6, we obtained a distance of 74 Mpc and the new values for their effective radius are R_ e =5.22 kpc and R_ e =5.80 kpc, respectively. For LSB8, which has a lower V_ sys , R_ e =1.12 kpc. Therefore, UDG22 and LSB6 turn out to be more diffuse and fainter, if they would be a UDG and a LSB in the background of Hydra I cluster.

gmw_00007045.name: LSB8
gmw_00007045.overview: LSB8 is a low surface brightness galaxy with a system velocity of 2718 km/s [arXiv:2308.11493]. Based on its central surface brightness and an effective radius of 1.12 kpc, it is classified as a foreground dwarf galaxy [arXiv:2308.11493].

sources.summary: The paper treats LSB8 as a velocity outlier, derives a smaller effective radius, and classifies it as a foreground dwarf galaxy from its surface brightness.

quotes.quote: The three remaining galaxies, UDG22 with V_ sys = 5198 5 km/s, LSB6 with V_ sys = 5193 5 km/s, and LSB8 with V_ sys = 2718 5 km/s, could be background or foreground galaxies.

For LSB8, which has a lower V_ sys , R_ e =1.12 kpc. Therefore, UDG22 and LSB6 turn out to be more diffuse and fainter, if they would be a UDG and a LSB in the background of Hydra I cluster. LSB8 is smaller and, according to its central surface brightness _ 0,g =23.20 mag/arcsec ^2 , it can be classified as a foreground dwarf galaxy.

gmw_00007046.name: CGCG 097-092
gmw_00007046.overview: CGCG 097-092 is a starburst galaxy that features a 30-kiloparsec cone-like extension of ionized gas [arXiv:1703.10301]. This extended gas may have originated from either ram-pressure stripping or a superwind driven by the central starburst [arXiv:1703.10301].

sources.summary: The paper reports that CGCG 097-092 has a 30 kpc cone-like ionized-gas extension, central starburst activity, and an uncertain origin involving ram-pressure stripping or a starburst superwind.

quotes.quote: CGCG 097-092 (Figure fig:097092 ) shows a EIG of a cone-like appearance extended to 30 kpc. The parent galaxy does not show a sign of AGN Gavazzi2011 , while the spectrum of the center of the parent galaxy shows a starburst feature. The UV light from the starburst would be the source of the ionization. In Table tab:clouds , we also showed the property of the tail excluding the core region. The origin of the EIG could be a ram-pressure stripping or a superwind by the central starburst Veilleux2005 .
The two will be distinguished if the recession velocity profile will be available in future.

gmw_00007047.name: CGCG 097-093
gmw_00007047.overview: CGCG 097-093 is a jellyfish-like galaxy that exhibits an enhancement of faint blue stars and H-alpha emission to its northeast [arXiv:1703.10301]. The galaxy displays asymmetric spiral arms and a gas morphology that suggest a combination of ram pressure and a recent tidal interaction [arXiv:1703.10301].

sources.summary: The paper presents CGCG 097-093 as a jellyfish-like galaxy with blue stars, H-alpha emission, asymmetric spiral arms, and gas morphology suggesting ram pressure plus possible tidal interaction.

quotes.quote: CGCG 097-093 (Figure fig:097093 ) shows a morphology like jellyfish galaxies e.g., Cortese2007,Smith2010,Ebeling2014,Poggianti2016 . Enhancement of faint blue stars and \ emission are seen to the northeast. Inside of the disk, an asymmetry of spiral arms is recognized, which may be a sign of a recent tidal interaction. The recession velocity of CGCG 097-093 is 7298 km s ^ 1 . Its south-west neighbor, CGCG 097-088 is 5616 km s ^ 1 , and north neighbor CGCG 097-094 is 7998 km s ^ 1 .
Meanwhile the morphology in \ suggests ram-pressure origin, as the galaxy-wide gas flow would be difficult to be made by a tidal interaction only.

gmw_00007048.name: CGCG 097-122
gmw_00007048.overview: CGCG 097-122 is an edge-on spiral starburst galaxy with an active star-forming disk and no signs of active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:1703.10301]. It features a distorted faint halo that may be partially formed by interactions with a nearby dwarf companion [arXiv:1703.10301].

sources.summary: The paper describes CGCG 097-122 as an edge-on spiral/starburst galaxy with a distorted halo, active star formation, peculiar H-alpha spurs, and mixed ram-pressure, interaction, and superwind signatures.

quotes.quote: CGCG 097-122 (NGC 3859) looks like an edge-on spiral galaxy with a distorted faint halo which was revealed by our deep imaging (Figures fig:097122 ). The halo is elongated toward the northeast almost along the major axis of the galaxy. At the southwest side of the galaxy, the halo is extended to the projected distance of 10 kpc above the galaxy disk. At the north of the galaxy, a small galaxy which is possibly an interacting dwarf companion is seen. A part of the extended halo may be formed by the interaction between this companion and CGCG 097-122.

The bright disk \ emission together with the very blue color of the galaxy and the emission line dominated nuclear spectrum indicate that the star formation of this galaxy is very active. Its nuclear spectrum is typical of starburst galaxies Gavazzi2013 and has no sign of AGN activity Gavazzi2011 . The total + flux of CGCG 097-122 is 10^ 12.65 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 Gavazzi2006 , and / = 0.36 Gavazzi2013 .

gmw_00007049.name: TXS 0506+056
gmw_00007049.overview: TXS 0506+056 is a radio-bright BL Lacertae object and a very high energy gamma-ray blazar [arXiv:1807.04461]. It is identified as the most plausible counterpart for IceCube astrophysical neutrino emissions in its region [arXiv:1807.04461].

sources.summary: The paper argues that TXS 0506+056 is the favored counterpart of the IceCube neutrino events, dominating high-energy gamma rays and showing states consistent with hadronic neutrino production.

quotes.quote: We find that PKS 0502+049 contaminates the ray emission region at low energies but TXS 0506+056 dominates the sky above a few GeV. TXS 0506+056, which is a very strong (top percent) radio and ray source, is in a high ray state during the neutrino alert but in a low though hard ray state in coincidence with the neutrino flare. Both states can be reconciled with the energy associated with the neutrino emission and, in particular during the low/hard state, there is evidence that TXS 0506+056 has undergone a hadronic flare with very important implications for blazar modelling. All multi-messenger diagnostics reported here support a single coherent picture in which TXS 0506+056, a very high energy ray blazar, is the only counterpart of all the neutrino emissions in the region and therefore the most plausible first non-stellar neutrino and, hence, cosmic ray source.

The most probable hint of an association ( 3 - 3.5 ) reported so far icfermi between an IceCube astrophysical neutrino and an extragalactic object is that of the neutrino IceCube-170922A and the radio bright ( 1 Jy at 5 GHz) and ray flaring BL Lac object TXS 0506+056 (also known as 5BZB J0509+0541, 2FHL J0509.5+0541, and 3FGL J0509.4+0541).

gmw_00007050.name: J095847.88+690532.7
gmw_00007050.overview: J095847.88+690532.7 is an active galactic nucleus at a redshift of approximately 1.3 that hosts a supermassive black hole of about one billion solar masses [arXiv:2303.08841].
