It exhibits variations in both soft and hard X-ray flux by a factor of more than two over approximately one week in its rest frame, as well as large variations in its power-law continuum photon index [arXiv:2303.08841].

sources.summary: The paper highlights J095847.88+690532.7 as a densely monitored high-Eddington AGN with a massive black hole, strong short-term X-ray flux changes, and variable photon indices.

quotes.quote: Indeed in J095847.88+690532.7 ( z 1.3 ), one of the most densely monitored objects hosting a 10^9 M_ supermassive black hole, we discovered a variation of the soft X-ray flux by a factor of > 2 over approximately one week (rest-frame). Large variations in the power-law continuum photon index are also observed, questioning expectations from previously reported _ Edd relations, for which 2 would be a ubiquitous hallmark of AGN with _ Edd 1 .

As an example, SDSS J095847.88+690532.7, one of the most densely observed X-HESS AGN hosting a 10^9 M_ black hole at redshift z 1.3 shows large variations in terms of and both soft ( E=0.5 2 keV) and hard ( E=2 10 keV) X-ray flux over more than a decade, as displayed in Fig. \ fig:2_LC . We find flux variations of a factor of > 2 in approximately one week in the rest frame of the source, which are relatively high for an AGN with such a massive SMBH. The values of in Fig. \ fig:2_LC range between 1.5 and 2.3 , indicating a large dispersion around the expected value from the adopted _ Edd relation, in this case the one from liu2021 , can be seen also in individual sources when repeatedly observed.

gmw_00007051.name: HS0810+2554
gmw_00007051.overview: HS0810+2554 is a radio-quiet, quadruply lensed quasar located at a redshift of approximately 1.51 [arXiv:2009.01277, arXiv:2305.05173]. The background galaxy is lensed by a foreground elliptical galaxy [arXiv:2405.04779] and features a faint radio jet [arXiv:2009.01277, arXiv:2305.05173], relativistic outflows [arXiv:2305.05173], and molecular gas that is more extended than its thermal dust [arXiv:2009.01277].

sources.summary: The paper models HS 0810+2554 with ALMA and CO because its continuum is weak, finding compact dust and molecular gas more extended than the dust.

quotes.quote: HS 0810+2554 HS 0810+2554 is a quasar at z_ s =1.51 that is lensed into a characteristic fold-configuration Reimers:2002,Hewett:2010 . The redshift of the foreground galaxy is unknown, but is estimated to be 0.9 based on the lens population distribution Mosquera:2011 . This quasar is faint at radio wavelengths, but VLBI investigations find evidence of compact, low-luminosity radio-jet structure that dominates the radio emission Hartley:2019 . HS 0810+2554 was observed with ALMA at 145 GHz, which also targeted the CO (3--2) emission line. We resolve thermal dust emission from this system with ALMA at 145 GHz that has a peak surface brightness of 6 (where is the rms noise per beam).
For HS 0810+2554, we perform the lens modelling with the integrated CO (3--2) spectral line, which provides more extended emission and a higher signal-to-noise ratio than the continuum.
For HS 0810+2554 and RX J0911+0551, in which we observe CO (5--4) and CO (3--2), which are usually associated with star-forming regions, we find the gas to be more extended than the dust.

sources.summary: The paper models HS 0810+2554 as a strong-lensing system with a foreground elliptical and quadruply imaged background galaxy to study fuzzy-dark-matter time-delay anomalies.

quotes.quote: Following hartley2019strong,amruth2023einstein , we focus our attention on the specific system HS 0810+2554. It consists of a foreground elliptical galaxy with a quadruply imaged, lensed background galaxy. The redshift of the foreground galaxy is z_ L =0.89 , while the redshift of the source is z_ S =1.51 hartley2019strong . The background galaxy is lensed by the foreground galaxy in such a way that each of its structural components produces four observable images. These components include a quasar (QSO) at optical/near-IR wavelengths observed by the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) kochanek1999results and a pair of jets at radio wavelengths observed by the European VLBI Network (EVN) hartley2019strong .

In this paper, we focused on the perturbation of time delays in strong lensing systems by DM structures. In order be specific and have an opportunity to relate our assessment to a real case, we studied a particularly well studied strong lensing system HS 0810+2554.

sources.summary: HS 0810+2554 contains three associated systems; one saturated doublet fully occults the quasar engine, while the other systems lack evidence for partial coverage.

quotes.quote: HS 0810+2554 ( =1.4862, 1.4948, 1.5025): There are three associated systems toward this quasar. The system at =1.5025 \ (Figure fig:hs0810a ) is detected in Ly , Ly , 977.020, and the 1031.926, 1037.617 doublet. The doublet is saturated and reaches a zero flux level, indicating that the high-ionization gas fully occults the quasar central engine.

In Figure fig:hs0810b , we show the detected 1031.926, 1037.617 doublet in the =1.4948 \ system, velocity-aligned flux profiles on the left, and evaluation of the velocity-dependent partial coverage on the right. The system at =1.4948 \ is detected only in the 1031.926, 1037.617 doublet. No other lines, most notably notably Ly , are detected.

sources.summary: The paper measures HS0810+2554 emission-line widths from LUCIFER spectra, modeling blended continuum and line components and finding the H-alpha FWHM consistent with Greene et al.

quotes.quote: HS0810+2554 --- The \ profile of HS0810 shows a small amount of absorption near the peak of the line. We interpolate over this region before making the line-width measurements and fitting the GH polynomial. Our results are consistent with or without the interpolation, as the absorption is weak and only seen near the very peak of the line. To fit the continuum and emission-line features that blended with the \ emission of HS0810+2554, a power-law continuum and \ broad-emission line template were fit to the spectrum based on the continuum regions listed in Table tab:nir_lineboundaries and the rest-frame optical \ template of Boroson92 from observations of I Zw1 see for more details Wills85, Dietrich02, Dietrich05 . Narrow \ emission was then removed by creating a template from a two-component Gaussian fit to the [ ] 5007 narrow line and then scaling it to [ ] 4959 based on standard emission line ratios.

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS 081331.28+254503.0 as a reported lensed quasar reclassified as a quasar-star pair, where HST shows the lensed system plus a superposed star.

quotes.quote: We find that two reported LQs, SDSS 081331.28+254503.0 Reimer2002,Jackson2015 and SDSS J234330.58+043557.9 Krone-Martins2019 , are classified as quasar-star pairs according to our proper motion/parallax criteria. Both of these two LQs have two Gaia detections. We think that Gaia only detected one of the lensed images and the other Gaia detection came from a superpositioned star with large proper motion or parallax. The HST archival images of the known LQ SDSS 081331.28+254503.0 clearly showed the lensed quasar system and Gaia only detected the brightest lensed image and a superpositioned star.

sources.summary: SDSS J081331.28+254503.0 is a radio-quiet quadruply lensed quasar observed by ALMA; archive imaging shows multiple images, and X-ray/VLBI work finds outflows plus a faint jet.

quotes.quote: SDSS J081331.28+254503.0 (HS 0810+2554), is a radio-quiet quadruply lensed quasar at a redshift of 1.508, observed with ALMA in bands 3 and 4 continuum and CO (3-2) chartas20, stacey21 . For this object, Fig. fig:lens we chose a band 6 image found in the archive, where the multiple images are clearly visible. Observations of this quasar in Chandra and XMM-Newton have revealed highly ionized and relativistic outflows chartas16 , while VLBI observations revealed a faint jet to dominate the radio emission hartley19 .

gmw_00007052.name: GRB 070521
gmw_00007052.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 070521 is a high-redshift system located at z = 2.0865 [arXiv:1908.01541], though it was previously modeled as a late-type galaxy at z = 1.35 [arXiv:0905.0001]. It exhibits a moderate star formation rate of approximately 50 solar masses per year [arXiv:1908.01541] and displays a possible extended radio emission offset from its main disk [arXiv:1305.2941].

sources.summary: The only GRB 070521 host candidate is a red pointlike object fit as a late-type galaxy at z about 1.35, with little host extinction despite the afterglow limits.

quotes.quote: In our observations, the most recent UVOT-enhanced XRT error circle includes a red, pointlike object near its eastern edge (Figure fig:070521 ). It is strongly detected in the NIR filters (except J , which was a relatively short exposure). However, in I band it is only marginally detected, slightly blended with another source located outside the XRT error circle, and was only detected in our V band imaging after a second visit to the field: uniquely among the host-galaxy candidates in this sample, this object is quite red. No other objects are present within the error circle at either optical or infrared wavelengths.

Using the package hyperz Bolzonella+2000 , the SED is well fit by a late-type galaxy template at a redshift of z = 1.35^ 0.16 _ +0.32 with a stellar age of 360 Myr and an extinction of only A_V = 0.4 mag. The apparent redness is, therefore, more likely to be due to the presence of the 4000 \ break rather than dust: indeed, the JHK SED redward of this break is quite normal. Therefore, as with the other host galaxies in our sample, little dust extinction is demanded by the host data. The amount of extinction required by the afterglow of this burst is as phenomenal as for 061222A. Assuming an intrinsic afterglow _ OX > 0.5 at 10^4 sec, the deep Gemini limit requires an extinction of at least 4.7 mag in the observed K band. At the putative host redshift of z=1.35 this corresponds to a limit of A_V > 9 mag (over the 95 confidence redshift range of z=0.95-2.05 , the constraint is A_V > 6 mag). A similarly large amount of extinction in the host SED is ruled out by our template modeling.

sources.summary: The paper detects FIR continuum from the GRB 070521 host, derives a moderate SFR consistent with optical estimates, and argues the radio excess likely reflects afterglow or AGN contamination.

quotes.quote: The host galaxy was identified within the XRT positional error circle Perley2013a . The redshift of the host galaxy, z=2.0865 , is determined by H emission Kruhler2015 . The H luminosity implies SFR _ H = 26 ^ +34 _ 17 M_ yr ^ 1 Kruhler2015 . Observed host SED covering from rest-frame UV to near-infrared (NIR) wavelengths indicates SFR _ opt SED = 40.4 ^ +62.1 _ 3.0 M_ yr ^ 1 with a stellar mass of M_ * =(3.08^ +1.89 _ 0.41 ) 10^ 10 M_ Perley2013a .

The derived SFR of the GRB 070521 host is 49.9 ^ +72.3 _ 2.9 M_ yr ^ 1 . The extinction-corrected optical SFRs in previous studies are SFR _ H =26^ +34 _ 17 M_ yr ^ 1 and SFR _ opt SED =40.4^ +62.1 _ 3.0 M_ yr ^ 1 Perley2013a,Kruhler2015 . Our SFR estimate is consistent with previous optical measurements within errors, while this work added the rest-frame FIR photometric data. The host galaxy does not show a strong dust-obscured star formation.

sources.summary: The paper reports a marginal convolved radio detection for the GRB 070521 host, probably extended and offset toward an HST extension, with an implied very high SFR if confirmed.

quotes.quote: GRB 070521 No highly significant point source appears at the location of GRB 070521 in the synthesized map at the array's native resolution (point-source limit F<20 Jy at any location consistent with the optical disk), but in the convolved image a marginal detection (98 confidence) appears with an apparent flux density of F = 28 10 Jy. GRB 070521 was not previously observed at long wavelengths and so its late-time afterglow flux is unconstrained, although the contribution of any point source to the detection is likely minor: the flux density value at the centroid of the radio detection is only 9 4 Jy in the unconvolved map. If the source is real at all, then, it is probably also extended and therefore associated with the host galaxy. We tentatively suggest that this source also likely represents a detection, but further observations would be necessary to unambiguously establish its reality. We note that the location of the source is offset slightly from the brightest part of the host in the HST image, at the location of an apparent extension stretching southeast of the main disk.
Similarly, the possible detection of GRB 070521 would imply an SFR ( 850 M_ yr ^ 1 ) vastly in excess of the optical value if confirmed by further observations.

gmw_00007053.name: GRB 090404
gmw_00007053.overview: GRB 090404 is associated with a point-like radio source detected by the Very Large Array [arXiv:1305.2941]. This emission is also detected in the millimeter band, though it remains ambiguous whether the signal originates from the host galaxy or an afterglow [arXiv:1305.2941].

sources.summary: The paper treats GRB 090404 as an ambiguous host/afterglow radio detection, with millimeter evidence, pointlike VLA emission, and an implied extreme host SFR if host-dominated.

quotes.quote: GRB 090404 GRB 090404 was also not observed in the radio at early times. It was, however, observed and detected in the millimeter band with the Plateau de Bure Interferometer deUgartePostigo+2012 . The reported 87 GHz flux density from this observation is 660 Jy at 3.4 days, which (assuming _a < < _m ) implies a radio flux density of 270 Jy at this time. Assuming evolution similar to our generic radio light curve described earlier (flat evolution for the first 30 days, then decay as t^ 1 ) we would expect a flux density of 10 Jy at the initial observation epoch of 792 days. While the actual afterglow contribution could easily be much less than this (in particular if < _a ), again we cannot unambiguously rule out an afterglow origin to the observed detection.

Higher-resolution versions of the map produced no significant change in source flux, indicating a point-like origin (although the weak detection renders this statement not strongly constraining). While the VLA detection is slightly north of the reported millimeter position, the offset is not significant given the uncertainties in both positions. Similarly, attempts to subdivide the data by frequency did not produce a useful constraint on the spectral index. As with GRB 080207, whether this source is associated with the host or an afterglow is ambiguous.

gmw_00007054.name: NGC 7135
gmw_00007054.overview: NGC 7135 is a merger remnant galaxy [arXiv:2101.07072] that hosts a LINER nucleus [arXiv:0707.2896]. It exhibits disrupted kinematics with counter-rotating gas [arXiv:2101.07072], as well as fine structures including shells, a plume, and a tidal tail [arXiv:0707.2896, arXiv:2101.07072].

sources.summary: NGC 7135 is described as a merger/LINER shell galaxy whose GALEX images reveal shells, plume, tidal tail, nuclear dust, blue center, and recent rejuvenation.

quotes.quote: NGC 7135 . Malin83 noticed that the galaxy presents``a curious jet and a shell" and a prominent tail on the opposite side of a sharp curved feature. Keel85 classified the galaxy as a merger galaxy with a LINER nucleus. Both the stellar tail and the elongated asymmetric distribution of the ionized gas, detected by Rampazzo03 suggest indeed a recent acquisition event. In addition the inner stellar radial velocity, measured by Longhetti98 , has a similar profile but shifted by about 200 km s ^ 1 with respect to the barycentric velocity of the ionized gas.

Contrary to the previous cases, the fine structures visible in the optical DSS image are also revealed both in the NUV and FUV images (see Figure fig4 top and mid panels). Specifically, the SW shell as well as the faint plume extending from the galaxy center towards the South are clearly visible. In the NUV image, the filamentary tidal tail in the NW direction of the galaxy nucleus visible in the optical image is also seen. This feature makes NGC 7135 a possible analog of the ``Medusa'' galaxy, NGC 4194, see Rampazzo03 .

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 7135 as a merger remnant with tails and shells, using MUSE data to identify ongoing counter-rotating gas, disrupted kinematics, and an older 6-10 Gyr merger.

quotes.quote: We take new MUSE data from the Snapshot Optical Spectroscopic Imaging of Mergers and Pairs for Legacy Exploration (SOSIMPLE) survey to examine NGC 7135. With counter-rotation of gas, disrupted kinematics and asymmetric chemical distribution, NGC 7135 is consistent with an ongoing merger. Though well hidden by the current merger, we are able to distinguish stars originating from an older merger, occurring 6-10 Gyr ago. We further find a gradient in ex-situ material with galactocentric radius, with the accreted fraction rising from 0 in the galaxy centre, to 7 within 0.6 effective radii.

The S01-pec galaxy NGC 7135 (AM 2146–350, IC 5136) in the constellation of Piscis Austrinus is a merger remnant galaxy Keel1985 that is likely en route to forming an S0 galaxy. It currently displays several immediately striking visual features including an extended tail, shell features, and curved structure (Figure phot ) based on photometry from the Carnegie-Irvine Galaxy Survey ho2011carnegie . NGC 7135 was first described as having `a curious jet and shell' in with the `jet' later shown to be a tail in .

gmw_00007055.name: S1192
gmw_00007055.overview: S1192 is classified as a triple radio galaxy consisting of three components [arXiv:0712.1409]. Its brightest component is centered on an infrared source, and its overall morphology is consistent with a bent wide-angle tail radio galaxy [arXiv:0712.1409].

sources.summary: S1192 is classified as a triple radio galaxy made of three components, centered on an infrared source, with morphology also consistent with a bent WAT.

quotes.quote: Source S1192 This source is an example of a triple radio galaxy. It consists of the three components C1192, C1192.1, and C1192.2 with mJy flux densities. The brightest component, C1192.1, is centred on the IR source SWIRE4 J003320.68-430203.6. The other two components are several arcsec away from the nearest IR sources, and the overall morphology thus indicates that this is a bent triple radio galaxy. It therefore also could be a WAT.

gmw_00007056.name: S773
gmw_00007056.overview: S773 is classified as a type 1 active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of 0.143 [arXiv:0712.1409]. It is a faint radio source with a bright infrared counterpart, a high 24 micron to radio ratio, and a bright X-ray detection [arXiv:0712.1409].

sources.summary: S773 is a faint radio source with a bright infrared counterpart, unusual 24 micron to radio ratio, type 1 AGN classification, redshift, and X-ray detection.

quotes.quote: Source S773 Source S773 is a rather faint radio source with S_ =0.37 mJy , but it has a very bright infrared counterpart with S_ 24 =28 mJy within 0.72'' , and is one of the few objects clearly visible in the SWIRE 70 \ image. Its unusual ratio of log _ 10 (S_ 24 /S_ )=1.89 lets it clearly stand out in Figure fig:radio-ir as a separated dot in the bottom right corner. It has been classified by as a type 1 AGN (based on optical line widths in excess of 1200 km/s) at redshift 0.143. Furthermore, it is one of the brightest X-ray sources found by in a BeppoSAX survey of the ELAIS-S1 region.

gmw_00007057.name: J011208+414702
gmw_00007057.overview: J011208+414702 is a radio galaxy that serves as the brightest central member of a roughly linear chain of galaxies [arXiv:2403.11290]. Its structure features a radio spiral embedded within a larger region of diffuse radio emission [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J011208+414702 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes radio spiral in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J011208+414702 (B3 0109+415): The source is identified with a galaxy which is the brightest (central) member of a roughly linear chain of galaxies (Fig. fig:J011208 ). From the VLASS map, the structure consists of a radio spiral of diameter 35'' embedded within diffuse radio emission of maximum extent 90'' , as measured on the LoTSS DR2 map (Fig. fig:J011208 a). The inner spiral could not be discerned in an earlier VLA map at 1.5 GHz Vigotti1989AJ.....98..419V , because of its 3 times larger beam.

gmw_00007058.name: J022830+382108
gmw_00007058.overview: J022830+382108 is a Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy identified with a bright host galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.0328 [arXiv:2403.11290]. Its eastern radio jet bends sharply northward by 90 degrees, while a collimated northward radio spur extends 66 kiloparsecs from its western jet [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J022830+382108 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes FR II, FR I, radio spur, jets in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J022830+382108 (B3 0225+381): The source is identified with a bright galaxy ( z = 0.0328) whose radio core is clearly detected in the VLASS map and also visible in the LoTSS DR2 map. (Fig. fig:J022830 ). This is a FR 1 type radio source, with the brighter eastern jet bending sharply northward by 90^ o . Both jets brighten up at 18'' (12 kpc) from the host galaxy. From the brightened region in the western jet emanates a spectacular `chimney-like' well-collimated northward radio spur of extent 100'' (66 kpc).

gmw_00007059.name: J083106+414741
gmw_00007059.overview: J083106+414741 is a large Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy hosted by a brightest cluster galaxy [arXiv:2403.11290]. It features a radio core, an associated jet-pair, and a bright radio ridge offset to the south [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J083106+414741 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes WAT, radio ridge, jets, BCG host in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J083106+414741: This large FR 1 radio galaxy is identified with a BCG (brightest cluster galaxy) at z (spec) = 0.13195 Ahn2012ApJS..203...21A . Its radio core is clearly detected in the VLASS map and the LOTSS DR2 map and it has a jet-pair associated with it (e.g., see the grey-scale image in Fig. fig:J083106 b). Also seen is a bright radio ridge of total length 110'' (260 kpc), which is offset southward of the jet pair and runs almost parallel to it. This extremely rare morphology is reminiscent of the radio galaxy 3C 338, a wide-angle-tail (WAT) hosted by the BCG of the rich cluster Abell 2199, in which a bright radio ridge of ultra-steep spectrum was detected Burns1983ApJ...271..575B .

gmw_00007060.name: J091900+315254
gmw_00007060.overview: J091900+315254 is a Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy hosted by a LINER galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.06194 [arXiv:2403.11290]. Its northern radio jet features a large kink with an amplitude of 38 kiloparsecs, which has no counterpart in the southern jet [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J091900+315254 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes WAT, FR I, jets in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J091900+315254: This FR I radio galaxy with the largest angular size of 10' (720 kpc) is hosted by a z = 0.06194 LINER galaxy whose radio core is clearly detected in the VLASS map at 3 GHz (Fig. fig:J091900 ). The most remarkable feature, seen in its LOFAR map is a huge `kink' in the northern jet, about 1 arcmin from the core. The kink has an amplitude 32'' (38 kpc) and a total length of 90'' (110 kpc). The kink has no counterpart in the southern jet.

gmw_00007061.name: J092229+545943
gmw_00007061.overview: J092229+545943 is an enigmatic radio galaxy hosted by a brightest cluster galaxy at a redshift of 0.18363 [arXiv:2403.11290]. The source exhibits a peculiar three-armed structure resembling a plier, with an overall radio extent of 622 kiloparsecs [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J092229+545943 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes BCG host in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J092229+545943 (The `plier'): This enigmatic radio source, resembling a plier (or a nutcracker) has a BCG ( z (spec)= 0.18363) situated at its pivot (tri-junction) (Fig. fig:J092229 ). The overall radio extent seen in the LOFAR map is 200'' (622 kpc). Much of the peculiar structure showing 3 arms, has remained undetected in the VLASS map which exhibits just two discrete patches coinciding with the northern radio arm. Roughly in the middle of these two radio patches is seen a faint optical object (J092229.12+545947.1) with a redshift of 0.18363.

gmw_00007062.name: J093032+311215
gmw_00007062.overview: J093032+311215 is a Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy with a centrally located compact radio core [arXiv:2403.11290]. The source features two well-aligned radio lobes, with two oppositely-directed radio spurs emanating from their mid-points [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J093032+311215 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes FR II, FR I, radio spur, lobes in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J093032+311215 (4C+31.34): As seen from Fig. fig:J092229 , the two lobes of this FR II source are well aligned with a centrally located compact radio core seen in the VLASS map. However, no optical counterpart (i.e., the host galaxy) to the core is present in the DeCAL database. Remarkably, the LoTSS DR2 map shows two oppositely-directed prominent radio spurs emanating from the two lobes, roughly at their mid-points. As in the case of the similarly large radio spur in J022830+382108 (see above), a brightening of the lobe near the foot of each spur is apparent and, once again, no optical object is visible near these two locations.

gmw_00007063.name: J094953+445658
gmw_00007063.overview: J094953+445658 is a Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of 0.195335 [arXiv:2403.11290]. It has been classified as an X-shaped radio galaxy, featuring two parallel radio lobes that exhibit a lateral offset [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J094953+445658 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes FR II, FR I, XRG, lobes in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J094953+445658: This source at z (spec) = 0.195335 has previously been classified as an XRG Yang2019ApJS..245...17Y and a C-shaped radio galaxy Liu2019RAA....19..127L . From Fig. fig:J094953 , it is seen to be a FR II radio galaxy with the core (and associated bright galaxy) located exactly in the middle of the two terminal hotspots. As seen from both LoTSS DR2 and VLASS maps, the two radio lobes, although parallel to each other, show a lateral offset. An example of such peculiar morphology, XRG J1328+5654, has recently been highlighted by Gopal-Krishna-3C223.1-2022A&A...663L...8G who have also pointed out potential difficulty in explaining the origin of the lateral offset between the two parallel primary lobes.

gmw_00007064.name: J101522+682351
gmw_00007064.overview: J101522+682351 is a Fanaroff-Riley Class I radio galaxy located at a redshift of 0.2098 [arXiv:2403.11290]. The system features a highly extended radio source spanning 932 kiloparsecs, which contains a symmetric pair of compact radio lobes embedded within an older structure [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J101522+682351 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes FR I, XRG, radio spur, radio ridge in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J101522+682351: The most remarkable feature of this is a highly extended FR I radio source of size 4.5' (932 kpc) is the conspicuous central radio bulge seen in the LoTSS DR2 map (Fig. fig:J101522 ). The redshift of the central bright galaxy is 0.2098, and it is detected in the VLASS map (Fig. fig:J101522 ). The galaxy is straddled by a symmetric pair of relatively compact radio lobes separated by 25'' . This appears to be a recently ejected pair of radio lobes, embedded within an older FR I source and sharing the radio axis with it.

gmw_00007065.name: J102544+345051
gmw_00007065.overview: J102544+345051 is a radio galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of 0.14721 [arXiv:2403.11290]. It is classified as an X-shaped radio galaxy, featuring a quadripolar structure with two diagonal axes misaligned by 70 degrees [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J102544+345051 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes XRG in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J102544+345051 (B3 1023+394) `The rectangle': The LoTSS map of this source exhibits a broadly rectangular shape. However, a closer inspection reveals a quadripolar structure with the two diagonal axes misaligned by 70 degrees (Fig. fig:J102544 a; ). Hence, it is classifiable as an XRG, with the east-west axis being the primary axis, as evident from the VLASS map (Fig. fig:J102544 ). Note that this map also shows a bright inner double straddling the optical host galaxy ( z (spec) = 0.14721, see Fig. fig:J102544 d), whose orientation further confirms that the primary axis has continued to be in east-west direction.

gmw_00007066.name: J102625+390929
gmw_00007066.overview: J102625+390929 is a wide-angle tail radio galaxy associated with a compact group of galaxies at a redshift of z = 0.14721 [arXiv:2403.11290]. It features a highly asymmetric, smoothly bending kiloparsec-scale jet that traces a nearly parabolic trajectory [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J102625+390929 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes WAT, jets in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J102625+390929: This WAT associated with a compact group of galaxies at z = 0.14721 Best2012MNRAS.421.1569B shows a smoothly bending kpc-scale jet, tracing a nearly perfect parabolic trajectory. However, the bending is very asymmetric, as it affects only the south-western jet. As a result, the apex of the jet profile is far removed from the host galaxy (Fig. fig:J102625 b). Such an unusually asymmetric bending of the two jets is possible when the direction of the galaxy's motion, which is opposite to the direction of the ram-pressure acting on the jet pair, is nearly parallel to the inner jet axis e.g, Bempong-Manfu2020MNRAS.496..676B .

gmw_00007067.name: J102704+444547
gmw_00007067.overview: J102704+444547 is a medium-distant radio galaxy with a spectroscopic redshift of approximately 0.586 [arXiv:2403.11290]. It is classified as a Fanaroff-Riley II source and a probable X-shaped radio galaxy, featuring a north-western jet and a strong radio bridge between its two hot spots [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J102704+444547 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes FR II, FR I, XRG, radio ridge in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J102704+444547: This medium-distant radio galaxy [ z (spec) = 0.58605 0.00007 ] has been classified as a probable XRG Yang2019ApJS..245...17Y . We find its LoTSS DR2 map to be fully consistent with FR II classification. It shows a prominent jet to the north-west and a strong radio bridge between the two hot spots. Remarkably, the bridge is highly asymmetric about the radio axis defined by the jets (Fig. fig:J102704 ).

gmw_00007068.name: J104234+364040
gmw_00007068.overview: J104234+364040 is a radio galaxy that initially appears to be a regular Fanaroff-Riley II source with two lobes connected by a radio bridge [arXiv:2403.11290]. However, it exhibits exceptional structural complexity, with its host galaxy candidate located near the northern edge of the southern lobe rather than the symmetry point [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J104234+364040 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes FR II, FR I, radio ridge, lobes in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J104234+364040: This source is a member of the BCG sample of Furnell2018MNRAS.478.4952F . Although, at the first glance, it seems to be a regular FR II source comprised of two lobes connected by a bridge of radio emission, a closer scrutiny unveils an exceptional structural complexity (Fig. fig:J104234 ). Firstly, there is no candidate for the host galaxy anywhere near the symmetry-point between the two lobes. The only object in the proximity of the radio axis is the bright z = 0.14133 galaxy located near the northern edge of the southern lobe.

gmw_00007069.name: J113222+555823
gmw_00007069.overview: J113222+555823 is a radio galaxy that has been classified as a candidate X-shaped radio galaxy [arXiv:2403.11290]. Its primary radio lobes extend north to south, while its wings lie along an orthogonal axis [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J113222+555823 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes FR II, FR I, XRG, lobes in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J113222+555823: Based on its FIRST survey map at 1.4 GHz Becker-first1995ApJ...450..559B , Proctor2011ApJS..194...31P noted structural peculiarity which he dubbed `Pretzel-with-handle'. More recently, Bera2022ApJS..260....7B included this source in their XRG sample, based on its LOFAR map and suggested that the primary lobes of the proposed XRG extend from north to south, whereas the wings lie along the orthogonal axis. If true, this would be the only known XRG with FR II wings. The optical field shows 3 galaxies (all having z 0.051) in the central part of the radio source, the southern two of which appear to blend.

gmw_00007070.name: J114429+370915
gmw_00007070.overview: J114429+370915 is a giant radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.115 [arXiv:2403.11290]. It is associated with an elliptical galaxy that exhibits starburst-like Balmer absorption lines and a young stellar population [arXiv:1508.03287, arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J114429+370915 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes XRG, lobes in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J114429+370915 (4C+37.32, B2 1141+37): This source has a long history of observations. Ulrich1980Natur.288..459U found it to be a GRG with 1 Mpc size, located at z = 0.115. In their VLA observations, Machalski1982AJ.....87.1150M found it to have a simple double-lobed structure, 267'' (560 kpc) in size and associated with a 16.5-mag elliptical which is the brightest in a chain of 4 galaxies and is centrally located between the two terminal radio hot spots (Fig. fig:J114429 ). They also suspected the presence of a very steep spectrum radio component, to be discovered at low frequencies.

sources.summary: B2 1141+37 is fit by a young stellar population, with starburst-like Balmer absorption and otherwise little optical emission.

quotes.quote: B2 1141+37. A young stellar population yields a good fit to the UV data. In principle, a power-law component might produce an equally plausible fit, but we note that the spectrum has clear star burst characteristics (higher Balmer lines present in absorption). The optical spectrum is otherwise lacking emission features.

gmw_00007071.name: J114434+672424
gmw_00007071.overview: J114434+672424 is a radio galaxy identified as a brightest cluster galaxy at a redshift of 0.11612 [arXiv:2403.11290]. It exhibits an S-shaped morphology featuring a radio core, a western lobe with two bending ridge lines, and an eastern lobe that splits into two collimated streams [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J114434+672424 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes radio ridge, lobes, jets, BCG host in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J114434+672424 (6C 114151+674026): This source is identified with a BCG at z (spec) = 0.11612 Ahn2012ApJS..203...21A . Its LoTSS map is broadly consistent with an S-shaped morphology, with a clear VLSS detection of a radio core in the host galaxy (Fig. fig:J114434 ). A morphological peculiarity revealed by the LoTSS DR2 map is that the western lobe has a pair of well-resolved ridge lines (jets?) emerging from the host galaxy, both undergoing sharp bending. Likewise, the emission from the eastern lobe, after a sharp bend, is later seen to split into two collimated streams, each containing some compact radio structure visible in the VLASS map (Fig. fig:J114434 ).

gmw_00007072.name: J115608+441058
gmw_00007072.overview: J115608+441058 is a double radio source with a basic radio axis defined by a ridge-line oriented at a position angle of 150 degrees [arXiv:2403.11290]. A compact chain of three galaxies at a redshift of 0.298 lies roughly orthogonal to this radio ridge [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J115608+441058 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes radio spur, radio ridge, jets in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J115608+441058: The basic radio axis of this double radio source seems to be defined by a ridge-line (twin-jet?) oriented at position angle (PA) 150 deg, in the middle of which lies a compact chain of 3 galaxies. These galaxies are aligned roughly orthogonal to the radio ridge and have spectroscopically measured redshifts of 0.298. Roughly along the galaxy chain lies a radio `spur' emanating from the central region of the double radio source. The optical-radio (VLASS) overlay shown in Fig. fig:J115608 d suggests that the `spur' may in fact be a NAT associated with the northernmost galaxy of the chain, which is also the least bright/massive of them and could well be moving under the gravitational influence of the other two galaxies.

gmw_00007073.name: J133116+441851
gmw_00007073.overview: J133116+441851 is an early-type elliptical galaxy with a photometric redshift of approximately 0.248 [arXiv:2403.11290]. It hosts an inner FR II double radio source with asymmetric lobes that bend sharply to connect with two giant synchrotron-emitting radio spiral arms [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J133116+441851 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes radio spiral, FR II, FR I, lobes in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J133116+441851 (6C 132908+443418): As seen from the LoTSS DR2 map (Fig. fig:J133116 a,b), this object is a very rare case of an early-type galaxy having two well developed `giant' spiral arms of synchrotron emission extending over 100'' . Spectroscopic redshift is not available for this `radio spiral'; but its photometric redshift of 0.248 0.033 (SIMBAD) implies a huge projected size of 392 kpc. The inner radio structure is a FR II double radio source of size 17'' (67 kpc), asymmetrically straddling the host elliptical and giving the radio source the appearance of a giant barred radio spiral. The inner radio structure, well resolved in the VLASS map, consists of an asymmetric pair of radio lobes extending over 18'' , which straddle the bright elliptical galaxy and bend sharply to join the two giant radio spiral arms (Fig. fig:J133116 ).

gmw_00007074.name: J135141+555938
gmw_00007074.overview: J135141+555938 is a spiral-shaped radio galaxy hosted by a dominant southern galaxy at a redshift of approximately 0.07 [arXiv:2403.11290]. This host galaxy features strongly twisted radio arms spanning 100 kiloparsecs and has a northern companion galaxy projected onto its upper radio arm [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J135141+555938 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes radio spiral, FR I in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J135141+555938: This is another rare case of a spiral-shaped radio galaxy, with strongly twisted radio arms. The optical field shows a pair of bright galaxies at z (spec) 0.07, separated north-south by 21'' (28 kpc) (Fig. fig:J135141 ,d). Each galaxy has a radio core detected in the VLASS map where the dominant southern galaxy can be identified as the host of the large spiral structure spanning 75'' (100 kpc). The northern galaxy is projected on the upper radio spiral arm and is faintly detected in the VLASS map (Fig. fig:J135141 d).

gmw_00007075.name: J140311+382756
gmw_00007075.overview: J140311+382756 is a double-double radio galaxy hosted by an elliptical galaxy at a photometric redshift of 0.541 [arXiv:2403.11290]. It features a twin-lobed radio structure and a radio spur that jets out orthogonally to the main double-double axis [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J140311+382756 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes XRG, radio spur, lobes, jets in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J140311+382756 (4C+38.38): Basic twin-lobed radio structure of this source became known from the 2.7 GHz VLA map published by Rudnick1979AJ.....84..437R . The VLASS map shows it to be actually a well-aligned double-double radio galaxy (DDRG), with a secure optical identification of photometric redshift of 0.541 (Fig. fig:J140311 b). Its another striking feature unveiled by the LoTSS DR2 map, is a prominent radio spur jetting out from the host elliptical orthogonally to the DDRG axis (Fig. fig:J140311 a ). This radio spur is not detected in the VLASS map and no optical object is seen to coincide with any part of the spur.

gmw_00007076.name: J143527+550756
gmw_00007076.overview: J143527+550756 is a double radio source identified with a brightest cluster galaxy at a redshift of 0.13985 [arXiv:2403.11290]. Its northwestern radio jet undergoes a sharp bend and expands into an FR I-like plume, while its eastern lobe is edge-brightened with an FR II morphology [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J143527+550756 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes FR II, FR I, HYMORS, lobes in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J143527+550756 (6C 143354+552059): This double radio source with LAS 65'' (160 kpc) is identified with a BCG at z (spec) = 0.13985 Ahn2012ApJS..203...21A . The VLASS map shows a bright radio core associated with a bright elliptical, as well as a prominent radio jet towards northwest direction (Fig. fig:J143527 ). The jet undergoes a sharp bend at a distance of 27'' (67 kpc) from the radio nucleus and thereafter expands to form a plume, all of which is clearly reminiscent of FR I morphology. In contrast, the eastern lobe is edge-brightened (FR II).

gmw_00007077.name: J165732+431941
gmw_00007077.overview: J165732+431941 is a complex radio source identified with a brightest cluster galaxy at a spectroscopic redshift of 0.204 [arXiv:2403.11290]. Its radio emission features a wide-angle tail structure and a 135-kiloparsec-long eastern radio arm [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J165732+431941 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes WAT, HYMORS, BCG host in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J165732+431941: This complex radio source is identified with a BCG at a (spectroscopic) redshift of 0.204. The LoTSS map shows 3 prominent radio arms with a central peak at their node (Fig. fig:J165732 ). The VLASS map shows a radio core at the position of the BCG, both significantly offset from the nodal point by 12'' (40 kpc) towards northwest. A joint inspection of the VLASS and LoTSS DR2 maps suggests that the western and southern radio arms together define a WAT associated with the BCG, The nature and role of the 40'' (135 kpc long eastern radio arm remains unclear).

gmw_00007078.name: J170940+342537
gmw_00007078.overview: J170940+342537 is a radio galaxy identified as the brightest cluster galaxy of the Abell 2249 cluster [arXiv:2403.11290]. It is classified as a wide-angle tail source with north-south oriented jets that bend sharply eastward to form two tails [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J170940+342537 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes WAT, jets, BCG host in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J170940+342537 (The `Square') (B2 1707+34, 4C+34.45): This source is identified with the BCG of the Abell cluster 2249 at z (photo)=0.0806 Owen1997ApJS..108...41O . Using VLA, Jetha2006MNRAS.368..609J have mapped this source at 8.5 GHz, with a 3^ 2^ beam. While their map is consistent with the VLASS map seen in Fig. fig:J170940 d, the higher sensitivity of their map make the WAT classification secure, with roughly north-south oriented jets bending sharply towards the east and forming the two tails. On the LOTSS DR2 map, the tails can be traced farther out to the east and merging together, thus giving this `fat WAT' a square-shaped appearance.

gmw_00007079.name: J221255+220523
gmw_00007079.overview: J221255+220523 is an FR II-type radio galaxy with a possible faint optical host galaxy [arXiv:2403.11290]. Its radio structure features two parallel, 45-arcsecond-long lobes that exhibit a lateral offset of approximately 15 arcseconds [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J221255+220523 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes FR II, FR I, lobes in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J221255+220523: Both VLASS and LoTSS DR2 maps clearly show this source to be FR II type, albeit without a radio core detection even in VLASS (Fig. fig:J221255 b). The faint (m 21.93) optical object seen near the line joining the two hotspots is a possible host galaxy, with coordinates: RA= 22h 12m 56.37s, DEC= 22d 05' 08.2'' . The LoTSS DR2 map shows an extended lobe behind each hot spot and while these 45'' long radio lobes run parallel to each other, they exhibit a lateral offset by about 15'' (Fig. fig:J221255 a). This FR II source is thus another example of `offset parallel twin-lobes' (OPTL), like the source J094953+445658 mentioned above see, also Gopal-Krishna-3C223.1-2022A&A...663L...8G and J074144+741440 Fig. 3 of Cohen2005ApJ...620L...5C .

gmw_00007080.name: J221558+290921
gmw_00007080.overview: J221558+290921 is a radio galaxy hosted by a bright optical galaxy [arXiv:2403.11290]. It exhibits a Fanaroff-Riley II morphology with parallel lobes that maintain a lateral offset of 112 kiloparsecs [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J221558+290921 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes FR II, FR I, lobes in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J221558+290921: As seen from the VLASS map, this source has a clear FR II morphology. The bright optical galaxy lying roughly in the middle of the two terminal hotspots is the most probable host galaxy, supported by its radio detection (Fig. fig:J221558 d). The LoTSS DR2 map (Fig. fig:J221558 a) shows both its lobes to extend right up to the parent galaxy, such that they run nearly parallel to each other, but maintaining a clear lateral offset of 30'' (112 kpc). Hence this source is one more example of the OPTL morphology (see above).

gmw_00007081.name: J235938+400644
gmw_00007081.overview: J235938+400644 is a radio galaxy with a peculiar morphology concentrated along two orthogonal axes [arXiv:2403.11290]. Its north-south radio ridge exhibits a head-tail structure, with the head coinciding with a bright galaxy at a photometric redshift of approximately 0.1967 [arXiv:2403.11290].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J235938+400644 as an ANOMERS radio galaxy/source and describes radio ridge in LoTSS/VLASS imaging.

quotes.quote: J235938+400644: The peculiar radio morphology of this source, seen in the LoTSS DR2 map, shows the emission to be concentrated along two orthogonal axes (Fig. fig:J235938 a b). The north-south radio ridge appears to have a head-tail morphology, as seen from the VLASS map (Fig. fig:J235938 a,d), with the head coinciding with a bright galaxy at z (photo) 0.1967, and the tail pointing to the north. About 13'' north of this galaxy is seen another fairly bright optical galaxy, which is not clearly detected in the VLASS (Fig. fig:J235938 d). Consequently, the host galaxy of the east-west radio ridge remains unclear.

gmw_00007082.name: Horologium I
gmw_00007082.overview: Horologium I is an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy located at a distance of approximately 68 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1809.02259]. It hosts an old, metal-poor stellar population and exhibits faint, low-density substructure to the south of its center [arXiv:1809.02259].

sources.summary: The paper finds Horologium I is most likely an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy with old, metal-poor populations, a compact half-light radius, possible substructure, and Magellanic-group membership evidence.

quotes.quote: Horologium I is most likely an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy at D_ = 68 3 kpc, with r_h = 23^ +4 _ 3 pc and [ Fe/H ] = -2.40^ +0.10 _ 0.35 dex. It's color-magnitude diagram shows evidence of a split sub-giant branch similar to that seen in some globular clusters. Additionally, Gaia DR2 data suggests it is, or was, a member of the Magellanic Cloud group.

A well-defined concentration of Hor I stars is visible at _0=02^ h 55^ m 28 3 , _0=-54^ 07' 17'' . The increased number statistics from this study also reveals the existence of faint features such as low density substructure beyond the half-light ellipse to the south of the centre, which could not be detected in previous shallower photometric surveys.

gmw_00007083.name: Pictor I
gmw_00007083.overview: Pictor I is an outer Milky Way halo system located 120 kpc away, equidistant from the Magellanic Clouds [arXiv:1809.02259]. It consists of an old, metal-poor stellar population with a mildly elliptical distribution [arXiv:1809.02259].

sources.summary: The paper treats Pictor I as an ultra-faint satellite candidate, finding a compact, old, metal-poor, mildly elliptical system whose properties closely resemble confirmed star clusters.

quotes.quote: Pic I is an outer MW halo system and can be found 20 degrees from the Large Magellanic Cloud and 33 degrees from the Small Magellanic Cloud (Figure fig:MCs ). Interestingly, its reported heliocentric distance of 120 kpc places it equidistant from the Magellanic Clouds ( 83 kpc). Unlike Hor I, Pic I has no line-of-sight overlap with the HI Magellanic Stream and is well isolated on the sky. It is roughly the same size and has a similar luminosity as the other satellites presented here ( r_ h 30 pc, M_ V = -3.1 ).

Overall, it is a well defined system with a mildly elliptical distribution. The inner ellipse of the best-fitting 2D-model has a semi-major axis length of 1 r_h and contains 50 of the Pic I stellar population, while the outer ellipse has a semi-major axis length of 2.32 r_h and contains 90 of the Pic I stars.
Similar to Hor I, Pic I consists of an old (11.8 Gyr), metal-poor ( [ Fe/H ] =-2.28 ) stellar population with [ /Fe ] _ avg =+0.2 .

gmw_00007084.name: Grus I
gmw_00007084.overview: Grus I is an outer Milky Way halo system that appears unrelated to the Magellanic Clouds [arXiv:1809.02259]. Rather than having a single central concentration, the system consists of two smaller, spatially extended stellar overdensities that may have been tidally distorted [arXiv:1809.02259].

sources.summary: The paper finds Grus I is an outer Milky Way halo system with two stellar concentrations, extended structure, and possible tidal distortion.

quotes.quote: At our derived heliocentric distance D_ =115 6 kpc, Gru I is around 94 kpc from the LMC and 69 kpc from the SMC. Given these relatively large distances it would appear that Gru I is an outer MW halo system unrelated to the Magellanic Clouds in the foreground. Structural Parameters Figure fig:GruIstellar_distribution shows the stellar distribution corresponding to the best-fit isochrone seen in the top right panel of Figure fig:cmdiso_GruI .

The increased number statistics in the density map resolves the central region of Gru I and reveals that the system is not made up of a well defined central concentration, but rather consists of two smaller stellar overdensities that are distributed on either side of the centre of mass at ( _0), ( _0) =(0,0) in Figure fig:GruIstellar_distribution . We calculated the centre of mass by allocating each Gru I star its stellar mass with the help of the best-fitting Dartmouth isochrone.
The overdensities are spatially extended suggesting they may have been tidally distorted.

gmw_00007085.name: Phoenix II
gmw_00007085.overview: Phoenix II is a compact, metal-poor dwarf galaxy with a half-light radius of 12.6 parsecs [arXiv:1809.02259]. It exhibits S-shaped tidal arms aligned with the Large Magellanic Cloud, indicating tidal stripping and a likely association with the Magellanic Cloud satellite group [arXiv:1809.02259].

sources.summary: The paper measures Phoenix II as a compact, metal-poor system with S-shaped tidal arms and likely association with the Magellanic Cloud group.

quotes.quote: Phoenix II has a half-light radius of r_h = 12.6 2.5 pc and an [ Fe/H ] = -2.10^ +0.25 _ 0.20 and exhibits S-shaped tidal arms extending from its compact core.

Phe II is the closest of the four study objects to the SMC ( 20 degrees) and is about 39 degrees from the LMC. With a reported heliocentric distance of 80 kpc, Phe II is approximately 32 kpc from the SMC and 54 kpc from the LMC. As can be seen in Figure fig:MCs , like Hor I, it resides in the outskirts of the HI gas component from the Magellanic Stream, raising the possibility that both of these systems might be part of the Magellanic Cloud satellite group.

Phe II's stellar density map shows remarkably clear evidence of tidal stripping (Figure fig:PheIIstellar_distribution ) with a nearly symmetrical and elongated distribution of stars extending on either side of a compact centre reaching beyond the half-light radius and following the S-shaped characteristic of mass-loss found around tidally disturbed globular clusters like Pal 5 Rockosi2002, Odenkirchen2003, Grillmair2006 , NGC 5466 Belokurov2006 , Eridanus 3, Pal 15 Myeong2017 and NGC 7492 Navarrete2017 . As demonstrated by the black line in Figure fig:PheIIstellar_distribution , Phe II's tidal arms are well aligned with the direction of the Large Magellanic Cloud.

gmw_00007086.name: NGC 4277
gmw_00007086.overview: NGC 4277 is a gas-poor lenticular galaxy located behind the Virgo cluster [arXiv:2208.04932, arXiv:2303.11441]. It hosts a short, weak stellar bar with a slow pattern speed and a high dark-matter fraction within the bar region [arXiv:2208.04932, arXiv:2303.11441].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4277 as a possible source of contamination for VCC 390 because its HI profile overlaps in velocity and the Arecibo beam side lobes slightly overlap it.

quotes.quote: However, it is possible that some of this line emission originates from contamination in the side-lobes due to the nearby galaxy NGC 4277, which is at a velocity 2504 , and has an HI line profile that overlaps in velocity with VCC 390's (see van Driel et al. 2000). We investigated this possibility by overlaying the Arecibo beam map onto a DSS image of VCC 390 and NGC 4277. We found that the outer lobes of the profile as centered on VCC 390 slightly overlapped with NGC 4277.

Short of this, the large velocity width could be the result of gas stripping. It is however impossible to determine using our current data how much these sources are contributing to the VCC 390 signal. Understanding this will require mapping the spatial extent of the HI distribution of VCC 390 and comparing it with that of NGC 4277.

sources.summary: The paper characterizes NGC 4277 as a lenticular barred galaxy whose short, weak stellar bar has a well-constrained slow pattern speed measured with photometry and MUSE kinematics.

quotes.quote: We characterised the properties of the bar hosted in lenticular galaxy NGC 4277, which is located behind the Virgo cluster. We measured the bar length and strength from the surface photometry obtained from the broad-band imaging of the Sloan Digital Sky Survey and we derived the bar pattern speed from the stellar kinematics obtained from the integral-field spectroscopy performed with the Multi Unit Spectroscopic Explorer at the Very Large Telescope. We also estimated the co-rotation radius from the circular velocity, which we constrained by correcting the stellar streaming motions for asymmetric drift, and we finally derived the bar rotation rate. We found that NGC 4277 hosts a short ( R_ bar =3.2^ +0.9 _ 0.6 kpc), weak ( S_ bar =0.21 0.02 ), and slow ( R =1.8^ +0.5 _ 0.3 ) bar and its pattern speed ( _ bar =24.7 3.4 km s ^ 1 kpc ^ 1 ) is amongst the best-constrained ones ever obtained with the Tremaine-Weinberg (TW) method with relative statistical errors of 0.2 . NGC 4277 is the first clear-cut case of a galaxy hosting a slow stellar bar ( R >1.4 at more than a 1 confidence level) measured with the model-independent TW method.

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 4277 is a gas-poor lenticular with a slow, short bar and a higher dark-matter fraction within the bar region than NGC 4264.

quotes.quote: We investigate the link between the bar rotation rate and dark matter content in barred galaxies by concentrating on the cases of the lenticular galaxies NGC 4264 and NGC 4277. These two gas-poor galaxies have similar morphologies, sizes, and luminosities. But, NGC 4264 hosts a fast bar, which extends to nearly the corotation, while the bar embedded in NGC 4277 is slow and falls short of corotation. We derive the fraction of dark matter f_ DM, bar within the bar region from Jeans axisymmetric dynamical models by matching the stellar kinematics obtained with the MUSE integral-field spectrograph and using SDSS images to recover the stellar mass distribution.

We find that the inner regions of NGC 4277 host a larger fraction of dark matter ( f_ DM, bar = 0.53 0.02 ) with respect to NGC 4264 ( f_ DM, bar = 0.33 0.04 ) in agreement with the predictions of theoretical works and the findings of numerical simulations, which have found that fast bars live in baryon-dominated discs, whereas slow bars experienced a strong drag from the dynamical friction due to a dense DM halo.

gmw_00007087.name: GOODS-S 983
gmw_00007087.overview: GOODS-S 983 is a galaxy featuring a red core surrounded by extended LINER-like emission that reaches out to 6 kpc [arXiv:1810.02819]. Because the galaxy lacks a Chandra X-ray source or [O III]/H detection, an active supermassive black hole is unlikely to be the source of this ionization [arXiv:1810.02819].

sources.summary: GOODS-S 983 shows extended LINER-like emission to 6 kpc around a red core; its line ratios and lack of X-ray/[O III] evidence argue against an active nucleus.

quotes.quote: GOODS-S 983 presents significant extended LINER-like emission out to 6 kpc (Figure fig:LINERpaper ), mostly centered around the red core visible in Figure fig:RGBpaper . This reinforces the notion that an evolved stellar population is providing the source of the ionization. Most of the pixels satisfy our criterion of [ SII ] /(H + [ NII ] ) >-0.4 , placing them in the AGN regime of the modified BPT diagram. However, given the lack of a detection in [ OIII ] /H or a matched Chandra source, it is unlikely that an active super-massive black hole is the source of LINER-like emission.

gmw_00007088.name: GOODS-S 15738
gmw_00007088.overview: GOODS-S 15738 is a galaxy characterized by LINER-like emission that likely has a non-AGN origin [arXiv:1810.02819]. It exhibits diffuse emission extending to 3 kpc, as well as a 9-kpc strand associated with a blue knot of active star formation [arXiv:1810.02819].

sources.summary: GOODS-S 15738 has diffuse and strand-like extended emission, partly associated with a blue knot; its line ratios and Chandra properties support non-AGN ionization.

quotes.quote: GOODS-S 15738 exhibits strong diffuse emission out to 3 kpc in addition to a strand that extends out to 9 kpc (Figure fig:LINERpaper ). The latter emission appears to track strongly with a blue knot (Figure fig:RGBpaper ) which may indicate that this specific emission comes from a region of active star formation in line with the `starburst hypothesis' of extended emission. The large spatial extent of the emission, most of which satisfies the [ SII ] /(H + [ NII ] ) criterion (Figure fig:BPTpaperH ), along with the lack of an [ OIII ] /H ratio detection suggest a non-AGN origin for the LINER-like emission. In addition, the corresponding Chandra detection for the object satisfies none of the Luo17CDFS AGN criteria and can be accounted for by black hole binary emission alone, further supporting the non-AGN hypothesis for ionization.

gmw_00007089.name: GOODS-S 26087
gmw_00007089.overview: GOODS-S 26087 is a star-forming galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.095 within a Hubble Ultra Deep Field overdensity [arXiv:1903.09167]. It exhibits extended LINER-like emission out to 6 kpc, which is likely not driven by active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:1810.02819].

sources.summary: ASPECS-LP.06 is a z=1.095 CO(2-1) source in a HUDF overdensity, with a rich star-forming MUSE spectrum and non-AGN X-ray detection.

quotes.quote: ASPECS-LP.06: CO(2-1) at z=1.095 . Part of an overdensity in the HUDF at the same redshift. Rich star-forming spectrum in MUSE with a wealth of continuum and emission features. Detected in X-ray, but not classified as an AGN 749 Luo2017 .

sources.summary: GOODS-S 26087 shows robust extended detections to 6 kpc, with line ratios, low [O III]/H, and X-ray behavior indicating LINER-like emission not driven by AGN activity.

quotes.quote: GOODS-S 26087 presents robust detections out to 6 kpc in almost every direction, most of which satisfies the requirement of [ SII ] /H + [ NII ] >-0.4 ( fig:BPTpaperH ). Despite no corresponding R or K_s band information, the pure X-ray AGN criteria are not satisfied, and the X-ray emission can be accounted for by black hole binaries. Coupled with a low [ OIII ] /H ratio, the areas of LINER-like emission are likely not excited due to AGN activity.

gmw_00007090.name: GOODS-S 36653
gmw_00007090.overview: GOODS-S 36653 is a galaxy that exhibits LINER-like emission extending up to 6 kpc in its northeastern region [arXiv:1810.02819]. Although its integrated line ratios resemble those of an active galactic nucleus, Chandra X-ray criteria support a non-AGN origin for this ionization [arXiv:1810.02819].

sources.summary: GOODS-S 36653 has LINER-like emission on the northeast side, integrated line ratios that would resemble AGN classification, but Chandra criteria support a non-AGN origin.

quotes.quote: GOODS-S 36653 presents a region of LINER-like emission up to 6 kpc in the northeast portion of the galaxy (Figure fig:LINERpaper ). This corresponds to the edge of the visible core in Figure fig:RGBpaper , an area that may be dominated by evolved stars. For this object, there are corresponding [ OIII ] to H detections which place all pixels confidently within the LINER regime. Furthermore, the overall Grizli emission measurements place the object as a whole in the LINER region of the modified BPT diagram, presenting an object that would likely be classified as an AGN in a typical spectroscopic survey. The discrepancy between the Chandra X-ray luminosity and estimated black hole binary contribution, a factor of about 2.3, is likely due to a contribution from hot gas, or large uncertainties in the SED modeled stellar mass. Like all the other candidates, GOODS-S 36653 does not satisfy any Chandra X-ray criteria, again reinforcing the non-AGN origin for the ionization.

gmw_00007091.name: GOODS-N 18043
gmw_00007091.overview: GOODS-N 18043 is a galaxy that exhibits extended LINER-like emission within its core and along a southeast spiral arm [arXiv:1810.02819]. This emission likely has a non-active galactic nucleus origin due to a weak [O III]/H ratio and the absence of a Chandra detection [arXiv:1810.02819].

sources.summary: GOODS-N 18043 shows extended core and spiral-arm LINER-like emission; its weak [O III]/H and absent Chandra match point toward a non-AGN origin.

quotes.quote: GOODS-N 18043 shows extended LINER-like emission in the core along with a strip along the southeast spiral arm (Figures fig:RGBpaper and fig:LINERpaper ). In addition, most of the pixels satisfy our [ SII ] /H + [ NII ] AGN selection (Figure fig:BPTpaperH ). While the integrated [ OIII ] /H ratio can be measured, there are no individual pixels with sufficient SNR for detection. The weak [ OIII ] /H , coupled with the lack of a matched Chandra detection point towards a non-AGN origin for LINER-like emission, whether from the red core, or from the bluer spiral arm.

gmw_00007092.name: GOODS-N 21285
gmw_00007092.overview: GOODS-N 21285 is a high-redshift galaxy that exhibits LINER-like emission extending past its core out to a distance of 6 kpc [arXiv:1810.02819]. Multi-band Chandra X-ray observations show no evidence of an active galactic nucleus, suggesting its X-ray emission is instead consistent with black hole binaries [arXiv:1810.02819].

sources.summary: GOODS-N 21285 has LINER-like emission extending beyond the core to 6 kpc; multi-band Chandra criteria show no AGN evidence and X-rays consistent with black hole binaries.

quotes.quote: GOODS-N 21285 shows large spatial extent of LINER [ SII ] /(H + [ NII ] ) ratios past the core, out to 6 kpc. This emission tracks with the bluer regions seen in Figure fig:RGBpaper , but is also present in the redder core. Most of the individual pixels satisfying our SNR cuts do satisfy our [ SII ] /(H + [ NII ] ) AGN selection threshold (Figure fig:BPTpaperH ). GOODS-N 21285 is our only candidate with matched observations across every band used in the Luo17CDFS Chandra X-ray criteria. Again, we find no evidence to suggest that the object is an AGN based on these detections and has X-ray emission consistent with black hole binaries.

gmw_00007093.name: GOODS-N 22593
gmw_00007093.overview: GOODS-N 22593 is a high-redshift galaxy characterized by spatially extended, ionized regions consistent with LINER-like emission lines [arXiv:1810.02819]. Although it exhibits compact emission, this emission still extends out to 3 kiloparsecs, and the lack of X-ray detection suggests its ionization is not driven by an active nucleus [arXiv:1810.02819].

sources.summary: GOODS-N 22593 is the most compact source, but its emission still extends to 3 kpc; line ratios and no X-ray detection favor ionization not caused by an active nucleus.

quotes.quote: GOODS-N 22593 shows the most compact emission of all of our sources, however it still extends out to 3 kpc. Furthermore, given the lack of an X-ray detection and emission line ratios which place almost all pixels on the modified BPT in an area dominated by LINERs (Figure fig:BPTpaperH ), it is likely that the ionization is not due to an active nucleus.

All eight of our candidates present ionized regions consistent with LINER-like emission lines which are spatially extended, and therefore, inconsistent with the purely nuclear understanding of an AGN.

gmw_00007094.name: GOODS-N 22815
gmw_00007094.overview: GOODS-N 22815 is a high-redshift galaxy that presents emission consistent with LINER-like ratios extending out to 4 kpc [arXiv:1810.02819]. Its diffuse emission, low [O III]/H ratios, and Chandra X-ray observations do not support the presence of an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1810.02819].

sources.summary: GOODS-N 22815 has LINER-like emission to 4 kpc and measured [O III]/H pixels; diffuse emission, low ratios, and Chandra tests do not support an active nucleus.

quotes.quote: GOODS-N 22815 presents emission consistent with LINER-like ratios out to 4 kpc. This is the second object, along with GOODS-S 36653, where we have measurements of the [ OIII ] /H ratios for individual pixels (Figure fig:BPTpaperH ). While the majority of the detected pixels lie below the [ SII ] /(H + [ NII ] ) cut, the diffuse nature of the emission and the low [ OIII ] /H measurements do not support an active nucleus as the source of the ionizing radiation. The discrepancy between the measured X-ray luminosity and the estimated galactic contribution, a factor of about 2.5, is likely due to a contribution from hot gas, or large uncertainties in the SED modeled stellar mass. Furthermore, the matching Chandra observations do not satisfy any AGN criteria by themselves or when compared to observations in other bands.

gmw_00007095.name: J 2225+0527
gmw_00007095.overview: J 2225+0527 is a luminous, radio-loud quasar at redshift z=2.323 [arXiv:2101.08291, arXiv:2110.00019] that features a one-sided radio jet extending southwest [arXiv:2110.00019]. The host galaxy contains a biconical ionized gas outflow [arXiv:2101.08291, arXiv:2110.00019] and a southwest clump photoionized by both star formation and the quasar [arXiv:2101.08291].

sources.summary: The paper presents 4C05.84 as a luminous quasar host with biconical outflow regions and a clump confirmed as part of the host rather than a separate galaxy.

quotes.quote: 4C05.84 is a luminous quasar at z=2.323 with a slightly reddened UV continuum. For this target, we identify three distinct regions. Regions ``S component A" and ``NE component A" are the blue(red) shifted outflow regions resembling a bi-conical outflow. They showcase broad extended emission with a line FWHM of 800 . The quasar photoionizes these regions.

Region ``SW component A clump", shows a line FWHM of 467.9 3.0 and is photoionized by a combination of star formation and the quasar. This clump is also detected in NIRC2 imaging of this object studied by , where they consider this clump to be associated with a damped Ly system. However, here we confirm that this objected is part of the quasar host galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes 4C 05.84 as a radio-loud quasar with a southwest jet, extended ionized gas, a biconical ionized outflow, and blueshifted clumpy CO(4-3) emission.

quotes.quote: 4C 05.84 is a radio-loud quasar at z=2.323. The one-sided radio jet in this system extends towards the southwest with a maximum extent of 12 kpc. There is a bright radio core component associated with the quasar's optical emission location. With Keck/OSIRIS observations, we detected extended ionized gas on an 8 kpc scale in the , , and emission lines Vayner19a . We detected a bi-conical ionized outflow extending along the northeast and southwest direction.

In our ALMA band 4 observations, we detect extended CO (4-3) emission that is offset in the western direction consisting of multiple clumps. The individual clump components and the spatially integrated emission is highly blueshifted relative to the quasar's systemic redshift. This emission is not associated with any known galactic component.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J 2225+0527 as a luminous red background quasar at z=2.323 whose colors were reddened by dust in the foreground DLA, causing SDSS misclassification.

quotes.quote: Although the vast majority of the quasars in these surveys were reddened by dust in the hosts rather than in foreground DLAs, we did encounter one very strong candidate: a DLA at redshift z=2.13 towards the quasar J 2225+0527 Interestingly, this target, although not classified as such in SDSS, is a well-known radio source discovered by Gower1967 and identified as 4C 05.84. The QSO was later spectroscopically observed and a redshift of z=2.323 was measured Barthel1990 .

We conclude that dust in the DLA is causing the colours of this intrinsically very luminous background quasar to appear much redder than average quasars, thereby not fulfilling the criteria for quasar identification in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey. Such chemically enriched and dusty absorbers are thus underrepresented in current samples of DLAs.

sources.summary: The paper adds 4C05.84 as a non-SDSS target and finds biconical extended plus nuclear outflows consistent with AGN radiation pressure or shocks.

quotes.quote: We added 4C09.17 and 4C05.84, which were not observed with SDSS, however, satisfy the radio, tip/tilt star, and archival imaging and spectroscopic data criteria.
We find bi-conical outflows in 3C318, 3C298 and 4C05.84 while we see one-sided conical outflows in 3C9, 4C04.81, 3C268.4 and 4C 09.17.
The nuclear outflow in 4C05.84 is also consistent with radiation pressure, however in a less dense environment compared to 4C04.81 and 4C57.29.

gmw_00007096.name: Abell 0122, D1
gmw_00007096.overview: Abell 0122, D1 is a remnant radio galaxy or fossil plasma likely associated with the brightest cluster galaxy 6dF J0057228 261653 [arXiv:2106.12281]. The source has a projected extent of 310 kpc and exhibits a curved low-frequency radio spectrum [arXiv:2106.12281].

sources.summary: The paper reclassifies Abell 0122 D1 as a likely remnant radio galaxy or BCG fossil plasma, measuring its extent and curved low-frequency radio spectrum.

quotes.quote: Reported by Duchesne2017 as an unclassified steep spectrum source. The source is detected in the MWA-2, TGSS, and deep ASKAP data, shown in fig:a0122:radio and fig:a0122:optical . The deep ASKAP data show a complex source with additional point source contributions (labelled in fig:a0122:optical ) and with contribution from what may be the core of the emission, the brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) (6dF J0057228 261653; Jones2009 ) indicated by a magenta arrow in fig:a0122:optical .

The projected extent of the source is 2.6 arcmin (corresponding to 310 kpc), including the protrusion to the West of Source A. This is slightly smaller than that reported by Duchesne2017 due to less source blending. The SED between 88--943 MHz is shown in fig:sed:a0122 , finding curvature between the MWA and ASKAP data after subtraction of the labelled sources, and with a spectral index from 88--216 MHz of _ 88 ^ 216 = -1.6 0.1 . We consider this a remnant radio galaxy, likely associated with the BCG, or otherwise fossil plasma originally from the BCG.

gmw_00007097.name: Abell S1099, D1
gmw_00007097.overview: Abell S1099, D1 is a remnant radio galaxy that is likely hosted by LEDA 195207 [arXiv:2106.12281]. The galaxy lacks an obvious core, clear lobes, or hot spots, suggesting its radio emission has diffused into the surrounding medium [arXiv:2106.12281].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Abell S1099 D1 as a likely remnant radio galaxy with diffuse emission, no clear core or lobes, and a putative LEDA 195207 host.

quotes.quote: MWA-2 radio data shown in fig:as1099:radio and PS1 optical data shown in fig:as1099:optical . RACS data shows no additional discrete sources beyond Source A, which is subtracted from flux density measurements where appropriate with _ A ,216 ^ 1400 = -0.5 0.1 . The resulting spectral index of the diffuse source D1 is found to be _ 88 ^ 1400 = -0.87 0.11 ( fig:sed:as1099 ).

The lack of obvious core or any clear lobes/hot spots suggests a remnant radio source that has diffused into the surrounding medium. No deep X-ray observations are available, no cluster or source is detected in the RASS image, and there is no detection as a Planck SZ source. We consider this a remnant radio galaxy with the putative host (LEDA 195207) indicated by a magenta arrow on fig:as1099:optical .

gmw_00007098.name: MCXC J0154.2 5937, D1
gmw_00007098.overview: MCXC J0154.2 5937, D1 is a source characterized by peripherally located, elongated extended radio emission [arXiv:2106.12281]. Its radio spectral energy distribution is consistent with a radio galaxy, which would be classified as a giant radio galaxy if it resides within the cluster [arXiv:2106.12281].

sources.summary: The paper reports MCXC J0154.2 5937 D1 as elongated peripheral radio emission with a possible WISEA optical host, consistent with a radio galaxy and potentially giant if in the cluster.

quotes.quote: We report peripherally located, elongated extended emission in the cluster, shown in fig:mcxc154:radio . In fig:mcxc154:optical a potential optical host is seen with matching emission in the RACS image (Source A, S_ 887 1 mJy, WISEA J015436.21 593929.9, no redshift). The radio SED of the whole source is uncertain, but consistent with a radio galaxy ( _ 88 ^ 887 = -0.65 0.13 ; fig:sed:mcxcj154 ). If in the cluster, the source is 1 Mpc in projected extent, classing it as a giant radio galaxy.

gmw_00007099.name: FAIRALL 0757
gmw_00007099.overview: FAIRALL 0757 is a head-tail radio galaxy located within the Abell 3164 cluster [arXiv:2106.12281]. It is physically connected to multiple remnant radio components, which likely originated from past episodic activity of the galaxy [arXiv:2106.12281].

sources.summary: The paper associates FAIRALL 0757 with a complex head-tail radio galaxy in Abell 3164, linking multiple remnant components to episodic activity and possible re-acceleration.

quotes.quote: MWA and the VAST data reveal a complex radio galaxy with at least three distinct remnant components (D1--3 in fig:a3164:radio ) with LASs 3.5, 3.8, and 3.1 arcmin and LLSs of 240, 260, and 210 kpc for D1--3, respectively . The head-tail (HT) galaxy (hosted by FAIRALL 0757; Fairall1984 ) in the cluster is clearly connected to D3 and is likely responsible for at least D2, however, it is not clear whether D1 has spawned from the same galaxy. The source-subtracted VAST data is shown in fig:a3164:radio and highlights the steepness of D2.

After subtraction of Sources A and B from the relevant measurements, we obtain spectral indices of D1: _ 88 ^ 887 = -1.48 0.08 ( fig:sed:a3164a ), D2: _ 88 ^ 216 = -2.3 0.1 (though fig:sed:a3164b shows some curvature within the MWA band, and D2 is also fit with a generic curved model between 88--887.5 MHz), D3: _ 154 ^ 887 = -1.78 0.07 ( fig:sed:a3164c ). We do not believe significant flux density is missing, since D1, which is comparative in angular scale, is recovered consistently alongside the MWA. We see steepening from D3--D2, but D1 is flatter, which suggests either (1) it may not be associated with past episode from the HT or (2) it has been re-accelerated/revived by ICM motion.

gmw_00007100.name: WISEA J055346.34 211119.6
gmw_00007100.overview: WISEA J055346.34 211119.6 is a spiral galaxy located near the center of diffuse radio emission associated with Abell 0550 [arXiv:2106.12281]. The galaxy lacks compact radio emission, suggesting that any surrounding lobes are not actively fueled by an active galactic nucleus but are instead related to star-formation processes [arXiv:2106.12281].

sources.summary: The paper notes this spiral galaxy near Abell 0550 diffuse emission, argues any associated lobes are not actively AGN-fueled, and favors star-formation or residual extended emission contributions.

quotes.quote: We note that there is a spiral galaxy (WISEA J055346.34 211119.6, no redshift) near the centre of the emission, shown in fig:a550:optical , denoted with an `S'. Spiral galaxies rarely host large-scale radio lobes, though a small number have been detected, sometimes with remnant lobes e.g. hso+11 and this may be such an example. With no compact emission detected in RACS from the spiral galaxy, we can rule out significant AGN contribution and suggest the emission seen in the NVSS and MWA-2 images is related to star-formation processes and that the galaxy, if the host, is not actively fuelling the lobes, consistent with a steep spectrum.

New kpc-scale jets from the spiral that are not subtracted may contribute to the spectral flattening, though this is unlikely to be so extreme and we suggest residual flux from the extended emission from the spiral is affecting the measurements. Across the MWA band we fit a power law model finding a spectral index of _ 88 ^ 154 = -2.4 0.3 .

gmw_00007101.name: CenA-dE1
gmw_00007101.overview: CenA-dE1 is a dwarf spheroidal galaxy dominated by an old stellar population [arXiv:1103.3706]. Its most recent period of star formation occurred approximately 9 Gyr ago, and it contains a small population of nine luminous asymptotic giant branch candidates [arXiv:1103.3706].

sources.summary: The paper studies CenA-dE1 as an old-population Centaurus A dwarf spheroidal, finding a small luminous AGB population and a most recent star-formation episode about 9 Gyr ago.
