Smoothed Spitzer 3.6 m and 4.5 m band images show perturbation signatures near the western edge of the disk, which are possibly the result of a tidal perturbation of the outer disk within the relaxation time scale, which is assumed to be approximately the time for a single disk rotation T _ rot [ Gyr ] = 6.1478 / black V_ rot , where = the optical radius [ kpc ] and black V_ rot = 0.5 V [ ] / sin(i). , i.e., 0.4 Gyr, based on W _ 20 from Table table2 .

gmw_00007193.name: SDSS J114250.97+202631.8
gmw_00007193.overview: SDSS J114250.97+202631.8 is a late-type galaxy projected 840 kpc from the northwest subcluster core [arXiv:1801.03601]. It features an uncertain neutral hydrogen detection, with its highest column density located approximately 4 kpc northwest of its optical center [arXiv:1801.03601].

sources.summary: The paper notes SDSS J114250.97+202631.8 as an uncertain outer-primary-beam HI detection with an HI maximum offset northwest of the optical centre.

quotes.quote: SDSS J114250.97+202631.8 Figure sdss1 shows the integrated \ map for this galaxy, which is projected 840 kpc from the NW subcluster core. SDSS J114250.97+202631.8 has its highest column density \ ( black 1.2 10 ^ 21 atoms cm ^ 2 ) detected 10.6 2.9 arcsec (4 kpc) NW of the optical centre. As noted in section obs , \ in this galaxy was detected beyond the FWHP of the VLA primary beam and there is no AGES spectrum available for this galaxy so its \ properties are more uncertain than for the other C--array LTGs.

gmw_00007194.overview: The galaxy 091 is characterized by a mostly symmetric disk with a high-column-density center [arXiv:1801.03601]. However, its low-column-density edge is extended to the south and east, and its disk is asymmetrically warped, suggesting a recent weak perturbation [arXiv:1801.03601].

sources.summary: The paper describes 091 as mostly symmetric but with an asymmetric warped HI disk, extended low-column-density gas, and evidence for a recent weak perturbation.

quotes.quote: 091 To a first order 091 presents a symmetric \ disk. The highest column density \ ( black 13 10 ^ 20 atoms cm ^ 2 ) is well aligned in projection with the optical centre, although the low \ column density disk edge extends further to the south and the east than north and west. The closed isovelocity contours in black the velocity field (Figure 97091 - right panel) indicates the \ disk is asymmetrically warped black E and W of the optical centre. Together the \ morphology and kinematics suggest the \ disk has suffered a recent weak perturbation.

gmw_00007195.name: 102N
gmw_00007195.overview: The galaxy 102N is part of an interacting pair and exhibits a highly irregular morphology [arXiv:1801.03601]. Its structure is severely skewed to the south and west, featuring a strongly warped disk and a column density maximum offset from its optical center [arXiv:1801.03601].

sources.summary: The paper describes 102N as an interacting pair member with irregular and skewed HI, an offset column-density maximum, warped velocity field, perturbed molecular disk, and disturbed stellar disk.

quotes.quote: 102N displays a quite irregular \ morphology with the column density maximum (5.1 10 ^ 20 atoms cm ^ 2 ) offset 11.1 2.9 arcsec (4.6 kpc) W of the optical centre and projected near the optical disk edge. black The \ morphology of 102N is severely skewed to the S and W of the optical centre. The velocity field for 102N, right panel of the figure, shows an overall rotating disk pattern. But the changing angle of the isovelocity contours indicates black a strongly warped disk , with the change in angle of the contours being greatest in the \ velocity range of the \ bridge/tail projected against 102S, i.e., V black 6380 \ to black 6460 .

gmw_00007196.name: 102S
gmw_00007196.overview: 102S is an elliptical galaxy that forms a close pair with its companion, 102N [arXiv:1801.03601]. Neutral hydrogen gas is projected against the optical disk of 102S, which likely represents a bridge of material tidally stripped from 102N during their interaction [arXiv:1801.03601].

sources.summary: The paper describes 102S as the companion in a close pair, with HI projected against its optical disk but lacking rotation, supporting a tidal-stripping interpretation from 102N.

quotes.quote: 102 \ is projected against the optical disks of both members of this pair, 102N and 102S (Figure 97102 ). There is an apparent \ bridge/tail joining the pair, although the \ detection in this feature where it is projected against 102S, is only at the 3 level. 102N displays a quite irregular \ morphology with the column density maximum (5.1 10 ^ 20 atoms cm ^ 2 ) offset 11.1 2.9 arcsec (4.6 kpc) W of the optical centre and projected near the optical disk edge. black The \ morphology of 102N is severely skewed to the S and W of the optical centre. The velocity field for 102N, right panel of the figure, shows an overall rotating disk pattern. But the changing angle of the isovelocity contours indicates black a strongly warped disk , with the change in angle of the contours being greatest in the \ velocity range of the \ bridge/tail projected against 102S, i.e., V black 6380 \ to black 6460 . black The \ velocities of the \ projected against the 102S black optical disk do not show a rotation pattern. This, the \ black for 102N and the classification of 102S as an elliptical (NED) suggests the \ in the bridge projected against 102S has been tidally stripped from 102N by an interaction between the pair.

gmw_00007197.overview: 121 is an Sab spiral galaxy with a symmetric outer disk that indicates its old stellar population is unperturbed [arXiv:1801.03601]. However, the galaxy exhibits an ongoing interaction that has significantly perturbed its neutral hydrogen disk, resulting in a gas deficiency and truncated gas distribution [arXiv:1801.03601].

sources.summary: The paper describes 121 as an Sab galaxy with a symmetric old stellar disk but truncated, offset, and perturbed HI plus gas deficiencies consistent with ram-pressure stripping signatures.

quotes.quote: 121 is an Sab galaxy black projected 14.9 arcmin (370 kpc) from the NW subcluster core. Although there is an unusual structure in the inner optical disk, smoothed Spitzer 3.6 m and 4.5 m images black show the morphology of the outer disk to be rather symmetric, indicating the old stellar population there is unperturbed. This galaxy has black an \ deficiency of 0.4 (Table table2 ) with the \ in the SE truncated 30 arcsec (12 kpc) inside the optical black disk. The highest column density \ ( black 6.4 10 ^ 20 atoms cm ^ 2 ) is projected 13.6 arcsec (6 kpc) black north of the optical centre and near the northern edge of the optical disk (Figure 97121 ). The \ velocity field (Figure 97121 , right panel) and black = 1.92 0.08 show the \ kinematics are consistent with an ongoing interaction which is significantly perturbing the \ disk.

gmw_00007198.name: 0129W
gmw_00007198.overview: 0129W is a large barred Sb spiral galaxy with a symmetric old stellar disk and two spiral arms [arXiv:1801.03601]. Also known as NGC 3861, it is classified as a red galaxy with non-continuing star formation and hosts a low-luminosity Seyfert II nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0202431].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 3861 as a red spiral galaxy with a hard X-ray spectrum best explained by an absorbed low-luminosity Seyfert II nucleus.

quotes.quote: NGC 3861 In optical NGC 3861 has a bright nucleus (Fig. 2). It was classified as one of the three ``red galaxies'' by Schommer Bothun (1983) based on the optical colors. These kinds of galaxies were considered to have non-continuing star formation activity.

The spectrum of NGC 3861 and this best-fit model are shown in Fig. 5. The 2 - 10 keV luminosity of the hard component is 3 10 ^ 41 ergs s ^ 1 . This amount of luminosity and absorption, imply that NGC 3861 is a low luminosity Seyfert II galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes 0129W as a large barred Sb spiral with symmetric old stellar arms but offset HI and perturbed, asymmetrically warped HI kinematics.

quotes.quote: 0129W This large black barred Sb spiral has a D _ 25 2 arcmin (50 kpc). black The two impressive spiral arms emanating from the ends of the bar encircle the galaxy (Figure 97129 ). The \ maximum ( black 1.7 10 ^ 21 atoms cm ^ 2 ) is offset 32.4 2.9 arcsec (13 kpc) E of the optical centre. A smaller cluster member 129E is projected at the eastern edge of the optical disk but its optical velocity is 2455 \ higher than 129W. Smoothed Spitzer 3.6 m and 4.5 m images suggest the old stellar disk of 129W, at the radius of the encircling optical arms, is to a first order symmetric.

gmw_00007199.overview: The blue galaxy 138 is viewed at a low inclination angle of approximately 36.3 degrees [arXiv:1801.03601]. It features a truncated neutral hydrogen disk to the southeast, an irregular H-band morphology, and ultraviolet maxima near its optical center and northwest edge [arXiv:1801.03601].

sources.summary: The paper describes 138 as a blue galaxy with truncated southeastern HI, an HI maximum near the northwest optical edge, low-inclination kinematics, UV maxima, and irregular H-band morphology.

quotes.quote: 138 This blue (SDSS g - i = 0.82) galaxy displays a rapid rise in \ column density in the SE where the edge of the \ disk is truncated closer to the optical disk edge compared to the black north and black west . The \ intensity maximum ( black 1.4 10 ^ 21 atoms cm ^ 2 ) is located close to the NW optical disk edge. The small \ W _ 20 value of 64 3 \ and the velocity field isovelocity contours (Figure 97138 ), which shows a systematic gradient in the SE--NW direction, suggest we are viewing the \ disk at low inclination angle (36.3 \ per Hyperleda for the optical disk). There are NUV (GALEX) maxima at the optical centre and 10 arcsec (4 kpc) NW of a bright m _g = 16.4 star projected in front of the optical disk.

gmw_00007200.name: RXJ0911+0551
gmw_00007200.overview: RXJ0911+0551 is an early-type galaxy that acts as a strong gravitational lens with measured time delays [arXiv:1511.01097]. The galaxy is located on the outskirts of a cluster with detected X-ray emission and has a satellite galaxy situated to its north-west [arXiv:1511.01097].

sources.summary: The paper identifies RXJ0911+0551 as an early-type strong lens with measured time delays, located on a cluster outskirts with detected X-ray emission and a nearby satellite galaxy.

quotes.quote: RXJ0911+0551 (hereafter 0911 ) is an early-type galaxy 1997A&A...317L..13B,2012A&A...538A..99S , and has measured time delays 2002ApJ...572L..11H . It lies on the outskirts of a cluster of which X-ray emission can be detected yielding a temperature of 2.3 keV 2001ApJ...555....1M . The center of the cluster lies at a position angle of about -160 ^ . There is furthermore a satellite galaxy to the north-west 2000ApJ...544L..35K .

gmw_00007201.name: Q0957+561
gmw_00007201.overview: Q0957+561 is a cD galaxy situated near the center of a galaxy cluster [arXiv:1511.01097]. This gravitational lens galaxy has a measured time delay and is associated with identified X-ray cluster emission [arXiv:1511.01097].

sources.summary: The paper describes Q0957+561 as a cD lens galaxy near a cluster center, with large image separation, X-ray cluster emission, and measured time delays used in the analysis.

quotes.quote: Q0957+561 (hereafter 0957 ) is a cD galaxy lying close to the centre of a cluster with a high spiral galaxy-fraction e.g. 1992MNRAS.254P..27G,1994A&A...291..411A,1998ApJ...504..661C . Due to the large image separation, large physical scales are probed. Identified X-ray emission furthermore locates the center of the cluster at a position angle of about 60 ^ , close to the lens galaxy 2002ApJ...565...96C . The lens has a measured time delay e.g. 2012A&A...540A.132S .

gmw_00007202.name: NGC 7017
gmw_00007202.overview: NGC 7017 is a bright galaxy pair associated with the Abell 3744 cluster [arXiv:1312.5311]. Both galaxies in the pair host central X-ray point sources and are surrounded by diffuse thermal X-ray emission [arXiv:1312.5311].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 7017 as a bright galaxy pair in Abell 3744, detecting X-ray point sources in both centers and thermal diffuse emission around the pair.

quotes.quote: NGC 7017 There is a bright pair of galaxies associated with \ (Fig.  fig:n7017X-2MASS ), although their velocity difference of 2505 36 km s ^ 1 qr is large. The centers of each were detected as X-ray point sources by wavdetect . We excluded the counts in circles of radii 3 and 1.2 arcsec around the centers of the western and eastern galaxy, respectively, and 176 net counts were then detected from an ellipse of semi-axis lengths of 17.4 and 14.2 arcsec shown in Figure  fig:n7017X-2MASS . A good fit to a thermal model (Table  tab:gspectab ) was found.

gmw_00007203.name: NGC 7018
gmw_00007203.overview: NGC 7018 is a galaxy pair consisting of an eastern and a western member [arXiv:1312.5311]. The eastern member hosts a radio-galaxy nucleus with point-like nuclear X-ray emission, while the western companion galaxy is associated with diffuse X-ray emission and weak radio emission [arXiv:1312.5311].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7018 as a galaxy pair whose eastern member hosts a radio-galaxy nucleus, with nuclear X-ray emission, companion diffuse gas, and weak radio emission from the western galaxy.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: circle of radius 5.6 arcsec centered on 21^ h 07^ m 25^ s . 65, -25^ 25' 43'' . 3

quotes.quote: NGC 7018 is also a galaxy pair. The eastern member hosts the radio-galaxy nucleus, and here point-like nuclear X-ray emission that strongly outshines diffuse gas emission is seen (Fig.  fig:n7018 ). 709 net counts from a circle of radius 5.6 arcsec centered on 21^ h 07^ m 25^ s . 65, -25^ 25' 43'' . 3 can be fitted well to a power-law model with no excess absorption (Table  tab:gspectab ). As for \ the power-law X-ray flux density (Table  tab:gspectab ) and 4.96-GHz core flux density of 36.0 mJy lie within the scatter of the correlation of unabsorbed nuclear emission components of evans , arguing in favor of a common non-thermal origin.

Diffuse X-ray emission, associated largely with the companion galaxy, is seen to the west of the radio nucleus. 385 net counts were extracted from an ellipse of semi-axis lengths 20.6 and 11.7 arcsec, excluding the circle described above and shown in Figure  fig:n7018 . The data give a good fit to a thermal model (Table  tab:gspectab ). We note that there appears to be a weak excess of radio emission associated with the western galaxy.

gmw_00007204.name: host of gamma ray burst GRB050904
aliases_local: host of gamma ray burst GRB050904
gmw_00007204.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 050904 is a primeval star-forming galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z=6.3 [arXiv:astro-ph/0703349, arXiv:1106.4441]. Observations indicate that this high-redshift galaxy has already enriched its interstellar medium with dust [arXiv:1106.4441], which may be primarily produced by supernovae [arXiv:astro-ph/0703349].

sources.summary: The paper finds unusual z=6.29 dust extinction in the GRB 050904 host, matching a high-redshift QSO curve and suggesting early dust dominated by supernova production.

quotes.quote: Galilei, 00040 Frascati, Italy 2 INAF -- Osservatorio Astronomico di Roma, via di Frascati 33, 00040 Monte Porzio Catone, Italy We investigate the dust extinction properties in the host galaxy of GRB 050904 at z=6.29 by analyzing simultaneous broad band observations of the optical and UV afterglow at three different epochs. We show that the peculiar afterglow spectral energy distribution (SED) observed at 0.5 days and at 1 day after the burst (1.6 and 3 hours rest frame) cannot be explained with dust reddening with any of the extinction curves observed at low redshift. Yet, the extinction curve recently inferred for the most distant BAL QSO at z=6.2 nicely reproduces the SED of GRB 050904 at both epochs. Our result provides an additional, independent indication that the properties of dust evolve beyond z 6.

The result obtained in this paper indicate that also in the host of GRB 050904 dust is likely produced mostly by SNe. Altogether, these findings suggest that, more generally, regardless of the specific class of object, at z > 6 SNe are the main source of dust. An independent evidence for different properties of the dust in the host of GRB 050904 comes from the comparison between the large column of absorbing gas observed at early times N _H 8 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 , cusumano07,campana06 and the little associated extinction observed in the optical-UV rest-frame. More specifically, the latter implies an A _V /N _H ratio more than 50 times lower than the Galactic value, which is unprecedented in lower redshift GRBs stratta04,stratta05 .

sources.original_name: host of gamma ray burst GRB050904
sources.summary: The paper reassesses the z=6.3 GRB 050904 host and argues its afterglow requires dust absorption, implying an early star-forming galaxy already enriched its ISM with dust.

quotes.quote: The only non-quasar host galaxy, with modest star formation rate, for which dust extinction has been tentatively detected at these early cosmic epochs, is the host of gamma ray burst GRB050904 at z=6.3 . However, the claim of dust extinction for this GRB has been debated in the past.

The SMC extinction curve and the SN-type one provide good fit to the data at all epochs, with an average amount of dust absorption at _ rest = 3000 of A_ 3000 = 0.25 0.07 mag. These results indicate that the primeval galaxy at z = 6.3 hosting this GRB has already enriched its ISM with dust.

Our findings indicate that the primeval star-forming galaxy at z=6.3 hosting this GRB has already enriched its ISM with dust. The type of extinction/attenuation curve is not well constrained by the data, although we find that only the SMC and SN-type extinction curves provide good fit to the data at all epochs.

gmw_00007205.name: SDSS J0743+4443
gmw_00007205.overview: SDSS J0743+4443 is a galaxy that hosts a weak, flickering active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2201.07250]. While its central region shows evidence of star formation, off-nuclear Seyfert emission is present outside the host galaxy's plane [arXiv:2201.07250].

sources.summary: The paper interprets SDSS J0743+4443 as hosting a weak, flickering AGN whose current X-ray emission and off-plane Seyfert-like optical emission evade central-fiber AGN classification.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0743+4443 This galaxy hosts a weak AGN (Table tbl-5 ), and it also was not classified as an AGN by any other set of observations (Table tbl-6 ). We interpret these results as evidence for a flickering AGN. This explains why the X-ray source is weak, and why the central 3^ spectrum shows evidence of SF but not AGN activity. Further, the BPT map shows off-nuclear Seyfert emission outside of the plane of the host galaxy (Figure fig:maps ), and this optical emission could be a light echo of past AGN activity.

gmw_00007206.name: SDSS J0931+4904
gmw_00007206.overview: SDSS J0931+4904 is classified as a star-forming galaxy by the SDSS collaboration [arXiv:2007.08541] and exhibits a relatively high star formation rate [arXiv:2201.07250]. However, strong X-ray emission and central Seyfert-like line ratios suggest the galaxy may harbor a weak, low-luminosity active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2007.08541, arXiv:2201.07250].

sources.summary: Object 1-155975 is star-forming by SDSS but shows strong X-rays and central Seyfert-like ratios; the authors conclude it may host a low-luminosity AGN.

quotes.quote: 1-155975 - This galaxy is identified as ``Star-forming'' by the SDSS collaboration, but exhibits strong X-ray emission and has Seyfert-like emission line ratios in the central MaNGA pixel. However, this signal is washed out by the 2 92 aperture used to construct the [ S 2 ] /H and [ O 1 ] /H BPT diagrams. Given the conflicting evidence but very strong X-ray emission, we conclude that this object may harbor a low-luminosity AGN.

sources.summary: The paper finds SDSS J0931+4904 hosts a weak AGN, but its relatively high star formation likely dominates the central optical spectrum and produces a star-forming classification.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0931+4904 This galaxy hosts a weak AGN (Table tbl-5 ) and has a relatively high SFR (8 M_ yr ^ 1 ; Table tbl-1 ), which implies that the SF may be dominating over the AGN emission in the central region of the galaxy. This would then explain the SF-like emission line ratios observed in the central 3^ spectrum.

gmw_00007207.name: SDSS J1407+4428
gmw_00007207.overview: SDSS J1407+4428 is a late-stage major merger containing two stellar bulges separated by approximately 8 kpc [arXiv:2201.07250, arXiv:1705.05465]. The system hosts an active galactic nucleus in its eastern nucleus [arXiv:2201.07250, arXiv:1705.05465], while its western nucleus exhibits LINER-like emission attributed to post-AGB stars and shocks [arXiv:2201.07250].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J1407+4428 as a major merger where an eastern-nucleus AGN explains the off-nuclear Seyfert region, while western LINER-like emission reflects stars and shocks.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1407+4428 SDSS J1407+4428 is a major merger with two stellar bulges that have a projected separation of 8 kpc and a line-of-sight velocity difference of 50 km s ^ 1 ( EL17.1 measured 30 km s ^ 1 , while FU18.1 measured 76.8 km s ^ 1 ). Using the MaNGA data, extracted a spectrum from a 2.6 kpc diameter circular aperture centered on each stellar bulge. They classified the spectra using the BPT diagnostic in combination with the WHAN diagram that relates equivalent width and / CI10.3 . They found that the western nuclear spectrum is Seyfert, while the eastern nuclear spectrum is LINER.

For the eastern source, they measured L_ 2-10 keV , unabs =(3.5 0.4) 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 and concluded that it is an AGN. This is similar to our measurement of L_ 2-10 keV , unabs =(5.5 1.1) 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 , which we also used to conclude that the eastern X-ray source is an AGN. For the western source, placed a 3^ aperture on the position of the western galaxy bulge and detected 2 soft (0.3-8 keV) and 10 hard (2-8 keV) X-rays. From this, they estimated a hardness ratio of 0.58 and L_ 2-10 keV , unabs =(5.5 1.1) 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 (assuming a power-law spectrum with =1.8 ). Based on the optical emission line ratio diagnostics, hardness ratio, and X-ray luminosity, they concluded that the western X-ray source is likely an AGN. Our search for a western X-ray source did not return a source detected at >3 above the background (Section chandra ), so we cannot claim a detection of a western X-ray source. In this system, the off-nuclear Seyfert region observed in the MaNGA data is explained by an AGN (the eastern X-ray source) in the nucleus of a companion galaxy, so that the Seyfert emission does indeed spatially coincide with an AGN. The LINER-like emission in the western source is then explained by post-AGB stars and shocks (Figure fig:maps ).

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J140737.17+442856.2 as a late-stage, approximately equal-mass major merger hosting dual AGN, selected by WISE and optical diagnostics and confirmed by Chandra X-ray point sources.

quotes.quote: We identify a single galaxy in the first MaNGA data release that passes the W1-W2>0.8 criterion: SDSS J140737.17+442856.2 (objID 588298663581974759). With a W1-W2=0.84 and W2-W3=2.44 , this source is also located within the 3-colour AGN selection box of Jarrett et al. (2011). Based on visual inspection of the SDSS image (Fig. image , upper panel), SDSS J140737.17+442856.2 appears to be a late stage galaxy merger in which two stellar nuclear are separated by 3 .

We present the discovery of a dual AGN separated by 8.3 kpc in SDSS J140737.17+442856.2, a major merger between two massive galaxies of log (M _ /M _ ), selected by combining a red WISE colour threshold ( W1-W2>0.8 ) and traditional optical emission line diagnostics. This combination of criteria selects a late stage merger which has one nucleus with W1-W2=0.84 (eastern nucleus in Fig image ) and the other nucleus that is classified as an optical (Type II) AGN (western nucleus in Fig image ). The nature of the dual AGN is confirmed by Chandra X-ray observations, the two nuclei exhibiting intrinsic 2-10 keV luminosities of 4 10^ 41 (western nucleus) and 3.5 10^ 43 (eastern nucleus) ; the latter of these has the highest X-ray luminosity measured by Chandra in a confirmed dual AGN to date.

gmw_00007208.name: SDSS J1430+5242
gmw_00007208.overview: SDSS J1430+5242 is a large, isolated barred spiral galaxy located in the Bootes void [arXiv:1510.07411]. It hosts a Seyfert 2 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1510.07411] that exhibits flickering activity [arXiv:2201.07250], a high star formation rate [arXiv:2201.07250], and spatially-extended light echoes [arXiv:2201.07250].

sources.summary: The paper characterizes SBS 1428+529 as a large isolated Bootes void galaxy with a bright bulge, spiral arms, strong bar, Seyfert 2 nucleus, and a weather-limited CO non-detection.

quotes.quote: SBS 1428+529 (IRAS F14288+5255) : This is also a distant galaxy located in the Bootes void. Like CG 598 it is large in size (radius 27^ or 24.6 kpc ) but appears to be isolated. It has a bright bulge, distinct spiral arms and a strong bar. It is the only galaxy in our sample that shows AGN activity; it hosts a Seyfert 2 type nucleus cruzen.etal.2002 .
The non-detection in SBS 1428+529 could be due to the short duration of the scan, which was limited by bad weather.

sources.summary: The paper interprets SDSS J1430+5242 as a flickering AGN with extended Seyfert light echoes, high X-ray luminosity, central obscuration, and star formation dominating the fiber spectrum.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1430+5242 We interpret this galaxy as hosting a flickering AGN. Since the source was not classified as an AGN by any other set of observations (Table tbl-6 ), and the BPT map reveals spatially-extended Seyfert emission with a hole at the middle of the galaxy (Figure fig:maps ), the spatially-extended emission could be an extended light echo of past AGN activity. The X-ray luminosity is also higher than the canonical scaling relation between L_ X,2-10 keV and L_ for AGNs (Section LxLOIII ), which is evidence of new, current AGN activity. The central bulge is obscured ( E(B-V)=0.78 ; the second highest obscuration in our sample), and the SFR is relatively high (12 M_ yr ^ 1 ; Table tbl-1 ), which would then explain why the central 3^ spectrum is dominated by SF.

gmw_00007209.name: SDSS J1518+4244
gmw_00007209.overview: SDSS J1518+4244 is an interacting galaxy pair [arXiv:2205.15335] undergoing a major merger [arXiv:2201.07250]. The system features two stellar bulges with a projected separation of 5.5 kpc and hosts confirmed dual active galactic nuclei [arXiv:2201.07250].

sources.summary: The paper uses MaNGA Plate-IFU 7443-12703 as a validation target, describing it as an interacting pair in MaNGA galaxy-pair and binary-AGN samples and showing reconstructed fluxes match MaNGA DRP channels with identical good-spaxel footprints.

quotes.quote: We carry out this test with MaNGA Plate-IFU 7443-12703 (the same target as from Figure fig:realifs_rss ). This target was chosen for testing arbitrarily from the 127-fibre IFU MaNGA targets and is conveniently a visually stunning interacting pair that belongs to the MaNGA galaxy pair and binary AGN samples of 2018ApJ...856...93F . For our spatial reconstruction of the MaNGA RSS data, we use the associated wavelength-dependent astrometric corrections to the effective fibre positions. These astrometric corrections account for offsets in the effective positions of fibres compared to their intended positions.

The middle row shows the flux in the same channels from the MaNGA LINCUBE for this target. The lower row shows offsets between the and MaNGA DRP flux reconstructions in each channel. reconstructs the specific fluxes for MaNGA Plate-IFU 7443-12703 with an exceptional median precision of f_ 10^ 8 dex in each channel. Additionally, the footprints of good spaxels are identical.

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J1518+4244 as a major merger with dual AGNs; obscuration of the northwest bulge helps make the SDSS fiber spectrum look star-forming.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1518+4244 SDSS J1518+4244 is a major merger with two stellar bulges that have a projected separation of 5.5 kpc and a line-of-sight velocity difference of 91.9 km s ^ 1 FU18.1 . Using the MaNGA data, extracted a spectrum from a 2.6 kpc diameter circular aperture centered on each stellar bulge and classified the spectra using the BPT diagnostic along with the WHAN diagram CI10.3 . They classified both nuclear spectra as starburst-AGN composite, which is different from the Portsmouth BPT classification of the northwestern nuclear spectrum as star-forming. concluded that this system hosts `apparent' binary AGNs, where the nuclear spectra could be explained by merger-driven shocks, a single AGN cross-ionizing its companion galaxy, or two separate AGNs. Here, we use Chandra observations to confirm that there are indeed dual AGNs in this galaxy merger (Table tbl-5 ).

The 3^ SDSS fiber is centered on the northwest AGN, and that stellar bulge is obscured ( E(B-V)=0.81 ; the highest obscuration in our sample), which helps explain why that fiber spectrum is dominated by SF-like emission line ratios.

gmw_00007210.name: SDSS J1630+2612
gmw_00007210.overview: SDSS J1630+2612 is a galaxy that may host a flickering active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2201.07250] with a central region dominated by intermediate-age stars [arXiv:1709.10086]. The system exhibits a relatively high star formation rate [arXiv:2201.07250], including at least one star formation episode within the last 10 Myr [arXiv:1709.10086].

sources.summary: The paper uses mangaID 1-269632 as a strong AGN example; its inner 3.7 kpc is dominated by intermediate-age stars, with old and young components indicating star formation within the last 10 Myr.

quotes.quote: Figure synthesis_exe presents an example of the results of the stellar population synthesis for the strong AGN mangaID 1-269632. The upper panels show the observed and synthetic spectrum as well as the corresponding residuals; the bottom panels display the resulting light (percent contribution of each age bin above to the light at 5700 ) and mass contributions of single and binned SSPs vectors. Also shown are the mean ages and metallicities. The inner 3.7 kpc of this galaxy is dominated by the contribution of intermediate age stars ( x_ ii 38 and x_ io = 19 ) with old stars contributing x_ o 9 and young stars x_ yy 19 , which implies that at least one episode of star formation has occurred in the last 10 Myr.

sources.summary: The paper argues SDSS J1630+2612 may host a flickering AGN that shut off, left an optical light echo, and then turned back on in X-rays amid high star formation.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1630+2612 This galaxy's BPT map shows off-nuclear Seyfert emission outside of the plane of the host galaxy (Figure fig:maps ), and the AGN has an X-ray luminosity that is higher than the canonical scaling relation between L_ X,2-10 keV and L_ for AGNs (Section LxLOIII ). Consequently, this galaxy could host a flickering AGN that shut off in the past and left a light echo behind, and then turned on again and produced the X-ray emission. The SFR is relatively high (17 M_ yr ^ 1 ; Table tbl-1 ), which would explain the SF classification of the central 3^ spectrum.

gmw_00007211.name: J234201.40 + 010629.2
gmw_00007211.overview: J234201.40 + 010629.2 is a non-active host galaxy of a tidal disruption event [arXiv:1009.1627, arXiv:1706.08965]. The galaxy exhibits Balmer and metal absorption features with negligible star formation [arXiv:1009.1627], and its nucleus has a measured velocity dispersion of 126 km/s [arXiv:1706.08965].

sources.summary: The paper identifies this TDE1 host as a non-active galaxy with Balmer and metal absorption, negligible star formation, unusually blue UV emission, and a constrained black-hole mass.

quotes.quote: We observed the host galaxy of TDE1 on 2009 November 7 with the MagE spectrograph on the Magellan II Clay telescope with a spectral resolution of R=4100 or 1.6 . The spectrum, shown in Fig. fig:obs_spec , shows Balmer absorption features, as well as Mg Ib and NaI absorption, which yields z=0.136 0.001 . No emission lines are detected: L_ H < 4.4 10^ 37 erg s ^ 1 and L_ [ OIII ] <1.3 10^ 38 erg s ^ 1 .

Star formation contributes little to the observed UV in TDE1. This follows from the L_ H - L_ FUV relation Kennicutt98 which predicts that if the FUV luminosity observed by GALEX 800 days after the optical flare were due to star formation, the H luminosity should be 2.4 10^ 40 erg s ^ 1 - two orders of magnitude above the observed upper limit. The contribution of stellar sources that evade the correlation between FUV and H (e.g., blue horizontal branch stars) is constrained by using the color-magnitude relation observed by Haines08 for galaxies that have been spectroscopically identified as passively evolving. At the luminosity of the host of TDE1 ( M_r=-19.85 ), these galaxies are observed at NUV = 5.4 . The scatter in this relation, _ NUV = 0.37 , can be attributed to different amounts of residual star formation.

sources.summary: For the TDE1 host, the paper finds a well-defined WHT velocity dispersion near 126 km/s, smaller than an earlier 137 km/s literature value, and an Eddington ratio of 0.02 among high-mass black-hole events.

quotes.quote: As an illustration, we show the result of the template fitting procedure in Figure fig:14li using the WHT spectrum of TDE1. Overlaid in red is the best-fitting template spectrum broadened to 126 km s ^ 1 . The residuals are shown in green, while blue regions are excluded in the fitting process. The velocity dispersion is well defined and the fit describes the data well, leaving little structure in the residuals.
For TDE1 these authors find = 137 12 , while we find a slightly smaller value of = 126 7 .

The peak luminosity of all TDEs is consistent with being at the Eddington limit except for the two events with the highest black hole masses, which have Eddington ratios of 0.02 for TDE1 and 0.07 for D3--13.

gmw_00007212.name: J232348.61 010810.3
gmw_00007212.overview: The galaxy J232348.61 010810.3 is the host galaxy of the tidal disruption event TDE2 [arXiv:1009.1627]. It has been observed in both pre-flare and post-flare states using GALEX ultraviolet imaging and the William Herschel Telescope [arXiv:1009.1627].

sources.summary: The paper discusses this TDE2 host through GALEX, WHT, and NTT data, measuring UV fluxes, emission-line luminosity, redshift, and a central flare component.

quotes.quote: We have GALEX NUV and FUV detections of the TDE2 host in both the pre-flare (2003 24 August) and post-flare (2008 14 October) state. The TDE2 host was also observed by GALEX on 2008 30 August, but the image was at the edge of the field and the sky was noisy, so we display the NUV value in Fig. fig:lc b) but do not use it further. Figure fig:GALEX-stamp shows the GALEX images.

In November 2010, 850 days after the first SDSS detection of the flare, the host galaxy of TDE2 was observed with the WHT for two nights. On 01 November two ACAM exposures of 900s were taken using a V400 grating and a slit width of 1" . On 05 November, the host was observed again using the same specifications and exposure times, except that a slit width of 1 5 was used. The data of both nights (1h total integration time) was reduced separately, using standard IRAF IRAF is distributed by NOAO, which is operated by AURA, under cooperative agreement with NSF routines, to yield four 1D spectra which were then combined. The overall flux normalization was obtained using a standard reference star; the wavelength dependent flux normalization was fixed using the SDSS PSF flux of the host.

gmw_00007213.name: CFRS 22.1313
gmw_00007213.overview: CFRS 22.1313 is a disk galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.819 [arXiv:astro-ph/0408465]. It features two distinct hydrogen-alpha emitting regions with a velocity separation of 220 km/s and has a rotation velocity of at least 180 km/s [arXiv:astro-ph/0408465].

sources.summary: The paper maps H emission in CFRS 22.1313 at z=0.819, finding two emitting regions, a high lower-limit rotation velocity, and little Tully-Fisher brightening at z=0.8.

quotes.quote: Using the CIRPASS spectrograph on Gemini-South we map the spatial and velocity distribution of H emission in the z=0.819 galaxy CFRS 22.1313. We detect two H emitting regions with a velocity separation of 220 10 km s ^ 1 . Combining the 2D map of H emission with HST F814W imaging, we determine a lower limit of 180 20 km s ^ 1 for the rotation velocity of this M_B (rest) 21 galaxy. We note that our value is significantly higher than the rotation velocity of 120 10km s ^ 1 reported by Barden et al. (2003) for their long-slit spectroscopic study of this galaxy. Our lower limit on the rotation velocity is entirely consistent with no evolution of the rest B band Tully-Fisher relation.

gmw_00007214.name: UGC 6100
gmw_00007214.overview: UGC 6100 is a Seyfert 2 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:0907.0180] that exhibits strong nuclear emission [arXiv:0712.0352, arXiv:0907.0180]. X-ray observations indicate that the galaxy's nucleus is heavily obscured by a very high absorbing column density [arXiv:0712.0352, arXiv:2007.02616].

sources.summary: The paper says UGC 6100's XMM-Newton data were background affected, but its low hard-X-ray flux, small T ratio, and strong [O III] imply Compton-thick nuclear obscuration.

quotes.quote: UGC 6100: The XMM-Newton observation of UGC 6100 have been strongly affected by bright background. The poor signal-to-noise prevented us from attempting detailed spectral fits. Here we give only a very rough, approximate description of the 0.5 -- 10 keV spectrum in terms of a single power- law with photon index fixed at 1.8. The resulting 2 -- 10 keV flux is 5.0 10 ^ 14 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 and T ratio is 0.05 ( < 0.1), indicating Compton-thick obscuration (Guainazzi et al. 2005). Note that UGC 6100 has the highest [ O iii ] luminosity in the present sample, which is related to strong AGN emission.

The observed low X-ray flux and thus small T ratio can only be attributed to heavily nuclear obscuration. We regard it as a Compton-thick one in this paper and give a a lower limit of 10 ^ 24 cm ^ 2 to N _ H . The observed 2 -- 10 keV luminosity is 1.0 10 ^ 41 erg s ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper finds UGC 6100 needs modest ionized-gas subtraction, retains nuclear emission from another mechanism, and has residual light probably explained by stellar emission and color gradients.

quotes.quote: UGC 6100 (Sy2) A range of f between 0.2 and 0.3 removes what seems to be the ionised gas emission. A higher fraction starts to leave patchy negative residuals near the nucleus. There is an important nuclear contribution which can be attributed to a mechanism different from the NC or [ NeV ] line emission, unless there is a strong variation in the value of f in these small scales, due to a varying ionisation parameter and/or thick obscuring patches. The photometric analysis suggests that the remaining light may probably be explained by stellar emission. As can be appreciated in the colour profiles of Fig. fig:U-V , there is a constant U--V colour gradient of -0.12 mag/100 pc extending out more than 1.5 kpc from the nucleus.

sources.summary: The paper notes UGC 6100 was undetected by XMM-Newton but detected by NuSTAR, with fits indicating a very high absorbing column density.

quotes.quote: UGC 6100: The source was observed but not detected by . However, it was detected by . We fitted the FPMA/FPMB spectra using an absorbed power law plus Pexmon, with the reflection fraction fixed at unity. The power law photon index was not constrained, so we fixed it to 1.8 and 2.2. Both values result in good fits and consistent results for the absorbing column density being very high.

gmw_00007215.name: NGC 3508
gmw_00007215.overview: NGC 3508 is a spiral galaxy classified as a luminous infrared galaxy [arXiv:2207.08929]. Its radio spectrum is characterized entirely by synchrotron emission, and it hosts a modest active galactic nucleus fraction of 6% [arXiv:2207.08929].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 3508 as a spiral LIRG whose densely sampled radio spectrum is fit by a synchrotron-only single component, with zero thermal fraction and modest CIGALE AGN fraction.

quotes.quote: NGC 3508 : classified as a spiral galaxy Veilleux95 , its densely sampled radio spectrum covering 2.9 decades in frequency (70 MHz-3 GHz) is fitted with a single component model characterized by synchrotron emission only. The synchrotron spectral index, = 0.73. The TF is 0 as this emission is characterized by synchrotron emission (i.e. no free-free component). The CIGALE modeling gives SFR _ IR = 3.90 M_ yr ^ 1 , L_ dust = 10 ^ 11.25 L_ , and AGN fraction = 6.00 , respectively, for this galaxy.

gmw_00007216.name: ESO 440-IG058
gmw_00007216.overview: ESO 440-IG058 is a closely interacting pair of galaxies undergoing a merger [arXiv:1509.08468, arXiv:2207.08929]. The system features a northern neighbor with a LINER-type active galactic nucleus and a southern system characterized by shock-dominated emission and ongoing star formation [arXiv:1509.08468, arXiv:2207.08929].

sources.summary: The paper describes this close pair as poorly studied but tidally disturbed, with IFS evidence for star formation plus shocked gas and possible outflow geometry.

quotes.quote: IRAS F12043-3140 (ESO 440-IG058) This pair of closely interacting galaxies is poorly studied and there exists no higher spatial resolution imagery to examine. The DSS image, though, shows tidal features indicative of the ongoing interaction. Our IFS data indicate a combination of ongoing star formation and shocked gas, seen in the diagnostic diagrams as region + composite spaxels and with a tail out to 200 in the velocity dispersion distribution. Our map shows the enhanced ratios seem to form a cone extending to the north and south of the southern galaxy, and agrees with the IFU observation of Monreal10 . The overlap in the two galaxies and the lower S/N of our data make it difficult to discern if this is indeed associated with an outflow from the southern galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes ESO 440-IG058 as a merger with a LINER-type northern neighbor and shock-dominated southern system, modeling its absorbed star-forming radio SED and infrared properties.

quotes.quote: ESO 440-IG058 : classified as a galaxy merger with a LINER-type AGN northern neighbor Corbett03 , this galaxy is known to show shock-dominated emission for the southern system Zaurin11 . Its radio SED covering 2.1 decades (70 MHz-10.0 GHz) is fitted with single-component star-forming region properties (including absorption) with a turnover at 140 MHz. Synchrotron spectral index, = 0.82. The computed TF is 3.17 . The CIGALE-estimated SFR _ IR , L_ dust , AGN fraction are 42.66 M_ yr ^ 1 , 10 ^ 10.99 L_ , 4.53 , respectively.

gmw_00007217.name: IC 4280
gmw_00007217.overview: IC 4280 is a spiral galaxy [arXiv:2207.08929] classified as a luminous infrared galaxy [arXiv:2107.12529, arXiv:2207.08929]. Its star-forming region exhibits a radio spectral turnover at 140 MHz [arXiv:2207.08929], and observations have successfully detected both CO and carbon lines within the galaxy [arXiv:2107.12529].

sources.summary: The survey detects CO and [C I] in IC 4280, but finds unclear continuum recovery: line fluxes show little missing flux while continuum flux appears strongly resolved out.

quotes.quote: IC 4280 Both CO (4--3) and [ C 1 ] (1--0) lines were detected. The continuum associated with [ C 1 ] (1--0) observation is detected, and the `` imfit " shows that spatially extended structure (the FWHM of 8 3 5 4 ) with the continuum flux density of 152 57 mJy. Because the extended structure is marginal (S/N 3), this flux density may be the upper limit of the continuum flux density. However, the continuum emission associated with CO (4--3) emission was not detected, and the upper limit was <33 mJy.

For this galaxy, the missing flux is difficult to determine. For example, S_ CO(4-3) v=361 74 Jy km s ^ 1 for ACA and S_ CO(4-3) v= [ 531,1010 ] Jy km s ^ 1 for SPIRE and S_ CI (1-0) v=166 34 Jy km s ^ 1 for ACA and S_ CI (1-0) v= [ 69,406 ] Jy km s ^ 1 for SPIRE. This means that the line missions do not have a significant missing flux. However, in the case of continuum emission, S_ 650 = 152 57 mJy for ACA but S_ 650 = [ 514, 566 ] mJy from SED fitting, suggesting that RF is approximately 30 .

sources.summary: The paper classifies IC 4280 as a spiral galaxy and fits its radio SED with an absorbed single star-forming component, deriving a 140 MHz turnover, synchrotron index, thermal fraction, SFR, dust luminosity, and AGN fraction.

quotes.quote: IC 4280 : classified as a spiral galaxy Fairall89, Jin19 , its radio SED covering 1.6 decades (70 MHz-3.0 GHz) is best-fitted with a single-component star-forming region properties (including absorption) with a turnover at 140 MHz. The synchrotron spectral index, = 0.9. The TF is 11.9 . The CIGALE-estimated SFR, L_ dust , AGN fraction are 11.75 M_ yr ^ 1 , 10 ^ 11.36 L_ , 6 , respectively.

gmw_00007218.name: ESO 453-G005
gmw_00007218.overview: ESO 453-G005 is a widely separated galaxy pair dominated by star-forming activity [arXiv:1509.08468, arXiv:2207.08929]. Despite being a pair, the galaxies show no apparent signs of interaction in available imaging [arXiv:1509.08468, arXiv:2207.08929].

sources.summary: The paper describes the widely separated pair as star-formation dominated, with low velocity dispersions, no shock component, and no apparent interaction signs in available imaging.

quotes.quote: IRAS F16443-2915 N S (ESO 453-G005) This widely separated pair of galaxies appear to be completely dominated by star formation. The emission line ratio maps are dominated by emission and the velocity dispersions in both systems are primarily a few tens of . The southern galaxy does show a larger turbulent component approaching 100 , but neither indicates any shock component. These galaxies show no apparent signs of interaction, though the available imaging data are low resolution.

sources.summary: The paper describes ESO 453-G005 as a star-formation-dominated galaxy pair without interaction signs, fitting its sparse radio SED with a single no-turnover star-forming component.

quotes.quote: ESO 453-G005 : a galaxy pair that appears to be dominated by star-forming activity, although it does not show signs of interaction Rich15 , its poorly sampled radio SED covering 1.3 decades in frequency (150 MHz-3.0 GHz) is best-fitted with emission from a single star-forming region without a low-frequency turnover. The synchrotron spectral index is 0.6. The TF is 2 . The CIGALE-estimated SFR, L_ dust , AGN fraction are 3.31 M_ yr ^ 1 , 10 ^ 10.97 L_ , 4.7 , respectively.

gmw_00007219.name: host galaxy of GRB 190829A
gmw_00007219.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 190829A, also identified as SDSS J025810.28-085719.2 [arXiv:2004.01538, arXiv:2106.07169], is a massive, dusty galaxy with a high star-formation rate [arXiv:2205.13940]. Located at a redshift of z = 0.0785 [arXiv:2106.07169], it exhibits radio emission [arXiv:2004.01538] but lacks compact central radio emission [arXiv:2106.07169].

sources.summary: SDSS J025810.28-085719.2 is identified as the GRB 190829A host; MeerKAT and AMI-LA data detect its radio emission, estimate a spectral index/flux, and show it dominates the late AMI plateau.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:comparison shows a point source from the MeerKAT image with coordinates consistent with those reported by XRT, i.e. the GRB, which is labelled `a', with a second source, labelled `b' with an arrow, 10" away, in black contours. The second source is identified as the host galaxy reported in NOT_GCN , a 2MASS source: SDSS J025810.28-085719.2. No counterpart is found in the NRAO VLA Sky Survey (NVSS) catalogue.

The plateau component, most likely, can be attributed to the host galaxy because of the second component seen in the MeerKAT field within the AMI-LA beam in Figure fig:comparison . The galaxy component has an 1.0-1.5 GHz in-band spectral index of _ 1.0-1.5 = 0.5 0.7 . Extrapolating to 15.5 GHz, the flux density of the source would be 10^ (3.6 1.6) Jy/beam. The flattening of the light curve seen from around day 70 has a flux level within the uncertainties of the predicted host galaxy flux at 15.5 GHz.

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J025810.28 085719.2 as GRB 190829A's host, gives its redshift, places the burst about 14.7 kpc from its center, and reports no compact central radio emission.

quotes.quote: The redshift of the host galaxy z = 0.0785 ( Valeev2019 , corresponding to a luminosity distance of approximately 368 Mpc adopting Planck cosmological parameters -- Planck2016 - or equivalently an angular diameter distance of 316 Mpc) makes this event one of the closest long GRBs known to date.

This could be tentatively explained by the large offset of the GRB location with respect to the host galaxy centre. Indeed, using the GRB coordinates derived from our VLBI observations and the host galaxy centre position from the 2MASS catalogue Skrutskie2006 , we measure a separation of 9.6 arcsec, corresponding to a physical projected separation of 14.7 kiloparsec. This is comparable to the largest previously measured offset in long GRBs that of GRB 080928 Blanchard2016 , placing it in principle in the underdense outskirts of its host galaxy.

As a side note, we have searched for compact radio components in the central region ( 2.46 2.46  arsec ^2 ) of the host galaxy Heintz2019,Rhodes2020 SDSS J025810.28 085719.2 with the wide field imaging function provided by the AIPS task IMAGR . We find no compact radio emission with a brightness 0.056  mJy beam ^ 1 ( 6 ) at 5 GHz.

sources.summary: The paper identifies the GRB 190829A host as a bright SDSS galaxy, detects it in DOT imaging, and models it as massive, dusty, and actively star-forming.

quotes.quote: The associated host galaxy of GRB 190829A is a significantly bright SDSS galaxy (SDSS J025810.28-085719.2). We observed this galaxy using 3.6m DOT in B, R, and I filters on 03 ^ rd October 2020. The host galaxy is clearly detected in each filter of our observations.
The SED modeling indicates that the host of GRB 190829A is a massive galaxy with a very high star-formation rate.
We find that GRB 190829A has a considerable amount of dust and gas in the local environment of its host galaxy.

gmw_00007220.name: host galaxy of GRB 200826A
gmw_00007220.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 200826A has been observed in the optical and near-infrared bands [arXiv:2205.13940]. It is detected in the I band, and its physical parameters are typical of long gamma-ray burst host galaxies [arXiv:2205.13940].

sources.summary: The paper reports optical-NIR DOT/TANSPEC observations of the GRB 200826A host, detects it in I band, and models it as typical of long-GRB hosts.

quotes.quote: We have obtained the optical and NIR photometric data of the host galaxy of GRB 200826A using the TIFR-ARIES Near-Infrared Spectrometer TANSPEC; 2018BSRSL..87...58O mounted on the 3.6m DOT, Nainital, India during the nights of 2020 November. TANSPEC is a unique instrument that provides simultaneous wavelength coverage from 0.5-2.5 m in imaging and spectroscopic modes. We have given exposures of one hour, 35 minutes, 35 minutes in I (4 ^ th November 2020), J (4 ^ th November 2020), K (11 ^ th November 2020) bands, respectively.
The host galaxy is detected in the I band as 22.71 0.10 mag, and there was an upper limit of J > 20.56 mag and K > 19.55 mag.
These parameters are typical to those observed for long GRBs host galaxies and consistent with .

gmw_00007221.name: VV 114 E
gmw_00007221.overview: VV 114 E is the dust-obscured eastern component of a merging galaxy system [arXiv:2312.04914]. It features a 4 kpc-long dense molecular filament with ongoing star formation [arXiv:2312.04914], a clumpy nucleus [arXiv:2312.04914], and spatially extended 18-micron emission [arXiv:1102.4854].

sources.summary: The paper describes VV 114 E as the dust-obscured eastern component of a luminous merger, with filamentary molecular emission, clumpy nucleus, debated AGN activity, and an unusual SW-s2 excitation source.

quotes.quote: While the western galaxy is bright in the UV gol02 and optical kno94 showing low visual extinction, a large concentration of dust obscures the eastern component (VV 114 E), which dominates the mid-IR emission. VV 114 E has been imaged in several molecular lines at millimeter wavelengths e.g. ion13,sai15,sai17 , showing enhanced emission along a narrow, 4 kpc-long dense filament in the east-west direction (including an overlap region between the two galaxies, see also Fig. maps a,b), in which Pa emission indicates ongoing star formation tat12,ion13 . This filament, which is resolved into dense clumps of several 10^6 M_ , suggests widespread shocks triggered by the dynamic interaction of the merging disk galaxies. The nucleus of VV 114 E is located at the easternmost extreme edge of the filament, and is also resolved into several massive clumps. JWST /MIRI photometry of up to 40 star-forming knots has been reported by . The possible presence of an AGN in VV 114 E has been debated for years.

Both maps clearly delineate the elongated filament and peak at the nuclear region of VV 114 E. The IR images of this nucleus, enlarged in Fig. maps f, show three main clumps, which will be denoted as NE, SW-s1, and SW-s2 (panel e).

We found a source of unusual CO and H _2 O excitation in the SW-s2 core, located just at the head of the 4 kpc filament (magenta circle in Fig. maps f). The JWST 2-10 m spectrum extracted from a 0.2'' aperture at the position of the maximum band strength is shown in Fig. maps g, where the spectral extent of the CO and H _2 O bands is indicated.

sources.summary: The paper finds VV 114 E has extended 18 micron structure, compact knots, weak silicate absorption, and likely diffuse emission missed by ground-based imaging.

quotes.quote: For LIRGs, only VV 114 E, ESO 602 G025, NGC 4945, NGC 1614, and Arp 193 display clearly detectable spatially extended structures. For VV 114 E, the two faint knots below the E-2 nucleus in Figure 1 were not recognizable in the low spatial resolution 15 m image taken with ISO , but similar structures were seen at 1.1 m lef02 , suggesting that they are starburst knots situated between the VV 114 E and W merging nuclei. IRAS 15250+3609, IRAS 20511 4250, and NGC 1377 show clearly detectable 18 m silicate dust absorption features, while the feature is weak in VV 114 E.

Given the 20 flux uncertainties of Spitzer spectra (Spitzer Infrared Spectrograph Data Handbook, version 1.0), we see significantly ( >> 20 ) smaller fluxes in our measurements only in VV 114 E and NGC 4945, both of which clearly display spatially extended emission structures. For VV 114 E, the sum of the 18 m fluxes for the compact components (E-1 and E-2) measured with our ground-based observations are > 30 lower than the Spitzer IRS measurement. Thus, a substantial amount of diffuse, faint emission component may be present in VV 114 E.

gmw_00007222.name: J0733+2721
gmw_00007222.overview: J0733+2721 is a core-dominated double radio source classified as a quasar [arXiv:1406.4797]. It is separated by approximately 10 arcseconds from an unrelated triple source that appears to be a foreground radio galaxy [arXiv:1406.4797].

sources.summary: The note describes this quasar as a core-dominated double near an unrelated foreground-looking triple source also visible in FIRST.

quotes.quote: J0733+2721: This source is a core dominated double separated by roughly 10 arcseconds from an unrelated triple source identified only as SDSS J073321.12+272054.4. NED lists a g = 22.7 magnitude for it but gives no other information. It has the appearance of a foreground radio galaxy. Both sources are visible in the FIRST image cutout.

gmw_00007223.name: J0833+3531
gmw_00007223.overview: J0833+3531 is a radio-loud quasar [arXiv:1406.4797]. It is likely a triple source that exhibits a complex extended structure [arXiv:1406.4797].

sources.summary: The note says this source is difficult to classify from a single low-resolution image, but is probably a complex triple.

quotes.quote: J0833+3531: This source is challenging to describe due to the single low resolution image available. It is probably a triple source with complex extended structure. Recent JVLA observations, currently being reduced, will clarify its structure.

gmw_00007224.name: J1016+2037
gmw_00007224.overview: J1016+2037 is a radio-loud quasar classified as a very compact double source [arXiv:2102.12609, arXiv:1406.4797]. It features a broad, 15-milliarcsecond radio jet [arXiv:1406.4797] and real, extended X-ray emission that is misaligned from its radio structure [arXiv:2102.12609].

sources.summary: The paper finds J1016 + 2037 has real extended X-ray emission misaligned from its radio feature; chance alignment and Chandra PSF artifacts are unlikely.

quotes.quote: However, J1016 + 2037 stands out amongst our sample due to its non-coincident X-ray and radio features, where the measured offset between the X-ray emission and radio feature centroid is 195^ . This offset is despite the excellent alignment of the X-rays and radio from its quasar core. Furthermore, the detection probability for the extended X-rays from J1016 is amongst the highest rated for our sample (Table table:extend ), suggesting that this feature is indeed real.

We began by verifying that the known \ PSF artifact (see Section sect:observation ) was not aligned with the observed X-ray feature in J1016, confirming that the extended X-rays are not due to a systematic effect from . We then investigated the possibility of an unassociated X-ray source being the origin of the extended X-rays observed in J1016. Our measured X-ray flux for the extended source is 10^ 14 erg cm^ 2 s^ 1 (Table table:flux ), and there are 100 X-ray sources deg ^ 2 above such a flux limit Civano2016 . As a result, there is only a 1 probability of a chance association within 5 \ of any quasar in our sample. We also confirmed that the X-ray feature is not coincident with any optical source in the Pan-STARRS 3Pi survey to a minimum brightness limit of 21.3 mag in the grizy bands Tonry2012 .

sources.summary: The note describes a compact double whose southern extended flux appears at 8 GHz and whose VIPS image shows a broad milliarcsecond jet.

quotes.quote: J1016+2037: This is a very compact double source. The southern extended flux is also visible at 8 GHz. The VIPS image shows a broad, 15 mas jet in position angle 30^ .

gmw_00007225.name: J1036+1326
gmw_00007225.overview: The radio-loud quasar J1036+1326 is a nearly collinear triple source that displays extended structure [arXiv:1406.4797]. High-resolution observations of the object reveal an unresolved core, along with a possible radio jet extending to the northwest [arXiv:1406.4797].

sources.summary: The note describes a nearly collinear triple with extended structure in all data sets, a possible northwest jet, and an unresolved EVN core.

quotes.quote: J1036+1326: This nearly collinear triple shows extended structure in all data sets. There is a hint of a jet to the NW in the 8 GHz image. EVN observations at 1.6 GHz show an unresolved core .

gmw_00007226.name: J1127+5650
gmw_00007226.overview: J1127+5650 is a core-dominated compact double radio source [arXiv:1406.4797]. Its detailed structure is difficult to discern due to an elongated point spread function, though it exhibits diffuse emission to the northeast [arXiv:1406.4797].

sources.summary: The note describes a core-dominated compact double whose detailed structure is hard to see, while VIPS shows diffuse northeast emission.

quotes.quote: J1127+5650: This is a core-dominated compact double source. The elongated point spread function makes it difficult to discern detail. The VIPS image shows diffuse structure to the north-east.

gmw_00007227.name: J1204+5228
gmw_00007227.overview: J1204+5228 is a high-redshift source classified as a quasar due to broad emission lines in its spectrum [arXiv:1406.4797]. It features a central radio core aligned with its optical position, a northward jet, and a southwest region of diffuse flux [arXiv:1406.4797].

sources.summary: The note classifies this broad-line source as a very asymmetric triple with a central VLBI core, northward jet, and unexplained southwest diffuse flux.

quotes.quote: J1204+5228: This is an interesting source. Some authors have classified it as a high redshift radio galaxy, but the SDSS spectrum shows broad lines of C III, C IV and Ly . Pentericci et al (2000) suggest that the brightest component, seen at the center of our image, is a hotspot despite its flat spectrum. However, the SDSS position is coincident with this component, as is also the VLBI core observed by Helmboldt et al. (2007) in the VIPS survey.

gmw_00007228.name: J1217+5835
gmw_00007228.overview: J1217+5835 is a highly core-dominated radio source, suggesting it is aligned at a small angle to the line of sight [arXiv:1406.4797]. It features a jet that makes an almost 90-degree bend away from a probable counter hotspot [arXiv:1406.4797].

sources.summary: The note describes an unusual highly bent triple with one-sided extended structure, a near-90-degree jet bend, and strong core dominance.

quotes.quote: J1217+5835: We have classified this unusual source as a highly bent triple, even though all the extended structure is seen on only one side of the core. The source appears to consist of a core with a jet that makes an almost 90 degree bend away from what is probably a counter hotspot with a spectral index 0.4 . The VIPS image shows a 25 mas jet in position angle 135^ .This is one of the most core-dominated sources in the sample, indicating a small angle to the line of sight, and consistent with exaggerated projection effects.

gmw_00007229.name: J1242+3720
gmw_00007229.overview: J1242+3720 is a radio-loud quasar that exhibits a slightly bent triple morphology [arXiv:1406.4797]. Its northern hotspot is not visible at 5 GHz, and its radio core appears almost unresolved [arXiv:1406.4797].

sources.summary: The note describes a slightly bent triple whose northern hotspot disappears at 5 GHz and whose 8 GHz image lacks extended emission.

quotes.quote: J1242+3720: This is a slightly bent triple source. The northern hot spot is not visible at 5 GHz, and no extended emission is detected at 8 GHz. The VIPS image shows an almost unresolved core.

gmw_00007230.name: J1346+2900
gmw_00007230.overview: J1346+2900 is a core-dominated compact double radio source [arXiv:1406.4797]. At 1.4 GHz, the galaxy displays faint emission close to the core on the northeast side, while only the core is visible at 8 GHz [arXiv:1406.4797].

sources.summary: The note describes a very core-dominated compact double at 5 GHz, with faint northeast 1.4 GHz emission and only the core seen at 8 GHz.

quotes.quote: J1346+2900: The 5 GHz image shows a very core-dominated compact double source. At 1.4 GHz there is faint emission close to the core on the NE side. At 8 GHz only the core is visible.

gmw_00007231.name: J1356+2918
gmw_00007231.overview: J1356+2918 is a triple radio source that exhibits a short jet pointing toward the northeast at 8.44 GHz [arXiv:1406.4797]. At 1.4 GHz, the source displays trailing diffuse extended structure to the west that is completely resolved out at 8 GHz [arXiv:1406.4797].

sources.summary: The note describes an unusual triple with a short northeast jet and westward diffuse 1.4 GHz structure resolved out at 8 GHz.

quotes.quote: J1356+2918: This unusual triple source shows a short jet pointing to the NE at 8.44 GHz. The 1.4 GHz image shows trailing diffuse extended structure to the W that is completely resolved out in the 8 GHz image. Unfortunately there are no 5 GHz data available. The morphology of this source is reminiscent of that of J1204+5228.

gmw_00007232.name: J1405+0415
gmw_00007232.overview: J1405+0415 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar at a redshift of approximately 3.2 [arXiv:astro-ph/0611425, arXiv:1202.2193]. It features a core-dominated double structure with a strongly curving radio jet [arXiv:1406.4797] and an extended X-ray jet [arXiv:1901.00057].

sources.summary: The paper presents VSOP/VLBA observations of PKS 1402+044, a z=3.215 flat-spectrum radio quasar, describing secondary and extended emission, a compact core, and parsec-scale core-jet morphology.

quotes.quote: PKS 1402+044 (J1405+0415) is a flat-spectrum radio source from the Parkes 2.7-GHz survey (0.58 Jy). Optically it is a 19.6-magnitude stellar object with the redshift z=3.215 . The MERLIN observations at 1.6 GHz indicated that there is a secondary component at a separation of 0.8^ to the south-west at the position angle of 123^ and a faint extended emission at 3.3^ at the position angle of 106^ . VLBI observations at 5 GHz found that the main component consists of a compact core and a resolved jet extending to 18 mas to the west.

Figure fig1 displays a clear core--jet morphology of the quasar PKS 1402+044. The naturally weighted VLBA image detects the weak emission extending up to 150 mas ( 1 kpc projected distance). The jet shows a wide section between 20 and 70 mas (140 -- 500 pc) indicating an expanding jet propagating in a dense ambient medium. Here we identify the compact core (component A) and five emission regions (components B -- F).

sources.summary: The paper reports Chandra and JVLA observations of quasar J1405+0415, finding an X-ray jet extension without corresponding downstream radio emission and estimating its jet and core X-ray luminosities.

quotes.quote: J1405+0415 Figure fig:J1405 shows the 0.5 to 7 keV X-ray data from our observation of J1405+0415. We have superposed contours from our JVLA observations centered at 6.2 GHz. The X-ray jet is seen as an extension along the line at position angle 231 \ from the quasar core through the radio knot 0 8 away. There is no radio emission detected along this line past 0 8.

The jet X-ray flux is 9 10 ^ 15 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , which would correspond to a luminosity of 8 10 ^ 44 erg s ^ 1 if it were isotropically emitted. The quasar core has 269 photons, giving an X-ray flux of 3.3 10 ^ 13 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 and a luminosity of 3.2 10 ^ 46 erg s ^ 1 . Thus the jet X-ray flux is 2.6 that of the quasar core, with a factor of 2 uncertainty.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1402+044 as a z=3.208 flat-spectrum radio quasar with complex but frequency-matched structure, a 24-degree inner jet, multiple polarized components, and a possible 8.4 GHz core feature.

quotes.quote: 1402+044 This flat-spectrum radio quasar has a redshift of 3.208 barthel1990 . Although the source structure is fairly complex, the intensity structures match well at the two frequencies. The components in the innermost jet lie along a position angle of 24^ , consistent with the higher resolution image of , who find an inner jet direction of 26^ from 5-GHz VSOP data. Polarized components A, B, C, and D agree well between the two frequencies, although it is not clear how these correspond in detail to the intensity components in the same regions. There is an additional polarized component A1 in the 8.4-GHz core, which does not have a counterpart in intensity or at 5 GHz; it is difficult to be sure whether this is a real feature or an artefact.

sources.summary: The note describes a core-dominated double whose bright core-adjacent elongation is likely a jet, supported by VSOP and VLBA observations.

quotes.quote: J1405+0415: This is a core dominated double source. The bright emission close to the core is seen to be elongated in the 8 and 15 GHz images, and is most likely a jet. Observations with VSOP and the VLBA show a strongly curving jet with an initial position angle of 45^ . See also O'Sullivan et al. (2011) for global VLBI polarization sensitive observations.

gmw_00007233.name: J1429+2607
gmw_00007233.overview: J1429+2607 is an asymmetric triple radio source [arXiv:1406.4797]. At 8 GHz, its extended structure is completely resolved, except for a small core extension pointing southeast toward a 50 milliarcsecond jet [arXiv:1406.4797].

sources.summary: The note describes an asymmetric triple where the core has a southeast extension identified with a VLBI-confirmed 50 mas jet.

quotes.quote: J1429+2607: This is an asymmetric triple source. There are no 5 GHz data; at 8 GHz the extended structure is completely resolved, except for a small extension to the core pointing towards the SE component. That this is a jet is confirmed by VLBI observations that show a fine 50 mas long jet pointing in the same direction (position angle 120^ ).

gmw_00007234.name: J1457+3439
gmw_00007234.overview: J1457+3439 is a triple radio source that features a fine, two-arcsecond-long jet [arXiv:1406.4797]. The brighter inner portion of this jet is visible at 8 GHz, while extended emission to the north is detected only at 1.4 GHz [arXiv:1406.4797].

sources.summary: The note describes a triple source with a fine 2 arcsec jet, visible inner 8 GHz emission, and northern 1.4 GHz extended emission.

quotes.quote: J1457+3439: The 5 GHz image shows a fine, 2 arcsec long jet, the brighter inner part of which is visible at 8 GHz. This source is actually a triple source, but the extended emission to the north is only visible in 1.4 GHz image. The VIPS image shows a 10 mas jet in position angle 270^ .

gmw_00007235.name: J1528+5310
gmw_00007235.overview: J1528+5310 is a compact double radio-loud quasar [arXiv:1406.4797]. Its extended radio structure fades at higher frequencies, and the surrounding field contains two other nearby radio sources that are likely galaxies [arXiv:1406.4797].

sources.summary: The note describes a compact double whose extended structure fades at high frequency, plus nearby unrelated-looking radio galaxies in the field.

quotes.quote: J1528+5310: The extended structure for this compact double vanishes at higher frequencies. It is interesting that there are two other radio sources nearby: a large FR II radio source at a distance of 40 arcsec in PA 55^ (also visible on the FIRST cutout), and a fainter double source at a distance of 80 arcsec in PA 240^ . SDSS shows faint optical objects at the location of both radio sources, which are probably galaxies. We show both the radio image of the quasar (figure 7 (f)) and a zoomed out view of the whole field (figure 7 (g))

gmw_00007236.name: J1559+0304
gmw_00007236.overview: J1559+0304 is a high-redshift quasar located at a redshift of 3.90 [arXiv:1202.2193]. It is an extremely asymmetric triple radio source with a ninety-degree bend, featuring a core region with two distinct polarized components [arXiv:1202.2193, arXiv:1406.4797].

sources.summary: The paper presents 1557+032 as the sample's second most distant quasar and uses polarization to reveal two real core-region components unresolved in total intensity.

quotes.quote: 1557+032 This quasar is the second most distant object in our sample with a redshift of 3.90 mcmahon1994 . There are two distinct polarized components in the core region where only one total intensity component is distinguished; the fact that these two components are visible and model fit at both frequencies with similar positions and polarization angles suggests that they are real. This appears to be a case when the polarized emission provides information on scales smaller than those evident in the total intensity image.

sources.summary: The note describes an extremely asymmetric triple with a 90-degree bend and a discrete northern core extension.

quotes.quote: J1559+0304: This is an extremely asymmetric triple source with a ninety degree bend. The northern extension of the core in the displayed 1.4 GHz image is a discrete component at a distance of 2.6 arcsec. Global VLBI at 5 and 8.4 GHz shows a short extension of the core to the south-east .

gmw_00007237.name: J1625+4134
gmw_00007237.overview: J1625+4134 is a compact, core-dominated radio source with a nearly collinear triple structure [arXiv:1406.4797]. It features a milliarcsecond-scale jet extending northward from the core, which has been traced out to 80 milliarcseconds [arXiv:1406.4797].

sources.summary: The note identifies 4C +41.32 as a compact core-dominated nearly collinear triple with a northward milliarcsecond-scale jet.

quotes.quote: J1625+4134: 4C +41.32 is a compact, core dominated, nearly collinear triple. The emission south of the core is not visible in the 8 GHz and 15 GHz images. The VIPS image shows a 35 mas jet in position angle 5^ . Pushkarev Kovalev (2012) follow the jet out to 80 mas with a global VLBI array at 2.3 GHz.

gmw_00007238.name: J1655+3242
gmw_00007238.overview: J1655+3242 is a lobe-dominated double radio source [arXiv:1406.4797]. The galaxy features a 3-arcsecond jet with a strong lobe and hotspot, but it lacks detectable emission on the opposite side of its core [arXiv:1406.4797].

sources.summary: The note describes a lobe-dominated double with a prominent jet and strong lobe/hotspot, but no detected counter-side emission.

quotes.quote: J1655+3242: This lobe dominated double source features a prominent 3 arcsecond jet with a strong lobe/hotspot. It is striking that there is no detectable emission on the other side of the core above a level of 130 Jy/beam. Unfortunately no data at other frequencies are available.

gmw_00007239.name: J1704+0134
gmw_00007239.overview: J1704+0134 is a high-redshift radio-loud quasar [arXiv:1406.4797]. The source is tentatively classified as an unresolved radio core, as a distant northwest component is likely an unrelated background source [arXiv:1406.4797].

sources.summary: The note tentatively treats the source as an unresolved core and argues a distant northwest component is probably unrelated background emission.

quotes.quote: J1704+0134: We have tentatively called this an unresolved core in Table 1. If the component 39 arcsec to the NW is related, then this source would be more than twice as big as any other source in the sample. There is not a hint of any extended structure around it or the quasar at the center of the field of view. Its spectral index is = 0.5 , and we believe it is most likely an unrelated background source. Since it is just outside the SDSS footprint, it is difficult to search for an optical counterpart, but NED lists no optical object at its position.

gmw_00007240.name: SDSS J121855.80+020002.1
gmw_00007240.overview: SDSS J121855.80+020002.1 is a broad-line type 1 active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z=0.327 [arXiv:1109.3659]. The system hosts a central supermassive black hole with an estimated mass of approximately 1.13 billion solar masses and exhibits photocenter variability [arXiv:1109.3659].

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS J121855.80+020002.1 as a broad-line quasar with photocenter variability, estimating a billion-solar-mass black hole and ruling out BLR perturbations as the cause.

quotes.quote: To explore whether the observed variations in SDSS J121855.80+020002.1 ( z=0.327 , 18^m.1 ) and SDSS J162011.28+172427.5 (Mrk 877, z=0.112438 , 16^m.2 ) are caused by perturbations in the accretion disk Both observed objects have broad lines (type 1 AGN); in our simulations, we found that the photocenter offset is significant only when the central source is partly obscured by the dust. Therefore, there is a small chance that the observed variations are caused by changes in the torus structure. , we first estimate the masses of the black holes ( M_ bh ) for these two objects. There are several estimators for M_ bh in AGN see e.g and reference therein m08 , and to measure them for these two objects we used spectra observed with HST (for SDSS J121855.80+020002.1) and from SDSS database (for SDSS J162011.28+172427.5).

The estimated masses for SDSS J121855.80+020002.1 are: M_ S =9.18 10^8 \ M_ , M_ V =1.37 10^9 \ M_ , and M_ N =1.10 10^9 \ M_ , or on average M_ bh =(1.13 0.23) 10^9 \ M_ . In the same way, we estimated the black hole masses of SDSS J162011.28+172427.5 to be M_ S =3.51 10^8 \ M_ , M_ V =5.25 10^8 \ M_ , M_ N =3.72 10^8 \ M_ , or on average M_ bh =(4.16 0.95) 10^8 \ M_ . To estimate the possibility that the photocenter variability is caused by some perturbation in the disk (or in the BLR), we calculated dimensions of the BLR of these two objects, using the relation between the BLR radius and luminosity at 5100 \ see e.g. v06 . We estimated the BLR sizes for SDSS J121855.80+020002.1 to be around 113 light days (that is 0.02 mas) and for Mrk 877 10 light days ( 0.004 mas). Therefore, the observed photocenter variability cannot be explained by the perturbation in the BLR.

gmw_00007241.name: SDSS J162011.28+172427.5
gmw_00007241.overview: SDSS J162011.28+172427.5, also known as Mrk 877, is a type 1 active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of approximately 0.112 [arXiv:1109.3659]. The galaxy hosts a central supermassive black hole with an average estimated mass of 4.16 × 10^8 solar masses and exhibits photocenter variability [arXiv:1109.3659].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J162011.28+172427.5/Mrk 877 as a broad-line AGN with photocenter variability, estimates its black-hole mass, and finds its BLR too compact to explain the jitter.

quotes.quote: To explore whether the observed variations in SDSS J121855.80+020002.1 ( z=0.327 , 18^m.1 ) and SDSS J162011.28+172427.5 (Mrk 877, z=0.112438 , 16^m.2 ) are caused by perturbations in the accretion disk Both observed objects have broad lines (type 1 AGN); in our simulations, we found that the photocenter offset is significant only when the central source is partly obscured by the dust. Therefore, there is a small chance that the observed variations are caused by changes in the torus structure. , we first estimate the masses of the black holes ( M_ bh ) for these two objects. There are several estimators for M_ bh in AGN see e.g and reference therein m08 , and to measure them for these two objects we used spectra observed with HST (for SDSS J121855.80+020002.1) and from SDSS database (for SDSS J162011.28+172427.5).

The estimated masses for SDSS J121855.80+020002.1 are: M_ S =9.18 10^8 \ M_ , M_ V =1.37 10^9 \ M_ , and M_ N =1.10 10^9 \ M_ , or on average M_ bh =(1.13 0.23) 10^9 \ M_ . In the same way, we estimated the black hole masses of SDSS J162011.28+172427.5 to be M_ S =3.51 10^8 \ M_ , M_ V =5.25 10^8 \ M_ , M_ N =3.72 10^8 \ M_ , or on average M_ bh =(4.16 0.95) 10^8 \ M_ . To estimate the possibility that the photocenter variability is caused by some perturbation in the disk (or in the BLR), we calculated dimensions of the BLR of these two objects, using the relation between the BLR radius and luminosity at 5100 \ see e.g. v06 . We estimated the BLR sizes for SDSS J121855.80+020002.1 to be around 113 light days (that is 0.02 mas) and for Mrk 877 10 light days ( 0.004 mas). Therefore, the observed photocenter variability cannot be explained by the perturbation in the BLR.

gmw_00007242.name: SDSS J150243.09+111557.3
